topics.title: Estimation of Power Density Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Power Density Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Power Density Spectrum (PDS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Density Spectrum (PDS)**, also known as **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**, is defined as the **Fourier Transform of the Autocorrelation Function** of a stationary random process. It quantifies how the **average power** of a signal is distributed across its constituent frequency components.

Mathematically, for a wide-sense stationary (WSS) discrete-time random process $x(n)$:

$$
S_{xx}(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{r_{xx}(k)\} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j\omega k}
$$

The fundamental relationship is known as the **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem (WKT)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Estimation of the PDS is crucial in biomedical signal processing because most physiological signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG) are **random / stochastic** in nature. Their frequency-domain energy distribution carries diagnostic information that is not visible in the raw time-domain waveform.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Prism" of Signals

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you stand outside on a sunny day and feel the sun's heat — a single, undifferentiated white beam. Now place a **glass prism** in its path. The prism splits the beam into a **rainbow spectrum** showing exactly how much energy exists at each colour (frequency).
> 
> - The **white sunbeam** = your raw time-domain biomedical signal $x(n)$.
> - The **glass prism** = the **Fourier Transform** (or autocorrelation operation).
> - The **rainbow spectrum** = the **Power Density Spectrum** $S_{xx}(\omega)$.
> 
> A doctor looking at an ECG simply sees a wavy line — but its **PSD** reveals hidden power concentrations in clinically meaningful bands (e.g., 0.5–40 Hz for EEG rhythms), enabling diagnosis of disorders that the time-domain trace conceals.

---

### 1.3 Physical Significance and Biomedical Relevance

| Signal | Dominant Frequency Band | Diagnostic Use of PDS |
|---|---|---|
| ECG | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | QRS detection, HRV analysis |
| EEG | **0.5 – 60 Hz** | Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, Gamma rhythm identification |
| EMG | **20 – 500 Hz** | Muscle fatigue, neuromuscular disorders |
| PCG (Heart Sound) | **20 – 2000 Hz** | Murmur detection, valve disorders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric — Parseval's Theorem for PSD:**
> The total **average power** of the signal equals the area under the PSD curve:
> $$\boxed{P_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{+\pi} S_{xx}(\omega)\, d\omega = r_{xx}(0)}$$
> This is the foundational link between **time-domain power** and **frequency-domain power distribution**.

---

### 1.4 Classification of PDS Estimation Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme emphasises two broad classes:
> 
> 1. **Non-Parametric (Direct) Methods** — Make no assumption about the underlying data-generating model. Operate directly on signal samples.
>    - Periodogram
>    - Bartlett's Method
>    - Welch's Method
>    - Blackman–Tukey Method
> 
> 2. **Parametric (Model-Based) Methods** — Assume the signal is the output of a parametric linear system driven by white noise.
>    - Autoregressive (AR) Model
>    - Moving Average (MA) Model
>    - Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA) Model

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Autocorrelation Foundation

For a stationary discrete signal $x(n)$ of length $N$, the **biased autocorrelation estimate** is:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert k \vert} x(n)\, x(n+k), \qquad -N < k < N
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why "biased"?** Dividing by $N$ (not $N-\vert k \vert$) makes $\hat{r}_{xx}$ a **positive semi-definite** sequence — ensuring its Fourier transform (the PSD) is guaranteed to be non-negative for all frequencies. This is critical because true PSD can never be negative.

---

### 2.2 Non-Parametric Estimation Methods

#### 2.2.1 The Periodogram (Schuster, 1898)

The **raw periodogram** is the squared magnitude of the normalized Discrete Fourier Transform:

$$
\hat{S}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(\omega) \vert^{2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The raw periodogram is an **inconsistent** and **biased** estimator of the true PSD. As $N \to \infty$, its variance does **NOT** vanish — it remains constant. This is a board-favourite exam trap.

---

#### 2.2.2 Bartlett's Method (Averaging Periodograms)

To reduce variance, Bartlett proposed dividing $x(n)$ into $K$ **non-overlapping** segments of length $L = N/K$ each, then averaging their periodograms.

$$
\hat{S}_{\text{BART}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \hat{S}_{\text{PER},i}(\omega)
$$

- Variance reduced by factor $K$.
- Trade-off: frequency resolution drops because $L < N$.

---

#### 2.2.3 Welch's Method (Modified Periodogram)

Welch improved Bartlett by allowing **overlapping** segments and **windowing**:

1. Segment $x(n)$ into $K$ overlapping blocks of length $L$ (overlap typically $50\%$).
2. Apply a data window $w(n)$ (e.g., Hamming, Hann) to each block.
3. Compute the modified periodogram of each windowed block:

$$
\hat{S}_{\text{WELCH},i}(\omega) = \frac{1}{L\, U} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n)\, w(n)\, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^{2}
$$

where $U$ is the window energy normalisation factor:

$$
U = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^{2}(n)
$$

4. Average across all blocks:

$$
\hat{S}_{\text{WELCH}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \hat{S}_{\text{WELCH},i}(\omega)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Welch's method is the most widely used non-parametric PSD estimator in biomedical applications** (e.g., EEG spectral analysis, HRV) because it offers the best trade-off between variance reduction and resolution.

---

#### 2.2.4 Blackman–Tukey Method

Compute the autocorrelation estimate, multiply by a lag window $w(k)$ (Bartlett, Hamming, etc.), then Fourier-transform:

$$
\hat{S}_{\text{BT}}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-M}^{+M} \hat{r}_{xx}(k)\, w(k)\, e^{-j\omega k}
$$

where $M < N$ is the truncation lag. This **smooths** the periodogram and reduces variance at the cost of resolution.

---

### 2.3 Parametric Estimation — AR Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Autoregressive (AR) model of order $p$** assumes the signal $x(n)$ can be generated by linearly filtering white noise $\epsilon(n)$ of variance $\sigma^{2}$ through an all-pole filter:
> $$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x(n-k) + \epsilon(n)$$

The PSD of an AR($p$) process is:

$$
\boxed{S_{xx}^{\text{AR}}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^{2}}}
$$

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master this table — it contains every equation expected in a KTU 2024 ESE question on PDS estimation.

| # | Method | Core Formula | Key Property | Unit of $\hat{S}_{xx}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wiener–Khinchin | $S_{xx}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} r_{xx}(k) e^{-j\omega k}$ | Defines PSD from autocorrelation | Watts / rad/sample |
| 2 | Biased Autocorrelation | $\hat{r}_{xx}(k) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n} x(n) x(n+k)$ | Positive semi-definite | Signal power units |
| 3 | Periodogram | $\hat{S}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(\omega) \vert^{2}$ | Inconsistent, biased | Watts / rad/sample |
| 4 | Bartlett | $\hat{S}_{\text{BART}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \hat{S}_{\text{PER},i}(\omega)$ | Variance $\propto 1/K$ | Watts / rad/sample |
| 5 | Welch (one block) | $\hat{S}_{i}(\omega) = \frac{1}{L U} \vert X_{i,w}(\omega) \vert^{2}$ | Uses window $w(n)$, overlap allowed | Watts / rad/sample |
| 6 | Welch (full) | $\hat{S}_{\text{WELCH}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \hat{S}_{i}(\omega)$ | Best non-parametric trade-off | Watts / rad/sample |
| 7 | Blackman–Tukey | $\hat{S}_{\text{BT}}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-M}^{+M} \hat{r}_{xx}(k) w(k) e^{-j\omega k}$ | Smooths periodogram | Watts / rad/sample |
| 8 | AR($p$) PSD | $S_{xx}^{\text{AR}}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \vert^{2}}$ | High resolution, parametric | Watts / rad/sample |
| 9 | Total Power | $P_{\text{total}} = r_{xx}(0) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} S_{xx}(\omega) d\omega$ | Parseval identity | Watts |
| 10 | Yule–Walker | $r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(m-k),\ \ m \geq 1$ | Linear equations for $a_k$ | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **EEG Machine Designers** use Welch's method to display real-time alpha/beta power bars.
> - **Pacemaker Algorithms** compute PDS of HRV signals to detect autonomic dysfunction.
> - **Sleep Lab Systems** use AR models because EEG has sharp spectral peaks that non-parametric methods smear.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: PSD from Autocorrelation (Wiener–Khinchin Theorem)

> **Goal:** Prove that $S_{xx}(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{r_{xx}(k)\}$.

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of the autocorrelation function for a WSS process:

$$
r_{xx}(k) = E\{x(n)\, x(n+k)\}
$$

**Step 2:** Since $x(n)$ is WSS, take the expectation over $n$ of the outer product $x(n+k) x^{*}(n)$ and recognise it is independent of $n$.

**Step 3:** Define the **periodogram estimator** of the random process via the Fourier Transform of a finite-length sample. For an $N$-length realisation $x(n)$, the truncated DTFT is:

$$
X_{N}(\omega) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j\omega n}
$$

**Step 4:** Form the squared magnitude (which by definition is non-negative):

$$
\frac{1}{N} \vert X_{N}(\omega) \vert^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, x(m)\, e^{-j\omega (n-m)}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $k = n - m$ (lag variable). Re-index the double summation as a sum over $k$:

$$
\frac{1}{N} \vert X_{N}(\omega) \vert^{2} = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{+(N-1)} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert k \vert} x(n)\, x(n+k) \right] e^{-j\omega k}
$$

**Step 6:** Identify the bracketed term as the **biased autocorrelation estimator** $\hat{r}_{xx}(k)$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{N} \vert X_{N}(\omega) \vert^{2} = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{+(N-1)} \hat{r}_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j\omega k} = \mathcal{F}\{\hat{r}_{xx}(k)\}}
$$

**Step 7:** As $N \to \infty$, $\hat{r}_{xx}(k) \to r_{xx}(k)$ in the mean-square sense (ergodic theorem), proving:

$$
S_{xx}(\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{r_{xx}(k)\} \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Yule–Walker Equations for AR Model

> **Goal:** Express the AR($p$) coefficients $a_k$ in terms of autocorrelation lags.

**Step 1:** The AR($p$) difference equation is:

$$
x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x(n-k) = \epsilon(n)
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $x(n-m)$ for $m \geq 1$ and take expectation:

$$
E\{x(n)\, x(n-m)\} + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, E\{x(n-k)\, x(n-m)\} = E\{\epsilon(n)\, x(n-m)\}
$$

**Step 3:** Since $\epsilon(n)$ is white noise with zero mean and uncorrelated with past values of $x(n)$, the term $E\{\epsilon(n)\, x(n-m)\} = 0$ for $m \geq 1$.

**Step 4:** Recognise the resulting expectations as autocorrelation values. We obtain:

$$
\boxed{r_{xx}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(m-k) = 0, \qquad m = 1, 2, \dots, p}
$$

**Step 5:** In **matrix form**, the Yule–Walker system is:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
r_{xx}(0) & r_{xx}(-1) & \cdots & r_{xx}(-(p-1)) \\
r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(0) & \cdots & r_{xx}(-(p-2)) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
r_{xx}(p-1) & r_{xx}(p-2) & \cdots & r_{xx}(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix}
=
-\begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}(1) \\ r_{xx}(2) \\ \vdots \\ r_{xx}(p) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 6:** Note that $r_{xx}(-k) = r_{xx}^{*}(k)$. For real signals, $r_{xx}(-k) = r_{xx}(k)$, so the matrix is a **real, symmetric, Toeplitz** structure — solvable efficiently by the **Levinson–Durbin algorithm** in $O(p^{2})$ time.

**Step 7:** The white noise variance is obtained by setting $m=0$:

$$
\sigma^{2} = r_{xx}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(-k) = r_{xx}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, r_{xx}(k)
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Variance Reduction in Bartlett's Method

**Step 1:** Assume the true PSD is $S(\omega)$. Each segment periodogram has variance $\text{Var}\{\hat{S}_{\text{PER},i}\} \approx S^{2}(\omega)$ (for Gaussian processes, the periodogram is exponentially distributed).

**Step 2:** Assuming independence between non-overlapping segments:

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{S}_{\text{BART}}\} = \frac{1}{K^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \text{Var}\{\hat{S}_{\text{PER},i}\} = \frac{S^{2}(\omega)}{K}
$$

**Step 3:** Hence variance drops by factor $K = N/L$ at the cost of reducing frequency resolution from $2\pi/N$ to $2\pi/L$ — a **$K$-fold** degradation in resolution.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use?

> [!TIP]
> Use this matrix during exam time when the question asks "compare and justify" a method.

| Criterion | Periodogram | Bartlett | Welch | Blackman–Tukey | AR Model |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Variance | High | Reduced ($\propto 1/K$) | Lowest (non-param) | Low | Very Low |
| Bias | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Window-dependent | Very Low |
| Resolution | Highest | Reduced ($L < N$) | Tunable ($L$, overlap) | Tunable ($M$) | Highest |
| Computational Cost | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(K L \log L)$ | $O(K L \log L)$ | $O(N \log N)$ | $O(p^{2})$ via Levinson–Durbin |
| Spectral Leakage | High | High | Reduced (windowing) | Reduced | Inherently smooth |
| Data Length Requirement | Long | Very Long | Moderate | Moderate | **Short data works well** |
| Best for Biomedical | Spectral display | Initial study | **Routine clinical PSD** | Offline analysis | EEG, HRV, short epochs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of PDS Estimation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PDS Estimation Techniques]
    A --> B[Non-Parametric Methods]
    A --> C[Parametric Methods]
    A --> D[Subspace Methods]

    B --> B1[Periodogram]
    B --> B2[Bartlett Method]
    B --> B3[Welch Method]
    B --> B4[Blackman Tukey Method]

    B1 --> B1a["Direct FFT squared magnitude"]
    B2 --> B2a["Average non-overlapping segments"]
    B3 --> B3a["Overlap + windowing + averaging"]
    B4 --> B4a["Windowed autocorrelation + FFT"]

    C --> C1[AR Model Autoregressive]
    C --> C2[MA Model Moving Average]
    C --> C3[ARMA Model]
    C --> C4[ARMAvia Yule Walker]

    C1 --> C1a["Yule Walker equations"]
    C1 --> C1b["Burg algorithm"]
    C1 --> C1c["Levinson Durbin recursion"]

    D --> D1[MUSIC]
    D --> D2[Pisarenko]

    B3a --> APP1[EEG Spectral Analysis]
    B3a --> APP2[HRV Analysis]
    C1a --> APP3[Short EEG Epochs]
    C1a --> APP4[Speech Formant Tracking]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Welch's Method Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Signal x of n]
    IN --> SEG[Segment into K overlapping blocks of length L]
    SEG --> WIN[Apply Window w of n to each block]
    WIN --> FFT[Compute FFT of each windowed block]
    FFT --> MAG[Compute squared magnitude spectrum]
    MAG --> NORM[Normalize by L times U window energy]
    NORM --> AVG[Average across all K blocks]
    AVG --> OUT[Welch PSD Estimate S of omega]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — AR($p$) Model for Parametric PSD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NOISE[White Noise epsilon with variance sigma squared]
    NOISE --> H[All Pole Filter H of z]
    H --> OUT[Output x of n AR p process]

    H_DESC["H of z equals 1 divided by 1 plus sum of a_k z to power minus k for k from 1 to p"]
    H -.-> H
```

### 4.4 Process Flow — Complete PDS Estimation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Acquire biomedical signal]
    S2[Step 2 Preprocess remove DC detrend filter]
    S3[Step 3 Choose method based on data length]
    S4{Decision Branch}
    S4 -->|Long data| S5[Use Welch non parametric]
    S4 -->|Short data| S6[Use AR parametric]

    S5 --> S7[Compute Welch PSD]
    S6 --> S8[Compute AR coefficients via Yule Walker]
    S8 --> S9[Plug into AR PSD formula]

    S7 --> S10[Step 4 Plot log magnitude spectrum]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[Step 5 Identify diagnostic frequency peaks]
```

### 4.5 Schematic — Trade-off Triangle in PDS Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TR[Trade off Triangle in PDS Estimation]
    TR --> T1[Variance]
    TR --> T2[Bias]
    TR --> T3[Resolution]

    T1 ---|Periodogram High| T2
    T2 ---|AR Model Low| T3
    T3 ---|Welch Balanced| T1

    NOTE["Optimal choice balances all three for biomedical application"]
    TR -.-> NOTE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

---

**Q1. Define Power Density Spectrum. State the Wiener–Khinchin theorem.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The Power Density Spectrum (PDS) of a wide-sense stationary random signal $x(n)$ is defined as the distribution of signal power as a function of frequency. It is mathematically defined as the Fourier Transform of the autocorrelation function $r_{xx}(k)$ of the signal.
> 
> **Theorem Statement (1.5 Marks):** The **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem** states that the Power Density Spectrum $S_{xx}(\omega)$ and the autocorrelation function $r_{xx}(k)$ form a Fourier Transform pair:
> $$S_{xx}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j\omega k} \quad \text{and} \quad r_{xx}(k) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{+\pi} S_{xx}(\omega)\, e^{j\omega k}\, d\omega$$
> 
> This theorem establishes that the autocorrelation (time-domain) and the power spectrum (frequency-domain) carry identical information about a stationary process.

---

**Q2. Distinguish between parametric and non-parametric methods of PSD estimation. Give one example of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> | Aspect | Non-Parametric Methods | Parametric Methods |
> |---|---|---|
> | Assumption on model | None — operates directly on data | Assume signal is output of linear model with white-noise input |
> | Equations used | FFT-based computations | Yule–Walker, Levinson–Durbin recursion |
> | Data length | Require long data records | Work well with short data |
> | Spectral resolution | Limited by FFT length | Can achieve high resolution |
> | Example | Periodogram, Welch's Method | AR model estimation |

> **Example (1 Mark):** Non-parametric — **Welch's Method**; Parametric — **AR($p$) model using Yule–Walker equations**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 ESE Style]**
> *Note: Module-level internal choice — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the autocorrelation function and the Power Density Spectrum using the Wiener–Khinchin theorem. Explain why the biased autocorrelation estimator is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A stationary process $x(n)$ has an autocorrelation function given by $r_{xx}(k) = (0.8)^{\vert k \vert }$. Compute and plot its Power Density Spectrum. Identify the dominant frequency. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

1. **(1 Mark)** Start with the periodogram estimator for a finite-length sample $x(n)$ of length $N$:
$$
\hat{S}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^{2}
$$

2. **(2 Marks)** Expand the squared magnitude and substitute $k = n - m$:
$$
\hat{S}_{\text{PER}}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{+(N-1)} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert k \vert} x(n)\, x(n+k) \right] e^{-j\omega k}
$$

3. **(1 Mark)** Recognise the inner summation as the biased autocorrelation estimate $\hat{r}_{xx}(k)$.

4. **(1 Mark)** Apply the ergodic theorem: as $N \to \infty$, $\hat{r}_{xx}(k) \to r_{xx}(k)$ in the mean-square sense. Hence:
$$
S_{xx}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j\omega k}
$$

5. **(1 Mark)** Justify biased estimator: Dividing by $N$ (not $N-\vert k \vert$) guarantees $\hat{r}_{xx}$ is **positive semi-definite**, ensuring $\hat{S}_{xx}(\omega) \geq 0$ for all $\omega$, which is the physically meaningful requirement for true power.

6. **(1 Mark)** Conclude with the Wiener–Khinchin theorem statement.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

1. **(1 Mark)** Given $r_{xx}(k) = (0.8)^{\vert k \vert }$. This is a geometric sequence in lag $k$.

2. **(2 Marks)** Apply the Wiener–Khinchin theorem:
$$
S_{xx}(\omega) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} (0.8)^{\vert k \vert} e^{-j\omega k}
$$

3. **(2 Marks)** Split into $k=0$, positive $k$, and negative $k$ terms, use $(0.8)^{\vert k \vert} = (0.8)^{k}$ for $k \geq 0$:
$$
S_{xx}(\omega) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} (0.8)^{k} e^{-j\omega k} + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} (0.8)^{k} e^{+j\omega k}
$$

4. **(1 Mark)** Each sum is a geometric series with ratio $0.8 e^{\pm j\omega}$:
$$
S_{xx}(\omega) = 1 + \frac{0.8\, e^{-j\omega}}{1 - 0.8\, e^{-j\omega}} + \frac{0.8\, e^{j\omega}}{1 - 0.8\, e^{j\omega}}
$$

5. **(1 Mark)** Combine and simplify to obtain the closed form:
$$
\boxed{S_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1 - 0.64}{1 - 1.6 \cos\omega + 0.64} = \frac{0.36}{1.64 - 1.6\cos\omega}}
$$

6. **(1 Mark — Discussion):** The dominant frequency occurs where $S_{xx}(\omega)$ is maximum. Maximum of denominator occurs at $\cos\omega = 1$, i.e., $\omega = 0$. Hence the **dominant frequency is $\omega = 0$** (DC region), and the spectrum is monotonically decreasing as $\vert \omega \vert$ increases. Peak value: $S_{xx}(0) = \frac{0.36}{0.04} = 9$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting the factor of $1/N$ in the periodogram — students often omit the energy normalisation.
> 2. Confusing $\cos\omega$ with $\cos(2\pi f)$ — KTU consistently uses normalised digital frequency $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.
> 3. Claiming the AR PSD formula applies to **non-AR signals** — model mismatch produces spurious peaks.
> 4. Writing $r_{xx}(0)$ instead of $r_{xx}(k)$ in the Yule–Walker equation at $m=0$ — the noise variance equation is **separate**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the periodogram method of PSD estimation. Why is it called an inconsistent estimator? How does Bartlett's method overcome this limitation? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the AR(2) model PSD for a signal whose Yule–Walker equations yield coefficients $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.7$ and white-noise variance $\sigma^{2} = 1$. Identify the spectral peaks. **(7 Marks)**

---

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation (7 Marks):**

1. **(2 Marks)** **Periodogram Definition:** The periodogram is computed as the squared magnitude of the normalised FFT:
$$
\hat{S}_{\text{PER}}(\omega_k) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(\omega_k) \vert^{2}, \qquad \omega_k = \frac{2\pi k}{N}
$$

2. **(2 Marks)** **Inconsistency Proof:** The variance of the periodogram for a Gaussian process at frequency $\omega$ (excluding $\omega = 0, \pi$) is:
$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{S}_{\text{PER}}(\omega)\} = S_{xx}^{2}(\omega)
$$
This variance is **independent of $N$** — increasing $N$ does NOT reduce it. Hence the estimator does not converge to the true PSD as $N \to \infty$, violating the definition of a **consistent estimator**.

3. **(2 Marks)** **Bartlett's Solution:** Partition $x(n)$ into $K$ non-overlapping segments of length $L = N/K$. Average the periodograms:
$$
\hat{S}_{\text{BART}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \hat{S}_{\text{PER},i}(\omega)
$$
If segments are independent, variance reduces by factor $K$:
$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{S}_{\text{BART}}\} = \frac{S_{xx}^{2}(\omega)}{K}
$$
As $N \to \infty$ with $L \to \infty$ (so $K \to \infty$), variance $\to 0$, achieving consistency.

4. **(1 Mark)** **Trade-off:** Frequency resolution drops from $2\pi/N$ to $2\pi/L$ — a $K$-fold loss in resolution.

**(b) AR(2) Computation (7 Marks):**

1. **(1 Mark)** AR PSD formula:
$$
S_{xx}^{\text{AR}}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^{2}}
$$

2. **(1 Mark)** Substitute $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.7$, $\sigma^{2} = 1$, $p=2$:
$$
S_{xx}^{\text{AR}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - 1.5\, e^{-j\omega} + 0.7\, e^{-j2\omega} \vert^{2}}
$$

3. **(2 Marks)** Use Euler's formula: $e^{-j\omega} = \cos\omega - j\sin\omega$. Expand the denominator magnitude squared:
$$
D(\omega) = \vert 1 - 1.5(\cos\omega - j\sin\omega) + 0.7(\cos 2\omega - j\sin 2\omega) \vert^{2}
$$
Separating real and imaginary parts:
$$
D(\omega) = \left( 1 - 1.5\cos\omega + 0.7\cos 2\omega \right)^{2} + \left( 1.5\sin\omega - 0.7\sin 2\omega \right)^{2}
$$

4. **(1 Mark)** Simplify using $\cos 2\omega = 2\cos^{2}\omega - 1$ and $\sin 2\omega = 2\sin\omega\cos\omega$:
$$
D(\omega) = \left( 1 - 1.5\cos\omega + 1.4\cos^{2}\omega - 0.7 \right)^{2} + \left( 1.5\sin\omega - 1.4\sin\omega\cos\omega \right)^{2}
$$
$$
D(\omega) = \left( 0.3 - 1.5\cos\omega + 1.4\cos^{2}\omega \right)^{2} + \sin^{2}\omega\,(1.5 - 1.4\cos\omega)^{2}
$$

5. **(1 Mark)** Factor the second term — both share the structure $(1.5 - 1.4\cos\omega)^{2}$. After factoring:
$$
D(\omega) = (1.5 - 1.4\cos\omega)^{2}\,[\text{simplified expression}]
$$

6. **(1 Mark — Peak Location):** $S_{xx}^{\text{AR}}(\omega)$ peaks where $D(\omega)$ is minimum. Set $\frac{d D(\omega)}{d\omega} = 0$ numerically. The minimum occurs near $\omega \approx \pi/2$ (since both coefficients are real and the pole lies on the upper half of the unit circle for this AR configuration). Hence a **dominant spectral peak near $\omega = \pi/2$** (i.e., quarter-Nyquist frequency) is observed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — AR Model Traps:**
> 1. Forgetting the **square** in the denominator magnitude — losing 2 marks easily.
> 2. Using the AR formula for MA or ARMA signals — must verify model order first.
> 3. Not justifying why AR models give **better resolution than FFT for short data** — always mention $O(p^{2})$ computation vs. $O(N \log N)$.
> 4. Confusing the white-noise variance $\sigma^{2}$ with the signal variance $r_{xx}(0)$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print or screenshot this checklist before every KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem** links autocorrelation $r_{xx}(k)$ and PSD $S_{xx}(\omega)$ via Fourier Transform.
- [ ] **Parseval's identity:** total power = $r_{xx}(0)$ = area under $S_{xx}(\omega)$ divided by $2\pi$.
- [ ] **Biased autocorrelation** divides by $N$ → positive semi-definite → guarantees $S_{xx} \geq 0$.
- [ ] **Periodogram** = $(1/N) \vert X(\omega) \vert^{2}$ → inconsistent estimator (variance independent of $N$).
- [ ] **Bartlett's method** = average of $K$ non-overlapping periodograms → variance reduced by factor $K$.
- [ ] **Welch's method** = Bartlett + overlapping segments + windowing → lowest variance non-parametric estimator; clinical standard for EEG.
- [ ] **Blackman–Tukey method** = windowed autocorrelation + FFT → smooths raw periodogram.
- [ ] **AR($p$) model:** $x(n) = -\sum a_k x(n-k) + \epsilon(n)$; produces all-pole spectrum.
- [ ] **AR PSD formula:** $S_{xx}^{\text{AR}}(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \vert^{2}}$.
- [ ] **Yule–Walker equations:** $r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k)$ for $m \geq 1$.
- [ ] **Levinson–Durbin recursion** solves Yule–Walker in $O(p^{2})$ for Toeplitz matrix.
- [ ] **Noise variance:** $\sigma^{2} = r_{xx}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(k)$ (set $m=0$ in AR equation).
- [ ] **AR models** work best for **short data** (EEG, HRV) with sharp spectral peaks; **Welch** for long routine analysis.
- [ ] **PSD units:** Watts per radian per sample (discrete) or Watts per Hz (continuous).
- [ ] **Biomedical applications:** EEG rhythm decomposition, HRV autonomic analysis, EMG fatigue detection, PCG murmur identification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Parametric Methods: Periodogram, Bartlett, Welch, Blackman and Tukey Methods - Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-Parametric Methods: Periodogram, Bartlett, Welch, Blackman and Tukey Methods - Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — NON-PARAMETRIC POWER SPECTRAL DENSITY (PSD) ESTIMATION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Parametric Spectral Estimation** refers to a class of Power Spectral Density (PSD) estimation techniques that do **not** assume any predefined mathematical model for the underlying signal-generating process. They operate directly on the finite observed data record and rely on transformations of the data (Fourier analysis) or its autocorrelation sequence.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (course code **PEEBT634**), the four classical non-parametric estimators under Module 1 are:

1. **Periodogram** (Schuster, 1899) — the foundational estimator.
2. **Bartlett Method** (1948) — averaged periodogram using non-overlapping segments.
3. **Welch Method** (1967) — averaged *modified* periodogram with windowing and overlap.
4. **Blackman–Tukey Method** (1958) — windowed autocorrelation (lag-window) approach.

### Formal Definition
Given a finite-length WSS random signal $x(n)$ of length $N$, the goal of non-parametric PSD estimation is to produce a function $\hat{P}_{xx}(f)$ (a function of continuous or discrete frequency $f$) that is a statistically meaningful estimate of the **true** PSD $P_{xx}(f)$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Musician's Ear" Intuition 🎧

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Doctor Listening to a Heartbeat**
>
> Imagine a cardiologist using a stethoscope on a patient's chest. The recorded heart sound is a time-domain waveform — it tells you *when* things happen. A PSD estimator acts like a **frequency prism**: it decomposes the heart sound into its constituent frequency components (lub-dub sounds, murmurs, valve clicks).
>
> - The **Periodogram** is like listening *once* and judging the music — fine detail (good resolution) but noisy and inconsistent.
> - **Bartlett's method** is like cutting the recording into multiple short clips, judging each clip, and averaging the verdicts — smoother but coarser.
> - **Welch's method** is like allowing the clips to *overlap* (you don't lose transitions) and applying a soft-focusing window (a perceptual filter) — smoother still, widely used in **ECG/EEG analysers** and **speech codecs**.
> - **Blackman–Tukey** is like first asking "how similar is the sound to itself after a small delay?" and then transforming that similarity into frequencies — best when the signal is short and clean.

---

## 1.3 Why Non-Parametric? The Bias–Variance Compass 🧭

Three performance metrics dominate KTU examination questions on this topic:

- **Resolution** — ability to distinguish two close sinusoids (Δf ≈ mainlobe width).
- **Variance** — stability of the estimate across different data realizations.
- **Bias** — systematic error introduced by windowing or truncation.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU university exams, every comparison question reduces to: *"What is the resolution–variance trade-off?"* Memorize this trade-off table; it earns **7 easy marks**.

| Metric | Periodogram | Bartlett | Welch | Blackman–Tukey |
|--------|-------------|----------|-------|----------------|
| Resolution | **Best** | Worst (M < N) | Tunable | Tunable via M |
| Variance | **Worst** (∝ $P_{xx}^2$) | Reduced by **1/K** | Reduced by **1/K** | Reduced via smoothing |
| Bias | Low (asymptotic) | Higher | Higher (windowing) | Tunable via lag window |

(Symbol definitions and derivations follow in Sections 2 and 3.)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Four Methods

### 🔹 Method 1: The Periodogram
- **Step 1:** Take a finite data record $x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)$.
- **Step 2:** Compute the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of the windowed data, treating the finite record as if it were the *entire* signal.
- **Step 3:** Compute the **squared magnitude** of the DTFT — this is the raw spectral energy distribution.
- **Step 4:** Normalize by $N$ to obtain the power density.

> **Why divide by N?** Because Parseval's theorem requires energy conservation: $\sum |x(n)|^2 = N \int |X(f)|^2 df$. Dividing by $N$ gives units of *power per Hz*.

### 🔹 Method 2: Bartlett Method
- **Step 1:** Segment the data $x(n)$ of length $N$ into $K$ non-overlapping segments, each of length $M = N/K$.
- **Step 2:** Compute the periodogram of each segment independently.
- **Step 3:** **Average** the $K$ periodograms segment-wise.
- **Key Idea:** Averaging *reduces variance* by a factor of $K$ because uncorrelated estimates average out.

### 🔹 Method 3: Welch Method
- **Step 1:** Segment $x(n)$ into $K$ overlapping segments of length $M$, with shift $D$ (typical: 50% overlap, $D = M/2$).
- **Step 2:** Apply a data window $w(n)$ (Hann, Hamming) to each segment.
- **Step 3:** Compute the periodogram of each windowed segment.
- **Step 4:** Normalize each windowed periodogram by the window-energy factor $U = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} |w(n)|^2$.
- **Step 5:** Average the normalized periodograms.
- **Why overlap?** It allows more segments $K$ from the same $N$, further reducing variance.

### 🔹 Method 4: Blackman–Tukey Method
- **Step 1:** Estimate the autocorrelation sequence $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ for $|m| \le M-1$ from the $N$-point data (where $M \ll N$).
- **Step 2:** Apply a lag window $w(m)$ (Bartlett, Hamming, Parzen) to taper the autocorrelation.
- **Step 3:** Compute the DTFT of the windowed autocorrelation — this is the PSD estimate.
- **Why a lag window?** It enforces the *positive-definiteness* of the PSD (ensures $\hat{P}_{xx}(f) \ge 0$ for all $f$).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — these are the exact equations KTU examiners expect in 14-mark derivations.**

| # | Method | PSD Formula $\hat{P}_{xx}(f)$ | Key Parameters |
|---|--------|-------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Periodogram** | $\hat{P}_{PER}(f) = \dfrac{1}{N}\left\vert \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\,e^{-j2\pi fn} \right\vert^{2}$ | $N$ = data length |
| 2 | **Bartlett** | $\hat{P}_{B}(f) = \dfrac{1}{K}\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \hat{P}_{PER}^{(i)}(f)$ | $K=N/M$, $M$ = segment length |
| 3 | **Welch** | $\hat{P}_{W}(f) = \dfrac{1}{K\,U}\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{K-1}\left\vert \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w(n)\,x(n+iD)\,e^{-j2\pi fn}\right\vert^{2}$ | $U = \frac{1}{M}\sum w^{2}(n)$, shift $D$ |
| 4 | **Blackman–Tukey** | $\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = \displaystyle\sum_{m=-(M-1)}^{M-1} w(m)\,\hat{r}_{xx}(m)\,e^{-j2\pi fm}$ | $M$ = max lag, $w(m)$ lag window |

### Variance & Resolution Reference Table (Exam Favourite ⭐)

| Method | $\text{Var}[\hat{P}(f)]$ | Resolution $\Delta f$ | Bias |
|--------|--------------------------|------------------------|------|
| Periodogram | $P_{xx}^{2}(f)$ | $\approx \dfrac{0.89}{N\,T}$ | Asymptotically unbiased |
| Bartlett | $\dfrac{1}{K}\,P_{xx}^{2}(f)$ | $\approx \dfrac{0.89}{M\,T}$ | Convolved with Dirichlet kernel of width $M$ |
| Welch | $\approx \dfrac{1}{K}\,P_{xx}^{2}(f)$ | $\approx \dfrac{0.89}{M\,T}$ (slightly wider due to $w(n)$) | Convolved with $|W(f)|^{2}$ |
| Blackman–Tukey | $\approx \dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{m=-(M-1)}^{M-1} w^{2}(m)\,P_{xx}^{2}(f)$ | Tunable via $M$ | Convolved with $W(f)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $T$ in the resolution column denotes the sampling period ($T = 1/f_s$). A common KTU error is writing $N$ instead of $M$ in Bartlett/Welch resolution — examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering 🏥

| Method | Typical Biomedical Use-Case |
|--------|-----------------------------|
| **Periodogram** | Quick-and-dirty ECG R-R interval power spectrum (for very short recordings) |
| **Bartlett** | Variance reduction in **heart-rate-variability (HRV)** studies |
| **Welch** | **EEG band-power extraction** (delta/theta/alpha/beta), polysomnography, evoked potentials |
| **Blackman–Tukey** | Spectral estimation of **short, clean** physiological signals (e.g., respiratory flow signals) |

> The **Welch method** is overwhelmingly the most common in modern biomedical DSP toolboxes (MATLAB `pwelch`, Python `scipy.signal.welch`) because the 50% overlap + Hann window combination gives an excellent bias–variance trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Periodogram from Parseval's Theorem

We start with the **DTFT** of a finite, rectangularly-windowed data record:

$$
X_N(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}
$$

This implicitly defines the data as $x_N(n) = x(n) \cdot \text{rect}_N(n)$, where $\text{rect}_N(n) = 1$ for $0 \le n < N$ and $0$ otherwise.

By **Parseval's theorem** for discrete-time signals:

$$
\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} |x(n)|^{2} = N \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} |X_N(f)|^{2}\, df
$$

The **average power** in the data record is therefore:

$$
\hat{P}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} |x(n)|^{2} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} \frac{1}{N} |X_N(f)|^{2}\, df
$$

By definition, the average power equals the integral of the PSD. Hence we **identify**:

$$
\hat{P}_{PER}(f) = \frac{1}{N} |X_N(f)|^{2} = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^{2}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The Periodogram is the *sample PSD* — the only "model" is that the data segment represents a stationary process.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean of the Periodogram (Bias Analysis)

The expected value of the periodogram of $x(n)$ (a zero-mean WSS process) is:

$$
E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = E\left\{ \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^{2} \right\}
$$

Expand the squared magnitude:

$$
E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} E\{x(n)x^{*}(m)\}\, e^{-j2\pi f(n-m)}
$$

Use the autocorrelation definition $E\{x(n)x^{*}(m)\} = r_{xx}(n-m)$:

$$
E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} r_{xx}(n-m)\, e^{-j2\pi f(n-m)}
$$

Apply the change of variables $k = n - m$ and let $N \to \infty$ in the asymptotic limit:

$$
E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left(1 - \frac{|k|}{N}\right) r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j2\pi f k}
$$

The factor $\left(1 - \frac{|k|}{N}\right)$ is the **triangular (Bartlett) kernel**. Recognizing its DTFT, we obtain the **fundamental bias equation**:

$$
E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_{xx}(\nu) \left( \frac{\sin \pi(f-\nu)N}{\pi(f-\nu)N} \right)^{2} d\nu
$$

> **Conclusion:** The periodogram is a *biased* estimator — its mean is the **true PSD convolved with the Fejér kernel** of width $\propto 1/N$. It is **asymptotically unbiased** as $N \to \infty$ at non-zero frequencies because the kernel collapses to a delta.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Variance of the Periodogram (The "Periodogram Paradox")

For a **real, zero-mean, Gaussian** process with true PSD $P_{xx}(f)$:

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} \approx P_{xx}^{2}(f)
$$

This result is **independent of $N$** — the *periodogram does not converge* in the mean-square sense! This is the famous **"inconsistency"** problem.

**Derivation sketch** (at frequency $f = 0$ for simplicity):

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}(0)\} = E\{\hat{P}^{2}(0)\} - [E\{\hat{P}(0)\}]^{2}
$$

Using Isserlis' theorem for Gaussian 4th moments:

$$
E\{x(n_1)x(n_2)x(n_3)x(n_4)\} = r(n_1-n_2)r(n_3-n_4) + r(n_1-n_3)r(n_2-n_4) + r(n_1-n_4)r(n_2-n_3)
$$

Substituting and simplifying (cancelling the squared-mean term):

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}(0)\} \approx P^{2}(0) \quad \text{(does not decay with } N\text{)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the central motivation for Bartlett, Welch, and Blackman–Tukey** — they all *average* or *smooth* to drive the variance down.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Bartlett Variance Reduction

Bartlett's estimator averages $K$ independent periodograms, each computed from a disjoint segment of length $M = N/K$. Assuming the segments are statistically independent:

$$
\hat{P}_{B}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \hat{P}_{PER}^{(i)}(f)
$$

Variance of a sum of independent, identically distributed random variables divided by $K$:

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{B}(f)\} = \frac{1}{K^{2}} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{PER}^{(i)}(f)\} = \frac{1}{K^{2}} \cdot K \cdot P_{xx}^{2}(f)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{B}(f)\} = \frac{1}{K}\,P_{xx}^{2}(f)\;}
$$

> **Trade-off:** Variance is reduced by factor $K$, but **resolution worsens** because the segment length drops from $N$ to $M = N/K$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Welch Normalization Constant $U$

Welch applies a window $w(n)$ to each segment before computing the periodogram. The windowed DTFT is:

$$
X_{i}(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w(n)\,x(n+iD)\,e^{-j2\pi f n}
$$

The expected value of $|X_{i}(f)|^{2}$ contains the **window-energy factor**:

$$
E\{|X_{i}(f)|^{2}\} \approx U \cdot M \cdot P_{xx}(f) \quad \text{(in mainlobe region)}
$$

where $U = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} |w(n)|^{2}$. Dividing by $MU$ ensures the estimator is **unbiased** in the mainlobe. Hence:

$$
\hat{P}_{W}(f) = \frac{1}{K\,U} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \frac{1}{M} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w(n)\,x(n+iD)\,e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^{2}
$$

With 50% overlap ($D = M/2$), the number of available segments is $K \approx 2N/M$, doubling the variance reduction compared to Bartlett.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Blackman–Tukey PSD via Wiener–Khinchin

The **Wiener–Khinchin theorem** states that the PSD and autocorrelation are a Fourier-transform pair:

$$
P_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m)\, e^{-j2\pi f m}
$$

Since only an *estimate* $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ is available (and only for $|m| \le M-1$), direct truncation causes spectral leakage. Blackman–Tukey apply a **lag window** $w(m)$:

$$
\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = \sum_{m=-(M-1)}^{M-1} w(m)\,\hat{r}_{xx}(m)\, e^{-j2\pi f m}
$$

Substituting the estimator $\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1-|m|} x(n+m)x^{*}(n)$ and taking the expectation, the bias equation mirrors that of the periodogram but with the **window's DTFT** $W(f)$ as the smoothing kernel.

**Variance (for large $N$, fixed $M$):**

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} \approx \frac{P_{xx}^{2}(f)}{N} \sum_{m=-(M-1)}^{M-1} w^{2}(m)
$$

The factor $\sum w^{2}(m)$ acts as an *effective smoothing bandwidth* — smaller $M$ reduces variance but worsens resolution.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Comparison of All Four Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["INPUT DATA x(n) of length N"] --> B["Choose Method"]
    B --> C1["PERIODOGRAM"]
    B --> C2["BARTLETT"]
    B --> C3["WELCH"]
    B --> C4["BLACKMAN-TUKEY"]
    C1 --> D1["1. Compute DTFT X_N f"]
    D1 --> E1["2. Squared magnitude divided by N"]
    E1 --> F1["PSD Estimate"]
    C2 --> D2["1. Split into K non-overlapping segments of length M"]
    D2 --> E2["2. Periodogram of each segment"]
    E2 --> F2["3. Average K periodograms"]
    F2 --> G2["PSD Estimate"]
    C3 --> D3["1. Split into K overlapping segments shift D"]
    D3 --> E3["2. Apply data window w n to each"]
    E3 --> F3["3. Windowed periodogram of each segment"]
    F3 --> G3["4. Normalize by window energy U"]
    G3 --> H3["5. Average K normalized estimates"]
    H3 --> I3["PSD Estimate"]
    C4 --> D4["1. Estimate autocorrelation r_xx m for m less than M"]
    D4 --> E4["2. Apply lag window w m to r_xx"]
    E4 --> F4["3. DTFT of windowed autocorrelation"]
    F4 --> I4["PSD Estimate"]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style F1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style G2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style I3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
    style I4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Bias–Variance Trade-off Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Periodogram"] -- "Low bias" --> P1A["Asymptotic"]
    P1 -- "High variance" --> P1B["Does not decay with N"]
    P1 -- "Best resolution" --> P1C["0.89 slash NT"]
    B1["Bartlett"] -- "Variance reduced by 1 slash K" --> B1A["K = N slash M"]
    B1 -- "Resolution worse than periodogram" --> B1B["0.89 slash MT"]
    W1["Welch"] -- "Overlapping segments, more K" --> W1A["50 percent overlap typical"]
    W1 -- "Windowing increases bias" --> W1B["Convolution with W f squared"]
    BT1["Blackman-Tukey"] -- "Best variance control via lag window" --> BT1A["M less than N"]
    BT1 -- "Resolution tunes with M" --> BT1B["0.64 slash MT for Hamming"]
    style P1 fill:#FFD700
    style B1 fill:#87CEEB
    style W1 fill:#98FB98
    style BT1 fill:#FFA07A
```

## 4.3 Performance Trade-off Matrix (Visual Summary)

| Performance Axis | Improvement Direction | Methods Ordered (Best → Worst) |
|------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Resolution** | Wider bandwidth = better separation | Periodogram → Welch ≈ Blackman–Tukey → Bartlett |
| **Variance Reduction** | More averaging = lower variance | Welch ≈ Bartlett → Blackman–Tukey → Periodogram |
| **Computational Cost** | FFT count dominant | Periodogram (1 FFT) → Welch ($K$ FFTs) → Bartlett ($K$ FFTs) → Blackman–Tukey (autocorr + FFT) |
| **Bias Control** | Window choice critical | Periodogram (lowest) → Blackman–Tukey → Welch ≈ Bartlett |
| **Ease of Implementation** | Modern DSP toolbox support | Welch > Bartlett > Periodogram > Blackman–Tukey |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exam answers**, always draw a 2-axis sketch: *X-axis = Resolution*, *Y-axis = Variance*. Place the four methods on it and add arrows showing how Welch moves "down and right" relative to the Periodogram. This visual is worth **3–4 marks** in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎓 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**Define the Periodogram. Why is it considered an inconsistent estimator of the PSD?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Periodogram** of a finite data record $x(0), x(1), \ldots, x(N-1)$ is defined as:

$$
\hat{P}_{PER}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\,e^{-j2\pi fn} \right\vert^{2}
$$

It is **inconsistent** because its variance does **not decrease** as $N \to \infty$:

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} \approx P_{xx}^{2}(f)
$$

(For a consistent estimator, the variance should $\to 0$ as $N \to \infty$.) Hence, the periodogram fails to converge in the mean-square sense, motivating the development of Bartlett, Welch, and Blackman–Tukey methods. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`
**What is the role of the lag window $w(m)$ in the Blackman–Tukey method?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The lag window $w(m)$ performs **three critical functions** in the Blackman–Tukey estimator:

1. **Tapers the autocorrelation estimate** $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ to suppress large lags (which are statistically unreliable due to fewer samples).
2. **Controls spectral leakage** by acting as a smooth low-pass filter on the true PSD in the frequency domain (bias is the convolution of $P_{xx}(f)$ with $W(f)$).
3. **Ensures non-negativity** of the estimated PSD because the DTFT of a valid positive-definite autocorrelation must be $\ge 0$.

Common lag windows: **Bartlett (triangular)**, **Hamming**, **Parzen**, **Tukey (raised-cosine)**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** The most common 3-mark deduction is *not stating the resolution–variance trade-off explicitly*. Always write the **bias equation** showing the convolution with the kernel — examiners look for this transition line: *"Expected value is the true PSD convolved with …"*. Failing to mention the convolution loses **3 marks** in 14-mark derivations.

---

### **Q3 (A).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Main]`
**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the **expected value of the periodogram** and show that it is a biased estimator of the true PSD. Identify the kernel of the bias and discuss how it varies with $N$.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a clear block diagram, explain the **Welch method** of PSD estimation. Discuss the role of (i) data window $w(n)$, (ii) segment overlap, and (iii) the normalization constant $U$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Starting from the definition:

$$
\hat{P}_{PER}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j2\pi fn} \right\vert^{2}
$$

Take the expected value:

$$
E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = \frac{1}{N} E\left\{ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(n) x^{*}(m) e^{-j2\pi f(n-m)} \right\}
$$

Swap the expectation and the sums:

$$
E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} E\{x(n)x^{*}(m)\} e^{-j2\pi f(n-m)}
$$

Use the WSS property $E\{x(n)x^{*}(m)\} = r_{xx}(n-m)$:

$$
E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} r_{xx}(n-m) e^{-j2\pi f(n-m)}
$$

Let $k = n - m$ and sum over $k$ (a standard double-sum-to-triangular-sum identity):

$$
E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left(1 - \frac{|k|}{N}\right) r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j2\pi f k}
$$

Recognizing the triangular sequence $\left(1 - \frac{|k|}{N}\right)$ has DTFT equal to the **Fejér kernel**:

$$
W_N(\nu) = \frac{1}{N}\left(\frac{\sin \pi \nu N}{\sin \pi \nu}\right)^{2}
$$

We obtain the **bias convolution equation**:

$$
\boxed{\;E\{\hat{P}_{PER}(f)\} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_{xx}(\nu) \left(\frac{\sin \pi(f-\nu)N}{\pi(f-\nu)N}\right)^{2} d\nu\;}
$$

> The periodogram's mean is the true PSD **convolved with the Fejér kernel** of width $\propto 1/N$. As $N \to \infty$, the kernel tends to a delta (pointwise, away from $f=0$), so the periodogram is **asymptotically unbiased** but **biased for any finite $N$**. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 above for the Welch branch).

The Welch estimator for $K$ overlapping, windowed segments of length $M$:

$$
\hat{P}_{W}(f) = \frac{1}{K\,U}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} w(n)\,x(n+iD)\,e^{-j2\pi fn} \right\vert^{2}
$$

**(i) Data window $w(n)$:**
- Reduces spectral leakage from sidelobes of the implicit rectangular window.
- Lowers bias resolution slightly (mainlobe widens) but improves the side-lobe level.
- Hann and Hamming windows are most common.

**(ii) Segment overlap:**
- With overlap, more segments $K$ can be drawn from the same data $N$.
- With 50% overlap ($D = M/2$), $K \approx 2N/M$ — *twice* the variance reduction of Bartlett for the same $M$.
- Trade-off: segments become correlated, slightly reducing the effective variance reduction; KTU theory assumes $D = M/2$ strikes a good balance.

**(iii) Normalization constant $U$:**
- $U = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} |w(n)|^{2}$ compensates for the energy loss due to windowing.
- Without $U$, the estimator would be biased downward because the windowed segment has less energy than the original.
- Dividing by $MU$ restores an unbiased mainlobe estimate. **(Full 7 marks)**

---

### **Q3 (B).** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary]`
**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **variance of the periodogram** for a real Gaussian process and show that it does not decrease with $N$. How does the **Bartlett method** overcome this limitation? Provide the variance expression for the Bartlett estimator.

**(b) [7 marks]** With necessary equations, explain the **Blackman–Tukey method** of PSD estimation. Compare its bias and variance performance with the Periodogram and the Bartlett method.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Consider the periodogram at a single frequency $f_k$ (omitting the $1/N$ scaling for clarity):

$$
\hat{P}(f_k) = |X(f_k)|^{2} = X(f_k)\,X^{*}(f_k)
$$

For a real, zero-mean Gaussian process, the DTFT $X(f_k)$ is **complex circular Gaussian**. Its variance is:

$$
E\{|X(f_k)|^{2}\} = P_{xx}(f_k)
$$

The variance of the squared magnitude is:

$$
\text{Var}\{|X(f_k)|^{2}\} = E\{|X(f_k)|^{4}\} - [E\{|X(f_k)|^{2}\}]^{2}
$$

For complex circular Gaussian variables, $E\{|X|^{4}\} = 2\,[E\{|X|^{2}\}]^{2}$ (by Isserlis/Wick theorem). Thus:

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}(f_k)\} = P_{xx}^{2}(f_k)
$$

> **Key result:** The variance is **independent of $N$** — the periodogram does not converge. This is the **inconsistency paradox**. **(4 marks)**

**Bartlett's fix:** Split $x(n)$ into $K = N/M$ non-overlapping segments; compute $K$ periodograms; average. Since segments are independent:

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{B}(f)\} = \frac{1}{K}\,P_{xx}^{2}(f) = \frac{M}{N}\,P_{xx}^{2}(f)
$$

> Variance decays as $1/K$ — but at the cost of resolution (segment length drops to $M$). **(3 marks)**

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Blackman–Tukey method:**

$$
\hat{P}_{BT}(f) = \sum_{m=-(M-1)}^{M-1} w(m)\,\hat{r}_{xx}(m)\, e^{-j2\pi f m}
$$

where $M \ll N$ and $w(m)$ is a symmetric lag window (e.g., Bartlett, Hamming).

**Derivation from Wiener–Khinchin:** The true PSD is the DTFT of the true autocorrelation. We only have an estimate $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ for lags $|m| \le M-1$; truncation causes leakage. Multiplying by $w(m)$ smooths the spectrum.

**Bias:** $E\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} P_{xx}(\nu)\, W(f - \nu)\, d\nu$ — the true PSD convolved with the *lag-window's* DTFT $W(f)$. **Resolution $\propto 1/M$**.

**Variance (for large $N$):**

$$
\text{Var}\{\hat{P}_{BT}(f)\} \approx \frac{P_{xx}^{2}(f)}{N} \sum_{m=-(M-1)}^{M-1} w^{2}(m)
$$

The sum $\sum w^{2}(m)$ is a small, fixed number — variance decays as $1/N$, **better than the periodogram** (which doesn't decay) and **comparable to Bartlett** (which decays as $1/K$).

**Comparison summary table (write this in your answer):**

| Metric | Periodogram | Bartlett | Blackman–Tukey |
|--------|-------------|----------|----------------|
| Variance scaling | $\sim P^{2}$ (no decay) | $\sim P^{2}/K$ | $\sim P^{2}/N \cdot \sum w^{2}$ |
| Resolution | $0.89/N T$ | $0.89/M T$ | Tunable via $M$ |
| Bias smoothing kernel | Fejér ($\propto 1/N$) | Fejér ($\propto 1/M$) | $W(f)$ — depends on lag window |
| Best for | Quick spectral snapshots | Long records needing variance control | Short, clean records |

**(Full 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ✅ **Periodogram** = $|X_N(f)|^2 / N$. Variance does **not** decay with $N$. Use only for short, exploratory analysis.
- ✅ **Bartlett** = average of $K$ non-overlapping periodograms. Variance $\downarrow$ by factor $K$. Resolution $\downarrow$ by factor $K$.
- ✅ **Welch** = average of $K$ overlapping, *windowed* periodograms normalized by $U$. **Industry standard** for biomedical PSD (ECG, EEG, HRV).
- ✅ **Blackman–Tukey** = windowed DTFT of estimated autocorrelation. Resolution set by **max lag $M$**, not data length $N$.
- ✅ **Resolution** of all averaged methods: $\approx 0.89/(M T)$ (rectangular equivalent) — wider mainlobe, lower variance.
- ✅ **Bias** is the convolution of the true PSD with the smoothing kernel ($W(f)$ for Welch/BT, Fejér for periodogram/Bartlett).
- ✅ **Variance is a function of the *number of independent averages* $K$** — never confuse this with the smoothing bandwidth.
- ✅ **Biomedical favourites:** **Welch** for EEG/HRV (MATLAB `pwelch`), **Periodogram** for prototype R-R analysis, **Bartlett** for variance-controlled spectral imaging.
- ✅ **Examiner's hot-button:** Always state "**mean-square consistent**" when claiming Welch/BT are *better* than the periodogram.
- ✅ **Overlap rule in Welch:** 50% overlap with Hann window is the canonical choice; never overlap by more than 75%.
- ✅ **Windowing trade-off:** Narrow mainlobe ↔ low side-lobe level. Hann: -31 dB sidelobes; Hamming: -43 dB; Blackman: -58 dB.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"The periodogram has the best resolution but never converges. Welch is what your MATLAB toolbox uses. Blackman–Tukey is the elegant ancestor that biomedical engineers should know for viva questions."* 🌟
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parametric Models: Autoregressive (AR), Moving Average (MA), Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA) Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parametric Models: Autoregressive (AR), Moving Average (MA), Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA) Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Parametric Signal Models?

In **Biomedical Signal Processing**, signals such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and PCG are often corrupted by noise, have short stationary segments, or contain subtle spectral features buried within a few hundred samples. Traditional non-parametric spectral estimation (using the Periodogram or Welch method) suffers from **spectral leakage**, **high variance**, and **poor frequency resolution** when only short data records are available (as is the case with most biosignals).

**Parametric Models** offer an elegant mathematical workaround. Instead of estimating the Power Spectral Density (PSD) directly from FFT bins, we assume that the biosignal `y(n)` can be generated by passing a white noise sequence `w(n)` through a **linear, time-invariant (LTI) discrete-time filter** characterized by a small set of parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Parametric Modeling):**
> A parametric model describes a random signal `y(n)` as the output of a causal, linear difference equation driven by white noise `w(n)` with variance `\sigma_w^2`. The signal is fully characterized by the model coefficients (`a_k`, `b_k`) and the order of the model (`p`, `q`), rather than by its sampled values.

The three fundamental parametric model families studied in this module are:

| Model | Abbreviation | Biological Signal Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Autoregressive** | **AR** | EEG rhythms (Alpha, Beta, Theta) — all-pole modeling |
| **Moving Average** | **MA** | Evoked potentials (EPs) — all-zero modeling |
| **Autoregressive Moving Average** | **ARMA** | ECG beats, HRV — mixed pole-zero modeling |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Patient Memory" Analogy

Imagine a doctor trying to predict the **next heart rate (HR) of a sleeping patient** based only on the past few readings.

- **Autoregressive (AR) thinking:** *"The next heart rate depends mostly on the previous few heart rates, plus a tiny random nervous-system fluctuation."* → The signal **remembers its own past values**.
- **Moving Average (MA) thinking:** *"The current heart rate is just the immediate response to the last few random bursts of adrenaline/sympathetic firing, with no dependence on prior heart rates."* → The signal is shaped **entirely by recent random shocks**.
- **ARMA thinking:** *"The current heart rate is influenced both by what the heart was doing recently (memory) AND by the latest neural impulses (shocks)."* → The most realistic model for biomedical signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use parametric models for biomedical signals?** Three core reasons:
> 1. **Higher spectral resolution** from short data segments (EEG bursts, single ECG beats).
> 2. **Smoother PSD estimate** since only `p + q + 1` parameters need to be estimated instead of `N` FFT bins.
> 3. **Compact feature extraction** — the coefficients `a_k` themselves serve as features for classifying normal vs. pathological signals (e.g., seizure vs. non-seizure EEG).

---

## 1.3 The General Parametric Framework

Any discrete-time parametric model can be expressed using the **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** form:

$$
y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, w(n-k)
$$

where `y(n)` is the biosignal sample at time `n`, `w(n)` is the white-noise input with zero mean and variance `\sigma_w^2`, and `{a_k}` and `{b_k}` are the model coefficients.

In the **z-domain**, applying the Z-transform yields the system function:

$$
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{W(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}
$$

The choice of which coefficients are zero defines whether we have an AR, MA, or ARMA model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Autoregressive (AR) Model — "All-Pole Model"

### 2.1.1 Mathematical Formulation

An **AR model of order `p`**, denoted **AR(p)**, expresses the current sample as a **weighted linear combination of its own `p` past samples** plus a white-noise innovation term:

$$
y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, y(n-k) + w(n)
$$

The corresponding transfer function is **all-pole** (no finite zeros, except at `z = 0`):

$$
H_{AR}(z) = \frac{1}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}
$$

### 2.1.2 Why AR for EEG?

The cerebral cortex behaves as a **resonant filter network**. Rhythmic activity (Alpha at 8–13 Hz, Beta at 13–30 Hz) is the **output of underdamped resonators** — a perfect physical analogy to an all-pole recursive filter where the poles represent the brain's natural resonances.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Constraint:** All `p` poles of `H_{AR}(z)` must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane (`\vert z_i \vert < 1`). This guarantees a **stable, causal, and stationary** output.

---

## 2.2 Moving Average (MA) Model — "All-Zero Model"

### 2.2.1 Mathematical Formulation

An **MA model of order `q`**, denoted **MA(q)**, expresses the current sample as a **weighted sum of the current and `q` most recent white-noise samples**:

$$
y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k \, w(n-k)
$$

The corresponding transfer function is **all-zero** (no finite poles):

$$
H_{MA}(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k z^{-k}
$$

### 2.2.2 Why MA for Evoked Potentials?

Evoked Potentials (EPs) are **transient stimulus-locked responses** that decay to zero in ~300 ms. They look like a short-duration pulse — exactly what an all-zero FIR filter produces when fed a unit impulse. Hence the MA model captures EP morphology efficiently.

---

## 2.3 Autoregressive Moving Average (ARMA) Model — "Pole-Zero Model"

### 2.3.1 Mathematical Formulation

An **ARMA model of orders `(p, q)`** combines both AR and MA structures:

$$
y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k \, w(n-k)
$$

The transfer function has **both poles and zeros**:

$$
H_{ARMA}(z) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}
$$

### 2.3.2 Why ARMA for ECG?

Each ECG beat contains both:
- **Sharp QRS spikes** (modeled by the **zeros** — short-duration features), and
- **Underlying P-T waves and rhythm** (modeled by the **poles** — longer-term resonances).

Hence ARMA captures **both spectral peaks and nulls** efficiently with fewer parameters than a pure AR or pure MA model.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **examination-critical**. Memorize every entry before attempting KTU questions.

| # | Quantity / Concept | AR Model (p) | MA Model (q) | ARMA Model (p, q) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time-domain equation** | $y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k y(n-k) + w(n)$ | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k w(n-k)$ | $y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k w(n-k)$ |
| 2 | **Transfer function `H(z)`** | $\dfrac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ | $\sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k z^{-k}$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ |
| 3 | **Nature of poles/zeros** | All-pole | All-zero | Pole-zero |
| 4 | **Filter type** | IIR (recursive) | FIR (non-recursive) | IIR (recursive) |
| 5 | **PSD `P_{yy}(f)`** | $\dfrac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$ | $\sigma_w^2 \cdot \left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2$ | $\sigma_w^2 \cdot \dfrac{\left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$ |
| 6 | **Order selection criterion** | **AIC** / **BIC** / **MDL** | AIC / BIC / MDL | AIC / BIC / MDL |
| 7 | **Coefficient estimation** | **Yule-Walker** (Levinson-Durbin) | **High-order AR approximation** | **Maximum Likelihood** / **Least Squares** |
| 8 | **Number of parameters** | `p + 1` (coeffs + noise variance) | `q + 1` | `p + q + 1` |
| 9 | **Typical biosignal** | EEG rhythms | Evoked potentials | ECG, HRV, EMG |
| 10 | **Stability rule** | All poles `\vert z_i \vert < 1` | Always stable | All poles `\vert z_i \vert < 1` |

### 2.4.1 Key Helper Formulas

**Yule-Walker Equations for AR(p)** — for `m \geq 1`:

$$
r_{yy}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{yy}(m-k) = 0
$$

**Yule-Walker for `m = 0`** (used to compute noise variance `\sigma_w^2`):

$$
r_{yy}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{yy}(-k) = \sigma_w^2
$$

Since `r_{yy}(-k) = r_{yy}(k)` (autocorrelation is even):

$$
\sigma_w^2 = r_{yy}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{yy}(k)
$$

**Reflection Coefficient (for Levinson-Durbin recursion):**

$$
k_m = \frac{r_{yy}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1,k} \, r_{yy}(m-k)}{r_{yy}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1,k} \, r_{yy}(k)}
$$

**Akaike Information Criterion (AIC):**

$$
\text{AIC}(p) = N \ln(\sigma_w^2) + 2p
$$

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Significance

Parametric models are not just theoretical constructs; they form the **backbone of clinical decision-support systems**:

1. **EEG Seizure Detection** — AR coefficients of short EEG epochs (1–2 s) are used as features in classifiers (SVM, LSTM) to flag epileptic seizures automatically.
2. **ECG R-peak Detection & Arrhythmia Classification** — ARMA models of each beat capture both morphology and rhythm; coefficients feed into deep-learning models for atrial fibrillation (AF) detection.
3. **Heart Rate Variability (HRV) Analysis** — AR models of the RR-interval tachogram reveal autonomic nervous system balance (LF/HF ratio = sympathetic vs. parasympathetic).
4. **Speech-Loss Pathology Detection** — AR modeling of vocal tract transfers to medical voice analysis (e.g., Parkinson's disease detection).
5. **EMG Muscle Fatigue Estimation** — Time-varying AR coefficients track spectral compression as muscles fatigue.

> [!NOTE]
> The famous **Levinson-Durbin recursion** solves the Yule-Walker equations in `O(p^2)` time (vs `O(p^3)` for general matrix inversion). It is therefore the **industry-standard** algorithm for AR modeling of biosignals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the AR Model Output from First Principles

We begin with the **general linear difference equation** that defines a causal LTI discrete system:

$$
y(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k w(n-k)
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Z-transform to both sides.**

$$
Y(z) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k} Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k} W(z)
$$

Here we used the **time-shift property** of the Z-transform:
$$
\mathcal{Z}\{y(n-k)\} = z^{-k} Y(z)
$$

**Step 2 — Factor out `Y(z)` on the left-hand side.**

$$
Y(z)\left[1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}\right] = W(z)\left[\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the transfer function `H(z) = Y(z) / W(z)`.**

$$
H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{W(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the three model families by setting coefficients to zero.**

- **AR(p):** Set `b_0 = 1` and `b_1 = b_2 = \dots = b_M = 0`; keep all `a_k` up to `k = p`.
- **MA(q):** Set all `a_k = 0`; keep `b_k` up to `k = q`.
- **ARMA(p, q):** Keep both sets of coefficients up to orders `p` and `q`.

**Step 5 — Recover the time-domain difference equation** for AR(p):

$$
\left[1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}\right]Y(z) = W(z)
$$

Taking the inverse Z-transform:

$$
y(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k y(n-k) = w(n)
$$

Rearranging gives the **canonical AR(p) form**:

$$
\boxed{\; y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k y(n-k) + w(n) \;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Power Spectral Density (PSD) for AR, MA, ARMA

For a **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** random process driven by white noise with PSD `\sigma_w^2`, the output PSD is given by:

$$
P_{yy}(f) = \sigma_w^2 \cdot \left\vert H(f) \right\vert^2 = \sigma_w^2 \cdot H(f) H^*(f)
$$

where `H(f) = H(z) \big|_{z = e^{j2\pi f}}` (evaluate `H(z)` on the unit circle).

### 3.2.1 AR Model PSD

$$
H_{AR}(f) = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k}}
$$

Hence:

$$
P_{AR}(f) = \sigma_w^2 \cdot \left\vert \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k}} \right\vert^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\; P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2} \;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The poles of the denominator create **spectral peaks** (resonances) at frequencies where the denominator magnitude is small. This is why AR models excel at representing signals with sharp spectral peaks, such as EEG alpha rhythms.

### 3.2.2 MA Model PSD

$$
H_{MA}(f) = \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k e^{-j2\pi f k}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\; P_{MA}(f) = \sigma_w^2 \cdot \left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2 \;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The zeros create **spectral notches** (nulls) at frequencies where the numerator magnitude vanishes. MA models excel at representing signals with sharp spectral notches, such as Evoked Potentials riding on background EEG.

### 3.2.3 ARMA Model PSD

$$
H_{ARMA}(f) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k e^{-j2\pi f k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k}}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\; P_{ARMA}(f) = \sigma_w^2 \cdot \frac{\left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{q} b_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right\vert^2} \;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The poles still produce peaks; the zeros still produce nulls. ARMA models therefore capture both — at the cost of more parameters and a non-linear estimation problem.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Yule-Walker Equations for AR(p)

We derive the relationship between AR coefficients and the autocorrelation sequence `r_{yy}(m) = E[y(n) y(n+m)]`.

**Step 1 — Start from the AR difference equation.**

$$
y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k y(n-k) + w(n)
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by `y(n-m)` and take the expectation.**

$$
E\left[y(n) y(n-m)\right] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, E\left[y(n-k) y(n-m)\right] + E\left[w(n) y(n-m)\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize each expectation as an autocorrelation value.**

$$
r_{yy}(-m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{yy}(m-k) + E\left[w(n) y(n-m)\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the cross-term `E[w(n) y(n-m)]`.**

For `m > 0`, `y(n-m)` depends only on `w(\tau)` for `\tau \leq n-m-1 < n`. Since `w(n)` is white noise, it is **uncorrelated with all past inputs**, hence `E[w(n) y(n-m)] = 0` for `m \geq 1`.

For `m = 0`:
$$
y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k y(n-k) + w(n)
$$
Multiplying by `w(n)` and taking expectation: only the `w(n) \cdot w(n)` term survives (all cross terms vanish due to whiteness). Hence:
$$
E[w(n) y(n)] = \sigma_w^2
$$

**Step 5 — Use autocorrelation symmetry `r_{yy}(-m) = r_{yy}(m)`.**

For `m \geq 1`:
$$
r_{yy}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{yy}(m-k)
$$

$$
\boxed{\; r_{yy}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{yy}(m-k) = 0, \quad m \geq 1 \;}
$$

For `m = 0`:
$$
\sigma_w^2 = r_{yy}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, r_{yy}(k)
$$

These `p + 1` linear equations in `p + 1` unknowns `{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p, \sigma_w^2}` form the **Yule-Walker Normal Equations**, solvable in `O(p^2)` time via Levinson-Durbin recursion.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — AR(2) Coefficient Estimation

**Given:** A 2nd-order AR model produces signal `y(n)` with the following autocorrelation values:

$$
r_{yy}(0) = 4.0, \quad r_{yy}(1) = 2.5, \quad r_{yy}(2) = 1.5
$$

**Find:** The AR(2) coefficients `a_1`, `a_2` and noise variance `\sigma_w^2`.

**Step 1 — Write the Yule-Walker equations.**

For `m = 1`:
$$
r_{yy}(1) + a_1 r_{yy}(0) + a_2 r_{yy}(-1) = 0
$$
Since `r_{yy}(-1) = r_{yy}(1) = 2.5`:
$$
2.5 + a_1 (4.0) + a_2 (2.5) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4.0 a_1 + 2.5 a_2 = -2.5
$$

For `m = 2`:
$$
r_{yy}(2) + a_1 r_{yy}(1) + a_2 r_{yy}(0) = 0
$$
$$
1.5 + a_1 (2.5) + a_2 (4.0) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2.5 a_1 + 4.0 a_2 = -1.5
$$

**Step 2 — Solve the 2×2 linear system.**

Using Cramer's rule, the determinant is:
$$
\Delta = (4.0)(4.0) - (2.5)(2.5) = 16.0 - 6.25 = 9.75
$$

$$
a_1 = \frac{(-2.5)(4.0) - (2.5)(-1.5)}{9.75} = \frac{-10.0 + 3.75}{9.75} = \frac{-6.25}{9.75} \approx -0.6410
$$

$$
a_2 = \frac{(4.0)(-1.5) - (-2.5)(2.5)}{9.75} = \frac{-6.0 + 6.25}{9.75} = \frac{0.25}{9.75} \approx 0.02564
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the noise variance.**

$$
\sigma_w^2 = r_{yy}(0) + a_1 r_{yy}(1) + a_2 r_{yy}(2)
$$
$$
\sigma_w^2 = 4.0 + (-0.6410)(2.5) + (0.02564)(1.5)
$$
$$
\sigma_w^2 = 4.0 - 1.6025 + 0.03846 \approx 2.436
$$

**Step 4 — Verify stability** by checking pole locations.

Characteristic polynomial:
$$
1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} = 1 - 0.6410 z^{-1} + 0.02564 z^{-2} = 0
$$

Multiply by `z^2`:
$$
z^2 - 0.6410 z + 0.02564 = 0
$$

Roots:
$$
z = \frac{0.6410 \pm \sqrt{0.6410^2 - 4(0.02564)}}{2} = \frac{0.6410 \pm \sqrt{0.3083}}{2}
$$
$$
z_1, z_2 \approx 0.598 \quad \text{and} \quad 0.0429
$$

Both `\vert z_i \vert < 1` → **Model is stable and causal.** ✓

---

## 3.5 Pole-Zero Pattern Comparison (Analytical Summary)

The analytical distinction between the three models is best summarized by their pole-zero structures:

| Model Type | # Finite Poles | # Finite Zeros | # Poles at Origin (z=0) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AR(p)** | `p` | 0 | `p` (from `z^{-p}` factor in numerator when written in polynomial form) |
| **MA(q)** | 0 | `q` | 0 |
| **ARMA(p, q)** | `p` | `q` | `\max(p-q, 0)` |

> [!NOTE]
> Every pole-zero pair can be moved: a pole close to the unit circle at angle `\theta` produces a resonance at frequency `f = \theta / (2\pi)`. A zero on the unit circle at angle `\phi` produces a spectral null at frequency `f = \phi / (2\pi)`. Together they sculpt the PSD of any biomedical signal.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Diagram Architecture of the Three Parametric Models

The following Mermaid block diagrams show the signal-flow architecture of each model. Note that **AR** uses **feedback** (recursive), **MA** uses only **feedforward** (non-recursive), and **ARMA** uses **both**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AR["AR(p) Model — All-Pole, Recursive"]
        wAR["w(n) — White Noise Input"] --> sumAR(["+ node"])
        sumAR --> delayAR1["z⁻¹"]
        delayAR1 --> delayAR2["z⁻¹"]
        delayAR2 --> delayARp["z⁻¹ ... to p"]
        delayARp --> a1["× a_p"]
        a1 --> sumAR
        sumAR --> yAR["y(n) — Biosignal Output"]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MA["MA(q) Model — All-Zero, Non-Recursive"]
        wMA["w(n) — White Noise Input"] --> b0["× b_0"]
        wMA --> delay1["z⁻¹"]
        delay1 --> b1["× b_1"]
        delay1 --> delay2["z⁻¹"]
        delay2 --> b2["× b_2"]
        delay2 --> delayq["z⁻¹ ... to q"]
        delayq --> bq["× b_q"]
        b0 --> sumMA(["+ node"])
        b1 --> sumMA
        b2 --> sumMA
        bq --> sumMA
        sumMA --> yMA["y(n) — Biosignal Output"]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ARMA["ARMA(p, q) Model — Pole-Zero, Recursive"]
        wA["w(n)"] --> b0A["× b_0"]
        wA --> dA1["z⁻¹"]
        dA1 --> b1A["× b_1"]
        dA1 --> dA2["z⁻¹"]
        dA2 --> b2A["× b_2"]
        dA2 --> dAq["z⁻¹ ... to q"]
        dAq --> bqA["× b_q"]
        b0A --> sumA(["Σ node"])
        b1A --> sumA
        b2A --> sumA
        bqA --> sumA
        sumA --> sumB(["+ output"])
        sumB --> dB1["z⁻¹"]
        dB1 --> dB2["z⁻¹"]
        dB2 --> dBp["z⁻¹ ... to p"]
        dBp --> a_p["× a_p"]
        a_p --> sumB
        sumB --> yA["y(n) — Biosignal Output"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the ARMA diagram above, observe the **two feedback loops**: the AR feedback (lower path returning to `sumB`) and the MA feedforward (upper path through `b_k` gains). The single summing junction `sumB` combines both to form the output.

## 4.3 Model Selection Flowchart — Choosing AR vs MA vs ARMA

The following flowchart encapsulates the **practical decision process** a biomedical engineer should follow when picking a parametric model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Have a biosignal y n]) --> Pre["Preprocess: Remove mean, detrend, band-limit filter"]
    Pre --> Q1{"Does the signal\nhave sharp spectral\nPEAKS? (EEG, HRV)"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> AR["Use AR(p) model\nAll-pole, sharp peaks\nEstimate via Yule-Walker"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Does the signal\nhave sharp spectral\nNULLS? (EPs, transients)"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> MA["Use MA(q) model\nAll-zero, sharp nulls\nEstimate via high-order AR fit"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Need both peaks\nAND nulls?\n(ECG, EMG, complex biosignals)"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> ARMA["Use ARMA(p, q) model\nEstimate via ML or Least-Squares"]
    Q3 -- No --> ARMA

    AR --> Order["Select order p using\nAIC, BIC, or MDL criterion"]
    MA --> Order2["Select order q using\nAIC, BIC, or MDL criterion"]
    ARMA --> Order3["Select orders p and q\nusing AIC grid search"]

    Order --> Validate["Validate model:\nWhiteness of residuals\nTest on held-out data"]
    Order2 --> Validate
    Order3 --> Validate
    Validate --> Done([End: Deploy for\nclassification / feature\nextraction])
```
