topics.title: Spectrum Estimation of EEG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spectrum Estimation of EEG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spectrum Estimation of EEG** refers to the computational procedure of estimating the Power Spectral Density (PSD) of electroencephalogram signals to quantitatively characterize the frequency content of brain electrical activity. The EEG is a non-stationary, stochastic biomedical signal with dominant spectral energy concentrated in the **0.5 Hz to 30 Hz** band, sub-divided into five canonical rhythms: **Delta (δ): 0.5–4 Hz**, **Theta (θ): 4–8 Hz**, **Alpha (α): 8–13 Hz**, **Beta (β): 13–30 Hz**, and **Gamma (γ): >30 Hz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Spectrum estimation is a mandatory topic because clinical diagnosis of neurological disorders (epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies) relies almost entirely on quantitative frequency-domain characterization of EEG, not on raw time-domain waveforms.

The mathematical goal is to compute $P_{xx}(f)$, the power spectral density function, defined as the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation sequence $r_{xx}(\tau)$:

$$P_{xx}(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(\tau) e^{-j2\pi f \tau} \, d\tau$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a busy railway station hearing a chaotic mixture of train horns, announcements, footsteps, and music. Your **brain** automatically separates these sounds by their "frequency fingerprint" — horns are low-pitched, footsteps are mid-pitched, announcements are high-pitched. This is exactly what **spectrum estimation does to an EEG signal**.

The EEG waveform recorded from the scalp is a "soup" of overlapping brain rhythms from billions of neurons. By estimating its spectrum, we essentially run the signal through a **mathematical prism** that splits it into its constituent frequency bands, allowing clinicians to identify which rhythm is dominant during wakefulness, sleep, or seizure activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Clinical Insight:** A normal awake adult with eyes closed shows a strong **Alpha peak at ~10 Hz** in occipital electrodes (the well-known "posterior dominant rhythm"). Absence or abnormality of this peak is clinically diagnostic.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Frequency (Fs):** Typically **100 Hz, 200 Hz, or 256 Hz** (Nyquist must be ≥ 2 × highest EEG frequency).
- **Standard Montage:** 10-20 International System.
- **Reference Electrode:** Linked ears, Cz, or average reference.
- **Resolution Bandwidth:** 0.5 Hz (clinical), 0.25 Hz (research-grade).
- **Epoch Length:** 2–4 seconds for stationary segments, longer for low-frequency rhythms.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Spectrum Estimation Methods

Spectrum estimation techniques for EEG fall into two broad categories:

**A. Non-Parametric (Classical) Methods**
These methods directly compute the PSD from the FFT of the signal without assuming any underlying model. They are simple, robust, and widely used clinically.

- **Periodogram** (Schuster, 1898): Direct FFT magnitude squared
- **Bartlett's Method**: Averaged periodogram over non-overlapping segments
- **Welch's Method**: Averaged periodogram over overlapping windowed segments (most popular for EEG)
- **Blackman-Tukey Method**: PSD via windowed autocorrelation

**B. Parametric (Model-Based) Methods**
These methods assume the EEG can be generated by a linear stochastic model (typically AutoRegressive AR), then estimate the model parameters.

- **AR (AutoRegressive) Modeling**: Most popular for short EEG epochs
- **MA (Moving Average)** modeling
- **ARMA (combined)** modeling

### 2.2 The Periodogram — Foundational Concept

The periodogram is the squared magnitude of the normalized FFT:

$$P_{PER}(f) = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

**Limitations (KTU-favorite exam point):**
- High variance: variance does not decrease as N increases
- Biased estimate (windowing introduces leakage)
- Poor frequency resolution for short data records

### 2.3 Welch's Method (Modified Periodogram) — Clinical Standard

Welch's method addresses the variance problem by dividing the signal into overlapping segments, windowing each, computing periodograms, and averaging:

$$P_{WELCH}(f) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_m(n) w(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

where $x_m(n)$ is the m-th segment, $w(n)$ is a window (Hamming/Hann), $L$ is segment length, $M$ is total segments, and overlap is typically **50% or 75%**.

### 2.4 Parametric AR Modeling for EEG

For short EEG epochs (1–2 seconds), AR models outperform FFT-based methods because they provide smoother spectral estimates. An AR(p) model is:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$$

where $w(n) \sim N(0, \sigma_w^2)$ is white noise, and $a_k$ are AR coefficients estimated via:
- **Yule-Walker equations** (autocorrelation method)
- **Burg's method** (most popular — works well for short EEG segments)
- **Least squares / covariance method**

The PSD of an AR(p) process is:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left| 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right|^2}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Use Case | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autocorrelation | $r_{xx}(\tau) = E[x(t)x(t+\tau)]$ | Base of PSD | Power |
| Wiener-Khinchin Theorem | $P_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{r_{xx}(\tau)\}$ | PSD ↔ Autocorr | W/Hz |
| Periodogram | $P_{PER}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\vert X(f) \vert^2$ | Baseline PSD | W/Hz |
| Bartlett Average | $P_{B}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} P_k(f)$ | Variance reduction | K segments |
| Welch PSD | $P_{W}(f) = \frac{1}{MU}\sum_{m}\vert X_m(f) \vert^2$ | Clinical standard | U = window energy |
| AR(p) PSD | $P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\vert 1+\sum a_k e^{-j2\pi fk} \vert^2}$ | Short EEG epochs | $\sigma_w^2$ = noise var |
| Yule-Walker | $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}$ | AR coefficient solving | Toeplitz matrix $\mathbf{R}$ |
| Model Order (Akaike) | $AIC(p) = N\ln(\sigma_w^2) + 2p$ | AR order selection | Min AIC |
| Delta rhythm | 0.5–4 Hz | Deep sleep, infants | High amplitude |
| Theta rhythm | 4–8 Hz | Drowsiness, memory | – |
| Alpha rhythm | 8–13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness | Occipital |
| Beta rhythm | 13–30 Hz | Active thinking | Frontal |
| Gamma rhythm | >30 Hz | Cognitive binding | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Spectrum estimation of EEG is foundational to **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)**, **seizure detection algorithms**, **sleep staging (polysomnography)**, **depth-of-anesthesia monitors (BIS)**, and **neuromarketing research**.

### 2.6 The "Why" Behind Method Selection

- **Use Welch** when you have ≥ 5 seconds of clean EEG and want reliable power estimates per band.
- **Use AR modeling** when epochs are short (1–2 s) and you need sharp spectral peaks — important for detecting alpha spindles, spike-wave discharges in epilepsy.
- **Use Welch + AR together** for clinical long-term monitoring: Welch for trend PSD, AR for event-locked transients.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Autocorrelation to Periodogram

**Starting Point:** Define the sample autocorrelation estimate from N data points:

$$\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-m-1} x(n) x(n+m), \quad m = 0,1,\ldots,N-1$$

**Step 1:** Apply the Wiener-Khinchin theorem — the PSD is the DTFT of the autocorrelation sequence:

$$P_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m) e^{-j2\pi f m}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the finite-sample estimate $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ (non-zero only for $0 \le m \le N-1$):

$$\hat{P}_{xx}(f) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}(m) e^{-j2\pi f m}$$

**Step 3:** Express this as a product of FFTs using the convolution theorem. The cross-correlation $r_{xx}(m)$ equals the convolution $x(m) * x(-m)$:

$$\hat{P}_{PER}(f) = \frac{1}{N} X(f) X^*(f) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(f) \vert^2$$

This is the **Periodogram**. The conversion logic: by the convolution property of DTFT, multiplication in frequency domain = convolution in time domain, so $|X(f)|^2$ is the DTFT of the autocorrelation, which equals the PSD.

### 3.2 Derivation: Welch's Method Spectral Estimator

**Step 1:** Segment the data $x(n)$ of length N into M overlapping segments of length L with overlap D:

$$x_m(n) = x(n + mD), \quad n = 0,1,\ldots,L-1, \quad m = 0,1,\ldots,M-1$$

**Step 2:** Apply a window $w(n)$ (Hamming/Hann) to each segment:

$$x_m^w(n) = x_m(n) \cdot w(n)$$

**Step 3:** Compute the periodogram of each windowed segment:

$$P_m(f) = \frac{1}{U} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_m^w(n) e^{-j2\pi f n} \right|^2$$

where $U = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ is the window energy normalization factor.

**Step 4:** Average across all M segments to reduce variance:

$$P_{WELCH}(f) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} P_m(f)$$

**Step 5 (Variance Analysis):** Since periodograms of disjoint segments are approximately independent, the variance is reduced by factor M:

$$\text{Var}[P_{WELCH}(f)] \approx \frac{1}{M} \text{Var}[P_{PER}(f)]$$

Trade-off: frequency resolution drops from $\frac{F_s}{N}$ to $\frac{F_s}{L}$ (acceptable for EEG since bands are wide).

### 3.3 Derivation: AR Model PSD via Yule-Walker

**Starting AR(p) difference equation:**

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$$

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $x(n-m)$ and take expectation:

$$E[x(n)x(n-m)] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E[x(n-k)x(n-m)] + E[w(n)x(n-m)]$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $w(n)$ is uncorrelated with past $x$ values, so the last term is zero for $m \ge 1$:

$$r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k), \quad m = 1,2,\ldots,p$$

**Step 3:** Write this as the matrix form (Toeplitz):

$$\begin{bmatrix}
r(0) & r(1) & \cdots & r(p-1) \\
r(1) & r(0) & \cdots & r(p-2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
r(p-1) & r(p-2) & \cdots & r(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix}
= -\begin{bmatrix} r(1) \\ r(2) \\ \vdots \\ r(p) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Solve via Levinson-Durbin recursion (O($p^2$) complexity, efficient for real-time EEG).

**Step 5:** Substitute AR coefficients into the rational PSD formula:

$$P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left| 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j2\pi f k} \right|^2}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: EEG Alpha Peak Detection

**Given:** A 2-second EEG epoch sampled at $F_s = 256$ Hz. We apply Welch's method with Hamming window, $L = 256$ samples (1 sec), 50% overlap.

**Step 1:** Number of segments:
$$M = \frac{N - L}{D} + 1 = \frac{512 - 256}{128} + 1 = 3$$

**Step 2:** Compute FFT of each windowed segment: $X_m(k)$ for $k = 0,1,\ldots,L-1$.

**Step 3:** For each frequency bin $f_k = \frac{k F_s}{L}$:

$$\hat{P}(f_k) = \frac{1}{3 \cdot U} \sum_{m=1}^{3} \vert X_m(k) \vert^2$$

**Step 4:** Locate spectral peak. If peak occurs at $k^* = 10$ in a 256-point FFT:

$$f_{peak} = \frac{10 \times 256}{256} = 10 \text{ Hz} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Alpha rhythm confirmed}$$

**Step 5:** Compute relative band power for clinical report:

$$\text{BandPower}_\alpha = \sum_{f=8 \text{Hz}}^{13 \text{Hz}} \hat{P}(f) \Delta f$$

This quantitative value becomes the biomarker reported to the neurologist.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EEG Spectrum Estimation Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw EEG Signal xn"] --> B["Preprocessing: Bandpass 0.5 to 40 Hz"]
    B --> C["Notch Filter at 50 Hz Line Noise Removal"]
    C --> D["Artifact Rejection: EOG EMG Channels"]
    D --> E{"Method Selection"}
    E --> F["Welch Periodogram Path"]
    E --> G["AR Model Path"]
    F --> F1["Segment Signal: 2 sec Epochs"]
    F1 --> F2["Apply Hamming Window"]
    F2 --> F3["Compute FFT of Each Segment"]
    F3 --> F4["Average Periodograms"]
    F4 --> H["PSD Estimate Pxx f"]
    G --> G1["Select Model Order p via AIC"]
    G1 --> G2["Estimate AR Coefficients via Burg Method"]
    G2 --> G3["Compute Rational PSD"]
    G3 --> H
    H --> I["Band Power Extraction: Delta Theta Alpha Beta"]
    I --> J["Clinical Decision Support Output"]
```

### 4.2 EEG Frequency Band Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EEGSpectrum["EEG Frequency Spectrum 0.5 to 100 Hz"]
        direction TB
        delta["Delta 0.5 to 4 Hz: Deep Sleep Infants"]
        theta["Theta 4 to 8 Hz: Drowsiness Memory Encoding"]
        alpha["Alpha 8 to 13 Hz: Relaxed Wakefulness Eyes Closed"]
        beta["Beta 13 to 30 Hz: Active Thinking Concentration"]
        gamma["Gamma above 30 Hz: Cognitive Binding Perception"]
    end
    delta --> theta
    theta --> alpha
    alpha --> beta
    beta --> gamma
```

### 4.3 Welch vs AR Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["EEG Epoch Available"] --> B{"Epoch Length"}
    B -->|Less than 2 seconds| C["Use AR p = 6 to 12"]
    B -->|Greater than 5 seconds| D["Use Welch Method"]
    B -->|Between 2 and 5| E["Both Methods: Compare"]
    C --> F["Burg or Yule Walker Solver"]
    D --> G["50 percent Overlap Hamming Window"]
    F --> H["Clinical PSD Output"]
    G --> H
    E --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Power Spectral Density (PSD) of an EEG signal and state its clinical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
Power Spectral Density (PSD) of an EEG signal is defined as the distribution of signal power as a function of frequency, mathematically given by the Fourier transform of the autocorrelation function (Wiener-Khinchin theorem). For EEG, it decomposes the complex time-domain waveform into physiologically meaningful rhythmic components — Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, and Gamma — each associated with specific brain states. **Clinical significance:** PSD allows quantitative detection of abnormalities such as alpha coma (loss of alpha peak), generalized spike-wave patterns in epilepsy, and sleep-stage transitions, which would be invisible or ambiguous in raw time-domain EEG traces. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List the five standard EEG frequency bands with their frequency ranges and one associated brain state each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Band | Frequency Range | Associated Brain State |
|------|----------------|------------------------|
| Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants |
| Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness, light sleep, memory encoding |
| Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| Beta (β) | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking, concentration, anxiety |
| Gamma (γ) | > 30 Hz | Cognitive binding, perception, consciousness |

**(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Welch's Method

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the limitations of the basic periodogram for EEG spectrum estimation. Derive the expression for Welch's averaged periodogram method.**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of Basic Periodogram:**
1. **High variance** — the variance of the periodogram does not decrease with increasing data length N; it remains of the same order as $P^2_{xx}(f)$.
2. **Spectral leakage** — finite-duration truncation is equivalent to multiplying the signal by a rectangular window, causing sidelobe leakage that smears sharp spectral peaks (e.g., alpha peak).
3. **Bias** — the rectangular window's spectral mainlobe introduces bias; close frequencies cannot be resolved.

**Derivation of Welch's Method:**

**Step 1:** The original signal $x(n)$ of length N is segmented into M overlapping segments of length L with shift D (overlap = L − D):

$$x_i(n) = x(n + iD), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, L-1$$

**Step 2:** Each segment is multiplied by a window function $w(n)$:

$$x_i^w(n) = x_i(n) \cdot w(n)$$

**Step 3:** The periodogram of each windowed segment is computed:

$$P_i(f) = \frac{1}{U} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i^w(n) e^{-j2\pi fn} \right|^2$$

where $U = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ is the window-energy normalization constant.

**Step 4:** Welch's PSD estimate is the linear average of all segment periodograms:

$$\boxed{P_{WELCH}(f) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=0}^{M-1} P_i(f)}$$

**Step 5 (Variance Reduction):** Assuming segment periodograms are approximately independent:

$$\text{Var}[P_{WELCH}(f)] \approx \frac{1}{M} \text{Var}[P_{PER}(f)]$$

Hence Welch's method reduces variance by factor M while accepting reduced frequency resolution (resolution becomes $F_s/L$ instead of $F_s/N$). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] For an EEG signal sampled at $F_s = 256$ Hz, an analyst uses Welch's method with Hamming window of length L = 512 samples (2 seconds) and 50% overlap. Compute: (i) the frequency resolution, (ii) the number of segments M if total recording length is 10 seconds, and (iii) the variance reduction factor.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Frequency Resolution:**
The frequency resolution of Welch's method is determined by the windowed segment length L:

$$\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{L} = \frac{256 \text{ Hz}}{512} = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Number of Segments M:**
Total samples: $N = F_s \times T = 256 \times 10 = 2560$ samples.
Overlap = 50% → shift $D = L/2 = 256$ samples.
Number of segments:

$$M = \frac{N - L}{D} + 1 = \frac{2560 - 512}{256} + 1 = \frac{2048}{256} + 1 = 8 + 1 = 9 \text{ segments}$$

**(iii) Variance Reduction Factor:**
Welch's method reduces periodogram variance by approximately M (assuming approximate independence of segment periodograms):

$$\text{Variance Reduction Factor} = M = 9$$

Thus Welch's PSD estimate has 1/9th the variance of the basic periodogram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — AR Modeling (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks] With relevant equations, explain the AutoRegressive (AR) model for EEG spectrum estimation. Why are AR models preferred for short-duration EEG epochs?**

**Model Solution:**

An AutoRegressive model of order p, denoted AR(p), expresses the current EEG sample $x(n)$ as a linear combination of its p past samples plus a white-noise innovation term $w(n)$:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x(n-k) + w(n)$$

where $a_k$ are the AR coefficients and $w(n) \sim N(0, \sigma_w^2)$ is white Gaussian noise.

The PSD of the AR(p) process, obtained by taking the squared magnitude of the inverse of the model's frequency response, is:

$$\boxed{P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left| 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, e^{-j2\pi fk} \right|^2}}$$

**Why AR models are preferred for short EEG epochs:**

1. **Smoother spectra from short data:** The AR model imposes a rational structure on the spectrum, producing smooth, high-resolution estimates even from 1–2 second EEG segments, where FFT-based periodograms would be noisy and biased.
2. **Sharp peak resolution:** Clinically important narrow features (alpha spindles, beta peaks, spike-wave transients) are preserved as sharp resonances rather than being smeared by windowing.
3. **Fewer data points needed:** An AR(p) model requires only $\approx 2p$ samples for stable estimation, versus the much larger L needed by Welch for the same resolution.
4. **Efficient parameter estimation:** Burg's algorithm and Levinson-Durbin recursion provide $O(p^2)$ computation, suitable for real-time BCI and seizure-detection hardware.
5. **Direct pole interpretation:** AR poles relate to physiological rhythms; pole movement tracks clinical state changes. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compute the AR(2) PSD at $f = 10$ Hz for an EEG segment where the estimated parameters are $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.7$, and $\sigma_w^2 = 0.05$. Sampling rate $F_s = 200$ Hz.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $p = 2$, $a_1 = -1.5$, $a_2 = 0.7$, $\sigma_w^2 = 0.05$, $f = 10$ Hz, $F_s = 200$ Hz.

**Step 1: Compute the normalized digital frequency.**
$$\omega = 2\pi \frac{f}{F_s} = 2\pi \times \frac{10}{200} = \frac{\pi}{10} \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2: Compute the complex exponentials.**
$$e^{-j\omega k} = \cos(\omega k) - j \sin(\omega k)$$

For $k = 1$:
$$e^{-j\omega} = \cos(\pi/10) - j\sin(\pi/10) = 0.9511 - j\,0.3090$$

For $k = 2$:
$$e^{-j2\omega} = \cos(\pi/5) - j\sin(\pi/5) = 0.8090 - j\,0.5878$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator term-by-term.**
$$a_1 \cdot e^{-j\omega} = (-1.5)(0.9511 - j\,0.3090) = -1.4267 + j\,0.4635$$

$$a_2 \cdot e^{-j2\omega} = (0.7)(0.8090 - j\,0.5878) = 0.5663 - j\,0.4115$$

**Step 4: Sum and add 1.**
$$1 + \sum_{k=1}^{2} a_k e^{-j\omega k} = 1 + (-1.4267 + j\,0.4635) + (0.5663 - j\,0.4115)$$
$$= (1 - 1.4267 + 0.5663) + j(0.4635 - 0.4115) = 0.1396 + j\,0.0520$$

**Step 5: Compute magnitude squared.**
$$\left| 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{2} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right|^2 = (0.1396)^2 + (0.0520)^2$$
$$= 0.01949 + 0.00270 = 0.02219$$

**Step 6: Compute the AR(2) PSD.**
$$P_{AR}(10 \text{ Hz}) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left| 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{2} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right|^2} = \frac{0.05}{0.02219}$$
$$\boxed{P_{AR}(10 \text{ Hz}) \approx 2.253 \text{ (arbitrary power units)}}$$

This value represents the EEG power density at 10 Hz (within the Alpha band). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Missing the normalization constant U** in Welch's periodogram — this costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Confusing basic periodogram with Welch's** — students often write the periodogram formula instead of the averaged version.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1/L or 1/N scaling factor** in numerical PSD computation.
> 4. **Using rectangular window without justification** — always specify Hamming/Hann for clinical EEG.
> 5. **In AR problems: forgetting that $w(n)$ must be white noise**, which is a fundamental model assumption.
> 6. **Unit errors**: PSD is in $\mu V^2/Hz$, not just $\mu V^2$ — examiners explicitly check units.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Spectrum Estimation** = computing PSD $P_{xx}(f)$ — the frequency-domain power distribution of EEG.
- ✅ **EEG has 5 rhythms**: Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30 Hz).
- ✅ **Wiener-Khinchin theorem** links autocorrelation ↔ PSD via Fourier transform.
- ✅ **Basic Periodogram** has high variance — never use alone for clinical EEG.
- ✅ **Welch's method** (clinical standard) = windowed segments + FFT + averaging; reduces variance by factor M.
- ✅ **AR modeling** is preferred for **short epochs**; gives smooth, high-resolution spectra.
- ✅ **AR(p) PSD formula**: $P_{AR}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi fk} \vert^2}$.
- ✅ **Burg's method** is the most common AR coefficient estimator for EEG.
- ✅ **Model order selection**: use Akaike Information Criterion (AIC) or Minimum Description Length (MDL); typical EEG AR order is **6 to 12**.
- ✅ **Sampling rate**: must be ≥ 2 × highest frequency of interest (Nyquist); use 200–256 Hz for clinical EEG.
- ✅ **Window choice**: Hamming or Hann for Welch — never rectangular for clinical work.
- ✅ **Overlap**: 50% (or 75%) for Welch's method.
- ✅ **Segment length**: 1–4 seconds depending on the rhythm being analyzed.
- ✅ **Clinical biomarkers**: Alpha peak frequency, alpha power, theta/beta ratio (used in ADHD diagnosis), delta power (sleep depth).
- ✅ **Applications**: seizure detection, BCI, sleep staging, anesthesia depth monitoring, cognitive workload assessment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Wavelet Analysis and Wavelet Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Wavelet Analysis and Wavelet Transforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wavelet Transform (WT):** A mathematical technique that decomposes a given time-domain signal into components localized in both **time** and **frequency** (or more precisely, **time and scale**) by convolving it with a family of zero-mean, finite-duration, oscillatory basis functions called **wavelets**.

The wavelet transform overcomes the fundamental limitation of the classical Fourier Transform, which provides only global frequency information and cannot indicate *when* a particular frequency occurred in a non-stationary signal such as an ECG, EEG, or EMG.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mother Wavelet (ψ(t)):** A prototype function of finite energy and zero mean, from which the entire family of wavelet basis functions is generated by operations of **dilation (scaling)** and **translation (shifting)**.

The general daughter wavelet is given by:

$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$$

where:
- $a$ = scale parameter (controls dilation/compression)
- $b$ = translation parameter (controls shift in time)
- $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ = normalization factor (energy preservation)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Musical Tuning Analogy"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are listening to a 10-minute recording of a symphony orchestra inside a large concert hall.
>
> - The **Fourier Transform** is like asking: *"What notes were played in the entire 10 minutes?"* — you get the overall pitch content, but not *when* the violin solo occurred.
> - The **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** is like asking: *"What notes were played in each 30-second window?"* — better, but the 30-second window is fixed, so fast drum beats get smeared.
> - The **Wavelet Transform** is like asking: *"What notes occurred at every instant, with a microscope that zooms in for high frequencies and zooms out for low frequencies."* — you get precise time information at high frequencies and precise frequency information at low frequencies, exactly suited to natural signals like ECG, EEG, and EMG.

## 1.3 Why Wavelets? — Motivation for Biomedical Signals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, PCG, EOG) are **non-stationary** — their spectral content changes with time. Features like QRS complexes, EEG spindles, EMG bursts, and heart murmurs are **transient** events whose detection requires joint time–frequency analysis.

**Limitations of STFT that wavelets resolve:**

| Aspect | STFT | Wavelet Transform |
|---|---|---|
| Window | Fixed width | Variable width (adaptive) |
| Time–Freq resolution | Constant | Multi-resolution (logarithmic) |
| Basis | Complex sinusoids | Compactly supported wavelets |
| Best for | Slowly varying signals | Transient / non-stationary signals |

## 1.4 Types of Wavelet Transforms

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** — Used for detailed time-scale analysis, redundant representation, feature extraction in biomedical imaging.
> 2. **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** — Used for compression, denoising, and feature extraction; computationally efficient via filter banks (Mallat's algorithm).
> 3. **Wavelet Packet Transform (WPT)** — Generalization of DWT; decomposes both approximation and detail sub-bands.
> 4. **Stationary Wavelet Transform (SWT)** — Translation-invariant variant of DWT used in biomedical denoising.
> 5. **Complex Wavelet Transform (CWT-Dual Tree)** — Used for shift-invariant and directionally selective analysis (e.g., in ECG QRS detection).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation of the Wavelet Transform

The Continuous Wavelet Transform of a continuous-time, square-integrable signal $x(t) \in L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$ with respect to the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ is defined as:

$$CWT_{x}(a,b) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}_{a,b}(t) \, dt = \langle x(t), \psi_{a,b}(t) \rangle$$

where $\langle \cdot , \cdot \rangle$ denotes the inner product and $\psi^{*}_{a,b}$ denotes the complex conjugate of the daughter wavelet.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the CWT

- **Step 1 — Choose a mother wavelet $\psi(t)$:** Must satisfy the **admissibility condition** (zero mean, finite energy, compact support).
- **Step 2 — Apply scaling ($a$):** Large $a \Rightarrow$ stretched (low-frequency) wavelet; small $a \Rightarrow$ compressed (high-frequency) wavelet.
- **Step 3 — Apply translation ($b$):** Slide the wavelet across the signal to localize events in time.
- **Step 4 — Compute inner product:** Measure similarity between signal and shifted/scaled wavelet at each $(a,b)$ pair.
- **Step 5 — Construct the scalogram:** $S(a,b) = \vert CWT_{x}(a,b) \vert^{2}$ — represents energy density in the time-scale plane.

## 2.2 The Admissibility Condition (Why it matters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A function $\psi(t)$ qualifies as a mother wavelet **if and only if** it satisfies the **admissibility condition**:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{+\infty} \frac{\vert \hat{\psi}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert} \, d\omega \;<\; \infty$$

where $\hat{\psi}(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $\psi(t)$ and $C_{\psi}$ is called the **admissibility constant**.

**Physical meaning:**
- It guarantees that $\psi(t)$ has **zero mean** (DC component = 0), i.e., $\hat{\psi}(0) = 0$.
- It guarantees **finite energy**: $\int \vert \psi(t) \vert^{2} dt < \infty$.
- It ensures that the CWT is **invertible** (reconstruction is possible).

The **inverse CWT** is given by:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} CWT_{x}(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da \, db}{a^{2}}$$

## 2.3 Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)

For digital/biomedical signals, the parameters are discretized as:

$$a = a_{0}^{m}, \quad b = n \, b_{0} \, a_{0}^{m}$$

with the standard dyadic choice $a_{0} = 2$ and $b_{0} = 1$:

$$\psi_{m,n}(t) = 2^{-m/2} \, \psi\!\left(2^{-m}t - n\right)$$

The **DWT coefficients** are:

$$DWT_{x}(m,n) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}_{m,n}(t) \, dt$$

## 2.4 Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) — Mallat's Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> MRA represents $x(t)$ at multiple resolutions by decomposing it into an **approximation** $A_{j}$ (low-frequency content) and a **detail** $D_{j}$ (high-frequency content) at each level $j$.

$$x(t) = A_{J}(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J} D_{j}(t)$$

Using orthonormal wavelet and scaling filters $g[n]$ (high-pass) and $h[n]$ (low-pass):

$$A_{j}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k-2n] \, A_{j-1}[k]$$
$$D_{j}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k-2n] \, A_{j-1}[k]$$

The factor of 2 in the index corresponds to **dyadic decimation (downsampling by 2)**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations to Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi\!\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right)$ | Daughter wavelet from mother |
| 2 | $CWT_{x}(a,b) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}_{a,b}(t) \, dt$ | Continuous Wavelet Transform |
| 3 | $x(t) = \dfrac{1}{C_{\psi}} \displaystyle\int\!\!\int CWT_{x}(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \dfrac{da \, db}{a^{2}}$ | Inverse CWT |
| 4 | $C_{\psi} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\vert \hat{\psi}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert} \, d\omega < \infty$ | Admissibility condition |
| 5 | $E = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2} dt = \dfrac{1}{C_{\psi}} \displaystyle\int\!\!\int \vert CWT_{x}(a,b) \vert^{2} \dfrac{da \, db}{a^{2}}$ | Energy conservation (Parseval-like) |
| 6 | $\psi_{m,n}(t) = 2^{-m/2} \, \psi(2^{-m}t - n)$ | Dyadic DWT basis |
| 7 | $x(t) = A_{J}(t) + \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{J} D_{j}(t)$ | MRA decomposition |
| 8 | $S(a,b) = \vert CWT_{x}(a,b) \vert^{2}$ | Scalogram (energy density) |
| 9 | $\Delta t \cdot \Delta f \geq \dfrac{1}{4\pi}$ | Heisenberg uncertainty (time–frequency) |
| 10 | $\text{Window area} = \Delta t \cdot \Delta f = \text{constant}$ | STFT has constant resolution |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **ECG analysis:** QRS detection, R-peak localization, denoising of powerline interference.
- **EEG analysis:** Detection of spikes, spindles, and seizure-related transients.
- **EMG analysis:** Burst detection and muscle fatigue assessment.
- **Medical imaging:** Denoising MRI/CT, multiscale texture analysis of tumors.
- **Phonocardiogram (PCG):** Detection of murmurs and S1/S2 heart sounds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Daughter Wavelet from Mother Wavelet

We start from the mother wavelet $\psi(t)$, which is assumed to be of finite energy, normalized, and admissible.

**Step 1 — Apply dilation (scaling) by factor $a$:**

Replace $t$ with $t/a$ to stretch ($\vert a \vert > 1$) or compress ($0 < \vert a \vert < 1$) the wavelet in time.

$$\psi_{\text{scaled}}(t) = \psi\!\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply translation by $b$:**

Replace $t$ with $t - b$ to shift the wavelet along the time axis.

$$\psi_{\text{shifted}}(t) = \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply energy normalization:**

To ensure $\int \vert \psi_{a,b}(t) \vert^{2} dt = \int \vert \psi(t) \vert^{2} dt$, divide by $\sqrt{\vert a \vert}$.

$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$$

This is the **daughter wavelet** used in both CWT and DWT.

## 3.2 Derivation: Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) as Inner Product

We wish to measure the similarity between $x(t)$ and $\psi_{a,b}(t)$ at every scale $a$ and shift $b$.

**Step 1 — Define the inner product in $L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$:**

$$\langle x(t), \psi_{a,b}(t) \rangle = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}_{a,b}(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the daughter wavelet expression:**

$$CWT_{x}(a,b) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

**Step 3 — Re-arrange constants:**

$$CWT_{x}(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

This is the final CWT formula. Each coefficient $CWT_{x}(a,b)$ tells **how much** of wavelet $\psi_{a,b}(t)$ is present in $x(t)$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Admissibility Condition

Starting from the requirement that the inverse CWT must recover $x(t)$ exactly.

**Step 1 — Substitute inverse CWT back into forward CWT:**

$$CWT_{x}(a,b) = \int x(t) \, \psi^{*}_{a,b}(t) \, dt$$

Plugging $x(t)$ from inverse:

$$CWT_{x}(a,b) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \iint CWT_{x}(a',b') \left[ \int \psi_{a',b'}(t) \, \psi^{*}_{a,b}(t) \, dt \right] \frac{da' \, db'}{a'^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Reproducing kernel must equal delta function:**

For perfect reconstruction:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi_{a',b'}(t) \, \psi^{*}_{a,b}(t) \, dt = C_{\psi} \, a' \, \delta(a' - a) \, \delta(b' - b)$$

**Step 3 — Use Plancherel theorem (Fourier-domain equality):**

$$\int \psi_{a',b'}(t) \, \psi^{*}_{a,b}(t) \, dt = \int \hat{\psi}_{a',b'}(\omega) \, \hat{\psi}^{*}_{a,b}(\omega) \, d\omega$$

Substitute $\hat{\psi}_{a,b}(\omega) = \sqrt{\vert a \vert} \, e^{-j\omega b} \, \hat{\psi}(a\omega)$:

$$\int \sqrt{\vert a' a \vert} \, e^{-j\omega(b'-b)} \, \hat{\psi}(a'\omega) \, \hat{\psi}^{*}(a\omega) \, d\omega$$

**Step 4 — Change of variable and integration:**

After algebraic simplification using the delta property:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \hat{\psi}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert} \, d\omega < \infty$$

This is the **admissibility condition**.

## 3.4 Derivation: MRA via Filter Banks (Mallat's Algorithm)

**Step 1 — Decompose signal into approximation and detail:**

$$x(t) = A_{1}(t) + D_{1}(t)$$

where $A_{1}(t)$ is the low-frequency approximation and $D_{1}(t)$ is the high-frequency detail.

**Step 2 — Apply scaling function $\phi(t)$ and wavelet $\psi(t)$:**

$$A_{1}(t) = \sum_{n} c_{1}[n] \, \phi_{1,n}(t), \quad D_{1}(t) = \sum_{n} d_{1}[n] \, \psi_{1,n}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Relate coefficients across scales via filters:**

$$c_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n] \, c_{j}[k]$$

$$d_{j+1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n] \, c_{j}[k]$$

The factor 2 reflects dyadic downsampling (halving of sample rate).

**Step 4 — Reconstruction (inverse DWT):**

$$c_{j}[n] = \sum_{k} h[n - 2k] \, c_{j+1}[k] + \sum_{k} g[n - 2k] \, d_{j+1}[k]$$

followed by upsampling by 2 (zero insertion between samples).

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Illustrative)

> [!TIP]
> **Example:** Compute CWT of a chirp signal $x(t) = \sin(2\pi(5 + 10t)t)$ using Morlet wavelet for $a = 1, b = 0$.

**Given:** $a = 1$, $b = 0$. The Morlet wavelet is $\psi(t) = e^{-t^{2}/2} \cos(5t)$.

**Step 1 — Daughter wavelet becomes:**

$$\psi_{1,0}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1}} \psi(t - 0) = e^{-t^{2}/2} \cos(5t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute CWT coefficient:**

$$CWT(1,0) = \int_{-T}^{T} \sin(2\pi(5+10t)t) \cdot e^{-t^{2}/2} \cos(5t) \, dt$$

The Gaussian window $e^{-t^{2}/2}$ selects a local slice of the chirp near $t = 0$, where instantaneous frequency is $5 + 10(0) = 5$ Hz. The wavelet matches this frequency → large coefficient.

**Step 3 — Repeat for $b = 1$ second:**

Instantaneous frequency at $t = 1$ is $5 + 10(1) = 15$ Hz → different scale $a$ needed for best match.

This illustrates the **time-localized, frequency-adaptive** nature of the CWT.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Comparison: Fourier vs STFT vs Wavelet (Time–Frequency Tiling)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["FOURIER TRANSFORM\nEntire signal\nGlobal frequency only"]
    A2["SHORT TIME FOURIER\nFixed window size\nUniform time frequency grid"]
    A3["WAVELET TRANSFORM\nAdaptive window\nMulti resolution tiling"]
    A4["BIOMEDICAL USE CASE\nStationary only\nSlowly varying signals\nNon stationary transient signals"]

    A1 --> B1["Time axis: NO info\nFreq axis: PRECISE"]
    A2 --> B2["Time axis: FIXED block\nFreq axis: FIXED block"]
    A3 --> B3["Time axis: PRECISE at HF\nFreq axis: PRECISE at LF"]

    B1 --> A4
    B2 --> A4
    B3 --> A4

    A1 --- C1["Limitation: Cannot locate transients"]
    A2 --- C2["Limitation: Fixed resolution trade off"]
    A3 --- C3["Advantage: Best of both worlds"]
```

## 4.2 DWT Multi-Resolution Decomposition Tree (Mallat's Algorithm)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inNode["INPUT SIGNAL x_n\nSampling rate Fs"]
    lp1["LOW PASS FILTER h_n\nCutoff Fs divided by 4"]
    hp1["HIGH PASS FILTER g_n\nCutoff Fs divided by 4"]
    ds1a["Downsample by 2"]
    ds1b["Downsample by 2"]
    A1["Approximation A1\nLevel 1 low freq content"]
    D1["Detail D1\nLevel 1 high freq content"]
    lp2["LOW PASS FILTER h_n"]
    hp2["HIGH PASS FILTER g_n"]
    ds2a["Downsample by 2"]
    ds2b["Downsample by 2"]
    A2["Approximation A2\nLevel 2 low freq content"]
    D2["Detail D2\nLevel 2 mid freq content"]

    inNode --> lp1
    inNode --> hp1
    lp1 --> ds1a
    hp1 --> ds1b
    ds1a --> A1
    ds1b --> D1
    A1 --> lp2
    A1 --> hp2
    lp2 --> ds2a
    hp2 --> ds2b
    ds2a --> A2
    ds2b --> D2
```

## 4.3 CWT Scalogram — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Input biomedical signal x of t\nECG EEG EMG PCG"]
    S2["Choose mother wavelet\nMorlet Daubechies Haar Symlet Coiflet"]
    S3["Loop over scales a from a min to a max\nlogarithmic progression"]
    S4["Loop over translations b from 0 to signal length"]
    S5["Compute inner product\nCWT a b equals integral x of t times psi star a b of t dt"]
    S6["Store coefficient matrix CWT of a b"]
    S7["Compute scalogram S equals magnitude squared of CWT"]
    S8["Visualize as 2D time scale plot\nColor encodes magnitude"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```

    Q1 --> W1["Daubechies db4 db6\nSymlet sym4\nBiorthogonal bior 4 4"]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the Continuous Wavelet Transform. Explain the role of scale and translation parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Continuous Wavelet Transform of a signal $x(t)$ with respect to a mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ is defined as:

$$CWT_{x}(a,b) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}_{a,b}(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

- **Scale parameter $a$:** Controls the dilation of the wavelet. Large $a$ → stretched wavelet (analyzes low frequencies); small $a$ → compressed wavelet (analyzes high frequencies).
- **Translation parameter $b$:** Controls the time-shift of the wavelet, enabling localization of events in time.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — scale role, 1 mark — translation role)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**State and explain the admissibility condition for a mother wavelet.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A function $\psi(t)$ qualifies as a mother wavelet if and only if it satisfies the admissibility condition:

$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \hat{\psi}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert} \, d\omega < \infty$$

- It implies $\psi(t)$ has **zero mean**, i.e., $\hat{\psi}(0) = 0$, ensuring that the wavelet captures only oscillatory (non-DC) behavior.
- It ensures $\psi(t)$ has **finite energy**, i.e., $\int \vert \psi(t) \vert^{2} dt < \infty$.
- It guarantees that the CWT is **invertible** and that the original signal can be reconstructed via the inverse formula.

*(1 mark — formula, 1 mark — zero mean, 1 mark — invertibility)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT). Derive the inverse CWT formula.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare CWT and DWT. Explain Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) using filter banks with suitable diagrams.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The CWT computation block diagram consists of the following stages:
1. Input biomedical signal $x(t)$ (e.g., ECG).
2. Selection of mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ (Morlet, Daubechies, etc.).
3. Scale generator — produces logarithmically spaced scales $a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{N}$.
4. For each scale $a$, generate the daughter wavelet $\psi_{a,b}(t)$ for all $b$.
5. Inner product computation — produces CWT coefficient $CWT_{x}(a,b)$.
6. Scalogram construction — $S(a,b) = \vert CWT_{x}(a,b) \vert^{2}$.

**Derivation of Inverse CWT (5 marks):**

We start with the requirement that the inverse transform must reconstruct $x(t)$ from $CWT_{x}(a,b)$:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \iint CWT_{x}(a,b) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da \, db}{a^{2}}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the forward CWT expression for $CWT_{x}(a',b')$:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \iint \left[ \int x(\tau) \, \psi^{*}_{a',b'}(\tau) \, d\tau \right] \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da' \, db'}{a'^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange the integrals:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int x(\tau) \left[ \iint \psi^{*}_{a',b'}(\tau) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da' \, db'}{a'^{2}} \right] d\tau$$

**Step 3:** Use the reproducing kernel identity:

$$\iint \psi^{*}_{a',b'}(\tau) \, \psi_{a,b}(t) \, \frac{da' \, db'}{a'^{2}} = C_{\psi} \, \delta(\tau - t)$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int x(\tau) \cdot C_{\psi} \, \delta(\tau - t) \, d\tau = x(t) \checkmark$$

The admissibility condition guarantees this identity holds, completing the proof.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | CWT | DWT |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters | Continuous $(a, b)$ | Discrete dyadic $(2^{m}, n \cdot 2^{m})$ |
| Redundancy | Highly redundant | Non-redundant (orthonormal) |
| Computation | Slow (O(N²)) | Fast via filter banks (O(N log N)) |
| Output | Time-scale scalogram | Approximation + detail coefficients |
| Application | Feature extraction, analysis | Compression, denoising, coding |
| Invertibility | Always | Always (with orthogonal wavelets) |

**MRA via Filter Banks (4 marks):**

The signal $x[n]$ is passed through:
- Low-pass filter $h[n]$ (scaling filter) → produces approximation $A_{1}[n]$ after downsampling by 2.
- High-pass filter $g[n]$ (wavelet filter) → produces detail $D_{1}[n]$ after downsampling by 2.

$$A_{1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n] \, x[k], \quad D_{1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n] \, x[k]$$

At the next level, $A_{1}$ is again decomposed into $A_{2}$ and $D_{2}$. After $J$ levels:

$$x[n] = A_{J}[n] + \sum_{j=1}^{J} D_{j}[n]$$

Reconstruction uses upsampling by 2 followed by synthesis filters $\tilde{h}[n]$ and $\tilde{g}[n]$.

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the limitations of Fourier Transform in analyzing biomedical signals. How does the Wavelet Transform overcome these limitations? Explain with the concept of multi-resolution analysis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Mallat's algorithm for DWT decomposition and reconstruction. Discuss any four applications of wavelets in biomedical signal processing.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Limitations of Fourier Transform (3 marks):**

1. Provides only **global spectral information** — does not indicate *when* a particular frequency occurred.
2. Assumes the signal is **stationary** — biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) are inherently non-stationary.
3. Cannot resolve **transient features** like QRS complex, EEG spike, or EMG burst.
4. Uses **infinite-duration sinusoids** as basis — not suited for localized features.
5. Suffers from the **Heisenberg uncertainty** limitation equally for all frequencies.

**How Wavelets Overcome These (2 marks):**

- Wavelets use **finite-duration, compactly supported** basis functions.
- Provide **joint time–frequency representation** via the scalogram.
- Employ **adaptive windowing** — narrow windows at high frequencies (good time resolution) and wide windows at low frequencies (good frequency resolution).
- Preserve **time localization** of transient features.

**Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) (2 marks):**

MRA decomposes a signal into multiple resolution levels. At each level $j$, the signal is split into:
- **Approximation $A_{j}$** — low-frequency, coarse representation.
- **Detail $D_{j}$** — high-frequency, fine representation.

$$x(t) = A_{J}(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J} D_{j}(t)$$

This enables detection of both slow trends (low frequencies) and sharp transients (high frequencies) simultaneously — ideal for ECG, EEG, and EMG.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Mallat's Algorithm for DWT (4 marks):**

Mallat (1989) proposed an efficient pyramidal algorithm using conjugate mirror filters:

**Decomposition (Analysis):**

$$cA_{j}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n] \, cA_{j-1}[k] \quad \text{(approximation)}$$

$$cD_{j}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n] \, cA_{j-1}[k] \quad \text{(detail)}$$

At each level, the input is filtered by low-pass $h$ and high-pass $g$, then downsampled by 2. Only the approximation is decomposed further (DWT). For Wavelet Packet Transform, both branches are decomposed.

**Reconstruction (Synthesis):**

$$cA_{j-1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[n - 2k] \, cA_{j}[k] + \sum_{k} g[n - 2k] \, cD_{j}[k]$$

followed by upsampling by 2.

**Applications of Wavelets in Biomedical Signal Processing (3 marks — any 4):**

1. **ECG R-peak detection** — DWT detail coefficients at appropriate scales highlight sharp R-peaks for accurate heart rate monitoring.
2. **EEG spike and seizure detection** — CWT scalogram reveals transient spikes typical of epileptiform activity.
3. **EMG signal denoising** — Thresholding detail coefficients removes high-frequency noise while preserving muscle burst morphology.
4. **Heart sound (PCG) analysis** — Wavelet decomposition separates S1, S2, murmurs, and noise components.
5. **Compression of medical images** — Wavelet-based JPEG2000 enables high-ratio compression of MRI/CT with diagnostic quality.
6. **Evoked potential detection** — Wavelet averaging extracts weak ERPs from background EEG noise.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students frequently **forget the normalization factor $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$** in the daughter wavelet formula — this costs 1 mark.
> - Confusing **scale $a$ with frequency $f$** — remember that scale is **inversely proportional** to frequency: large $a$ = low frequency, small $a$ = high frequency.
> - Writing the admissibility condition with the **wrong denominator** — it must be $\vert \omega \vert$ in the denominator, NOT $\omega^{2}$.
> - In Mallat's algorithm, forgetting the **downsampling by 2** factor — students often write $cA_{j}[n] = \sum h[k] \, cA_{j-1}[k]$ without the index shift $[k - 2n]$.
> - Mixing up **approximation coefficients $cA$** and **detail coefficients $cD$** in reconstruction formulas — the synthesis uses both $h$ and $g$ filters with the index pattern $[n - 2k]$, not $[k - 2n]$.
> - Not mentioning the **mother wavelet** selection criteria for biomedical applications — examiner expects at least one specific wavelet (e.g., Daubechies db4 for ECG) for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU exams):**

- **Wavelet Transform** = time-scale representation of a signal using finite-duration, zero-mean basis functions.
- **Mother Wavelet ψ(t)** — must satisfy admissibility condition; finite energy, zero mean.
- **Daughter Wavelet** formula: $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$.
- **CWT** = inner product of $x(t)$ with $\psi_{a,b}(t)$; continuous $(a,b)$ → highly redundant scalogram.
- **DWT** = dyadic discretization ($a = 2^{m}$, $b = n 2^{m}$); fast, non-redundant, used in compression/denoising.
- **Admissibility Condition:** $C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \hat{\psi}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\vert \omega \vert} d\omega < \infty$.
- **Inverse CWT:** $x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \iint CWT_{x}(a,b) \psi_{a,b}(t) \frac{da\,db}{a^{2}}$.
- **MRA Decomposition:** $x(t) = A_{J}(t) + \sum_{j=1}^{J} D_{j}(t)$.
- **Mallat's Algorithm:** Low-pass $h[n]$ + High-pass $g[n]$ + downsample by 2 at each level; only $A_j$ decomposed further in DWT.
- **Scalogram:** $S(a,b) = \vert CWT_{x}(a,b) \vert^{2}$ — energy density in time-scale plane.
- **Heisenberg Uncertainty:** Wavelet achieves optimal trade-off — narrow in time at high frequencies, narrow in frequency at low frequencies.
- **Common Wavelets for Biomedical Signals:** Daubechies (db4, db6) for ECG/EEG; Morlet for CWT analysis; Symlet for symmetric features; Haar for fast computation; Coiflet for smooth approximations.
- **Advantage over STFT:** Adaptive window size — multi-resolution vs fixed resolution.
- **Advantage over Fourier:** Preserves time information of transient features.
- **KTU-Favorite Comparison Topics:** Fourier vs STFT vs Wavelet; CWT vs DWT; MRA pyramid; Mallat filter bank structure.
- **Biomedical Applications:** ECG R-peak detection, EEG seizure detection, EMG denoising, PCG heart sound analysis, MRI/CT image compression.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) and Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT): Orthogonal Wavelet Decomposition, Orthonormal Wavelets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) and Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT): Orthogonal Wavelet Decomposition, Orthonormal Wavelets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) — Formal Definition

The **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** is a time–frequency analysis tool that decomposes a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a set of time-shifted and scaled (dilated) versions of a prototype function called the **mother wavelet** $\psi(t)$. The CWT provides a *two-dimensional* representation of a *one-dimensional* signal, mapping it onto the **scale–translation (a, b) plane**.

Mathematically, the CWT is defined as:

$$CWT_x(a,b) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

where:
- $a \in \mathbb{R}, a \neq 0$ is the **scale parameter** (inverse of frequency).
- $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **translation (time-shift) parameter**.
- $\psi^{*}(t)$ is the complex conjugate of the mother wavelet.
- The factor $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ ensures **energy normalization** at every scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissibility Condition** (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight): For $\psi(t)$ to qualify as a mother wavelet, it must satisfy:
> $$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \hat{\psi}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega}\, d\omega \;<\; \infty$$
> This guarantees that $C_{\psi} > 0$ and permits perfect **inverse reconstruction** of the original signal.

### 1.2 Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) — Formal Definition

The **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)** operates on discrete-time signals by sampling the CWT parameters on a **dyadic grid**: $a = 2^{-j}$ and $b = k \cdot 2^{-j}$, producing a non-redundant, orthogonal, and computationally efficient representation.

The DWT coefficients are defined as:

$$DWT(j,k) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi_{j,k}(t)\, dt$$

where the **wavelet basis functions** are:

$$\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\, \psi\!\left(2^{j} t - k\right)$$

Here, $j \in \mathbb{Z}$ controls **dilation** (frequency resolution) and $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ controls **translation** (time localization).

### 1.3 Orthogonal vs. Orthonormal Wavelets

> [!NOTE]
> **Orthogonal Wavelets**: Basis functions $\psi_{j,k}(t)$ satisfy
> $$\langle \psi_{j,k}, \psi_{m,n} \rangle = 0 \quad \text{whenever} \quad (j,k) \neq (m,n)$$
> **Orthonormal Wavelets**: A special, **energy-preserving** subset of orthogonal wavelets that also satisfy the **normalization** condition
> $$\Vert \psi_{j,k} \Vert^{2} = \langle \psi_{j,k}, \psi_{j,k} \rangle = 1$$
> *“Orthonormal is orthogonal with the bonus of unit energy at every scale.”*

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mathematical Microscope"

Imagine the wavelet transform as a **zoom lens on a microscope**:
- **CWT** is like a *high-resolution digital microscope* — you can continuously slide the lens to *any* magnification (scale) and *any* position (translation). It produces a rich, redundant map, ideal for exploratory biomedical signal analysis (e.g., ECG R-peak detection, EEG spike detection).
- **DWT** is like a *calibrated microscope with discrete magnification levels* — you only snap to powers of two ($1\times, 2\times, 4\times, 8\times$). It produces a compact, *orthogonal* image, perfect for **compression, denoising, and feature extraction** in real-time medical devices.

### 1.5 Key Standards & Constants

- **Nyquist Criterion Equivalent**: The highest resolvable scale corresponds to a sampling rate of **≥ 2× the signal's highest frequency** (i.e., $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$).
- **Standard Mother Wavelets**: Daubechies (dbN), Symlet (symN), Coiflet (coifN), Morlet, Mexican Hat, Haar.
- **Energy Conservation Constant**: $C_{\psi}$, the admissibility constant, is **finite and strictly positive**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the CWT

The CWT executes the following **algorithmic logic steps**:

1. **Select a mother wavelet** $\psi(t)$ — must be oscillatory, finite-energy, and admissible.
2. **Choose a scale $a$** — large $a$ → low-frequency probe; small $a$ → high-frequency probe.
3. **Dilate $\psi$ by $a$** to obtain $\psi_a(t) = \psi(t/a)$.
4. **Translate by $b$** to obtain $\psi_{a,b}(t) = \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$.
5. **Compute inner product** of the signal $x(t)$ with the scaled–translated wavelet.
6. **Slide across all $(a, b)$** to build a 2D scalogram $CWT_x(a, b)$.
7. **Invert via the resolution of identity** to recover $x(t)$:
$$x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} CWT_x(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the $\frac{1}{a^{2}}$ measure?** It compensates for the natural stretching of $\psi$ at large scales, ensuring the **inversion integral is invariant to scale choice**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of the DWT (Dyadic Grid)

The DWT uses **dyadic (powers of two) sampling** of $(a, b)$ to ensure an *orthogonal* tiling of the time–frequency plane.

1. **Decompose** the signal into a **low-frequency approximation** (A) and **high-frequency detail** (D) using a pair of quadrature mirror filters (low-pass $h[n]$, high-pass $g[n]$).
2. **Downsample by 2** (the famous $\downarrow 2$ operator) to remove redundancy.
3. **Iterate** on the approximation branch only — yielding the **pyramid algorithm** of Mallat.
4. **Store** only the detail coefficients $\{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_J\}$ and the final approximation $a_J$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int x(t) \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ | Continuous Wavelet Transform definition |
| 2 | $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2} \psi(2^{j} t - k)$ | Dyadic wavelet basis (DWT) |
| 3 | $C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \hat{\psi}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$ | Admissibility condition for $\psi$ |
| 4 | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$ | **Scaling (Two-Scale) Equation** |
| 5 | $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} g[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$ | **Wavelet (Two-Scale) Equation** |
| 6 | $g[n] = (-1)^{n} h[N-1-n]$ | Quadrature Mirror Filter relation (QMF) |
| 7 | $a_{j-1}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k-2n]\, a_{j}[k]$ | Approximation coefficients (Mallat) |
| 8 | $d_{j-1}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k-2n]\, a_{j}[k]$ | Detail coefficients (Mallat) |
| 9 | $\Vert x \Vert^{2} = \sum_{k} \vert a_{J}[k] \vert^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{J} \sum_{k} \vert d_{j}[k] \vert^{2}$ | **Parseval / Energy Conservation** |
| 10 | $\sum_{n} h[n] = \sqrt{2}, \quad \sum_{n} g[n] = 0$ | Low-pass DC, High-pass zero-mean |
| 11 | $\sum_{n} h[n] h[n-2k] = \delta[k]$ | Orthonormality of $h$ |
| 12 | $\sum_{n} g[n] g[n-2k] = \delta[k]$ | Orthonormality of $g$ |
| 13 | $\sum_{n} h[n] g[n-2k] = 0$ | Cross-orthogonality (h ⊥ g) |
| 14 | $\hat{\psi}(\omega) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} G\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \hat{\phi}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | Wavelet–Filter Fourier relation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024)**: Equations (4)–(6) and (7)–(8) carry the **highest weightage** in 14-mark derivations. Memorize the QMF relation $g[n] = (-1)^{n} h[N-1-n]$ as it is asked almost every semester.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG Denoising**: DWT decomposes ECG into approximation (QRS complex) and details (high-freq. noise), enabling threshold-based denoising.
- **EEG Feature Extraction**: CWT scalogram reveals transient epileptic spikes with high time-frequency precision.
- **Medical Image Compression** (DICOM, JPEG2000): Uses biorthogonal/orthonormal wavelets for lossy/lossless compression of CT/MRI scans.
- **EMG Signal Classification**: DWT sub-band energies feed neural-network classifiers for prosthetic control.
- **Phonocardiogram (PCG) Analysis**: CWT detects S1/S2 heart-sound boundaries for murmur identification.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Continuous Wavelet Transform

**Starting Point**: We require a transform that *localizes a signal in both time and frequency* — the shortcoming of the Fourier Transform is that it gives only global frequency information.

**Step 1**: Start with the **windowed Fourier transform (STFT)** and recognize its fixed-window limitation.

**Step 2**: Introduce a function $\psi(t)$ that has both *finite duration* and *zero mean* (band-pass behavior). Normalize its energy to unity:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \psi(t) \vert^{2} dt = 1$$

**Step 3**: Define a **scaled** (dilated) wavelet:
$$\psi_{a}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)$$
*Logic:* The $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ factor keeps the energy *scale-invariant*:
$$\int \vert \psi_{a}(t) \vert^{2} dt = \int \vert \psi(u) \vert^{2} du = 1$$

**Step 4**: Introduce **time-shift $b$**:
$$\psi_{a,b}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}\, \psi\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right)$$

**Step 5**: Compute the **inner product** $\langle x, \psi_{a,b} \rangle$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CWT_x(a,b) &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi_{a,b}^{*}(t)\, dt \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **definition of the CWT**.

**Step 6**: Derive the **inverse CWT** using the resolution of identity. For an *admissible* wavelet:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} CWT_x(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \psi_{a,b}^{*}(\tau)\, d\tau \cdot \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}} \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \left[\int\!\!\int \psi_{a,b}^{*}(\tau)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}\right] d\tau
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7**: Apply the **reproducing kernel identity** which reduces, by the admissibility condition, to $C_{\psi}\, \delta(t-\tau)$. Therefore:

$$
x(t) = \frac{1}{C_{\psi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} CWT_x(a,b)\, \psi_{a,b}(t)\, \frac{da\, db}{a^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: The inner product structure makes the CWT a *correlator* — it correlates the signal with a bank of band-pass filters of continuously varying center frequencies.

### 3.2 Derivation of the DWT from the CWT (Dyadic Discretization)

**Step 1**: Restrict scale $a$ to a **dyadic set** $a = 2^{-j}, j \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Step 2**: Restrict translation to $b = k \cdot 2^{-j} = k \cdot a$.

**Step 3**: Substitute into the CWT definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT(j,k) &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^{-j}}} \int x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t - k \cdot 2^{-j}}{2^{-j}}\right) dt \\
&= 2^{j/2} \int x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(2^{j} t - k\right) dt \\
&= \int x(t)\, \psi_{j,k}^{*}(t)\, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\psi_{j,k}(t) = 2^{j/2}\, \psi(2^{j} t - k)$. The DWT is thus a *sampled, non-redundant* CWT.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Two-Scale (Dilation) Equation

**Step 1**: For an orthonormal wavelet basis, there exists a companion **scaling function** $\phi(t)$ that spans the **approximation subspace** $V_j$.

**Step 2**: Because the subspace $V_{j-1} \subset V_j$, the scaling function at coarser resolution is expressible in terms of finer-scale scaling functions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi_{j-1, k}(t) &= 2^{(j-1)/2}\, \phi\!\left(2^{j-1} t - k\right) \\
&= \sum_{n} h[n]\, 2^{j/2}\, \phi(2^{j} t - 2k - n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Equating the two representations yields the **two-scale (refinement) equation**:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$$

**Step 4**: Since the wavelet $\psi(t)$ spans the *orthogonal complement* $W_j$ of $V_j$ in $V_{j+1}$ (i.e., $V_{j+1} = V_j \oplus W_j$), a parallel relation gives:

$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} g[n]\, \phi(2t - n)$$

**Step 5**: Because $W_j \perp V_j$, the filters $h$ and $g$ must be **orthogonal**, leading to the **Quadrature Mirror Filter** condition:

$$g[n] = (-1)^{n}\, h[N-1-n]$$

**Step 6**: For orthonormal basis, the filters must also satisfy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n} h[n] h[n-2k] &= \delta[k] \quad \text{(h-self orthonormality)} \\
\sum_{n} g[n] g[n-2k] &= \delta[k] \quad \text{(g-self orthonormality)} \\
\sum_{n} h[n] g[n-2k] &= 0 \quad \text{(h-g cross-orthogonality)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Mallat's Pyramid Algorithm

**Step 1**: Approximate $x(t)$ at resolution $2^{j}$ by the projection onto $V_j$:

$$P_{j} x(t) = \sum_{k} a_{j}[k]\, \phi_{j,k}(t)$$

**Step 2**: Use the two-scale equation to expand $\phi_{j,k}$ in terms of $\phi_{j+1, n}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{j}[k] &= \langle P_{j} x, \phi_{j,k} \rangle = \sum_{n} \langle \phi_{j+1, n}, \phi_{j,k} \rangle\, a_{j+1}[n] \\
&= \sum_{n} h[n - 2k]\, a_{j+1}[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

Renaming $n \to k$ and $k \to n$:

$$a_{j}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n]\, a_{j+1}[k]$$

This is convolution with $h$ **followed by downsampling by 2**.

**Step 3**: Similarly, the **detail coefficients** in $W_j$ are:

$$d_{j}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n]\, a_{j+1}[k]$$

**Step 4**: For reconstruction, the inverse relations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_{j+1}[k] &= \sum_{n} h[k - 2n]\, a_{j}[n] + \sum_{n} g[k - 2n]\, d_{j}[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

which is **upsampling by 2 followed by filtering**.

**Step 5**: The **Parseval / Energy Conservation** theorem for orthonormal wavelets states:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Vert x \Vert^{2} &= \sum_{k} \vert a_{J}[k] \vert^{2} + \sum_{j=1}^{J} \sum_{k} \vert d_{j}[k] \vert^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the cornerstone of **DWT-based signal compression and denoising** in biomedical engineering.

### 3.5 Worked Example: Haar Wavelet (db1) Filter Coefficients

The simplest orthonormal wavelet is the **Haar wavelet**. Its filters are:

$$h[0] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad h[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$
$$g[0] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad g[1] = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Verify orthonormality of $h$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n} h[n] h[n-2k] &= h[0]h[-2k] + h[1]h[1-2k] \\
\text{For } k=0: \; & h[0]^{2} + h[1]^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} = 1 = \delta[0] \\
\text{For } k \neq 0: \; & = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verify QMF relation**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g[0] &= (-1)^{0} h[1-0] = +h[1] = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \checkmark \\
g[1] &= (-1)^{1} h[1-1] = -h[0] = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic**: The Haar wavelet is the *default test case* in KTU exam questions. Always verify the four filter relations for it: (a) $\sum h = \sqrt{2}$, (b) $\sum g = 0$, (c) h-self orthonormality, (d) h-g cross-orthogonality.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the Continuous Wavelet Transform. State the admissibility condition for a function to be a mother wavelet.**

**Model Answer:**
The Continuous Wavelet Transform of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as
$$CWT_x(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$
where $a$ is scale, $b$ is translation, and $\psi(t)$ is the mother wavelet.

The **admissibility condition** is:
$$C_{\psi} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\vert \hat{\psi}(\omega) \vert^{2}}{\omega} d\omega < \infty$$
This requires $\hat{\psi}(0) = 0$, i.e., the mother wavelet must be a **band-pass function** (zero mean), ensuring perfect signal reconstruction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Distinguish between orthogonal and orthonormal wavelets with mathematical justification.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Orthogonal wavelets** satisfy $\langle \psi_{j,k}, \psi_{m,n} \rangle = 0$ for $(j,k) \neq (m,n)$.
- **Orthonormal wavelets** are a special case of orthogonal wavelets that additionally satisfy $\Vert \psi_{j,k} \Vert^{2} = 1$ for all $(j, k)$.

Mathematically:
$$\langle \psi_{j,k}, \psi_{m,n} \rangle = \delta_{j-m}\, \delta_{k-n}$$
The orthonormality guarantees the **Parseval energy-conservation identity**:
$$\Vert x \Vert^{2} = \sum_{j,k} \vert \langle x, \psi_{j,k} \rangle \vert^{2}$$
which is foundational for DWT-based compression in biomedical signals.

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Format Note**: Each Part B question has internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below; the student answers ONE.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Orthogonal Wavelet Decomposition and Filter Relations*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) Derive the two-scale (dilation) equations for the scaling function and wavelet function in an orthonormal wavelet basis. State the four orthonormality conditions that the analysis filters $h[n]$ and $g[n]$ must satisfy.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) For the Haar wavelet, determine the analysis filter coefficients $h[n]$ and $g[n]$. Verify the Quadrature Mirror Filter relation and the orthonormality conditions.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Refer to Section 3.3 derivation. The two-scale equations are:

$$\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} h[n] \phi(2t - n)$$
$$\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \sum_{n} g[n] \phi(2t - n)$$

The four orthonormality conditions are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)} \quad & \sum_{n} h[n] = \sqrt{2} \quad \text{(DC preservation)} \\
\text{(ii)} \quad & \sum_{n} g[n] = 0 \quad \text{(High-pass zero mean)} \\
\text{(iii)} \quad & \sum_{n} h[n] h[n-2k] = \delta[k] \quad \text{(h orthonormality)} \\
\text{(iv)} \quad & \sum_{n} h[n] g[n-2k] = 0 \quad \text{(h-g cross-orthogonality)}
\end{aligned}
$$

These follow from the requirement that $V_{j+1} = V_j \oplus W_j$ with $V_j \perp W_j$.

**(b)** For the **Haar wavelet**, the scaling and wavelet functions are:

$$\phi(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}, \quad \psi(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & 0 \leq t < 1/2 \\ -1, & 1/2 \leq t < 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

From the two-scale equation:
$$h[0] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad h[1] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$
$$g[0] = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad g[1] = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Verify QMF**: $g[n] = (-1)^{n} h[1-n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g[0] &= (-1)^{0} h[1] = +\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \checkmark \\
g[1] &= (-1)^{1} h[0] = -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verify orthonormality**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{n} h[n] h[n] &= \left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} + \left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} = 1 \quad \checkmark \\
\sum_{n} h[n] h[n-2] &= 0 \quad \text{(no overlap at shift 2)} \quad \checkmark \\
\sum_{n} h[n] g[n] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \left(-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All conditions satisfied — the Haar wavelet is orthonormal.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Mallat's Algorithm and Multi-Resolution Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) Explain Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA). Derive Mallat's pyramid algorithm for the computation of DWT coefficients using filter bank structures.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A discrete signal $x[n] = [4, 6, 10, 12, 8, 6, 4, 2]$ is decomposed using the Haar wavelet (db1). Compute the first-level approximation and detail coefficients.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** MRA represents $x(t)$ at multiple resolutions using nested subspaces $V_j$:

$$V_{-\infty} \subset \cdots \subset V_{-1} \subset V_{0} \subset V_{1} \subset \cdots \subset V_{\infty} = L^{2}(\mathbb{R})$$

with the orthogonal complement $W_j$ such that $V_{j+1} = V_j \oplus W_j$.

Mallat's algorithm (refer to Section 3.4):
$$a_{j}[n] = \sum_{k} h[k - 2n]\, a_{j+1}[k] \quad \text{(Low-pass + Downsample)}$$
$$d_{j}[n] = \sum_{k} g[k - 2n]\, a_{j+1}[k] \quad \text{(High-pass + Downsample)}$$

**Block structure**: $x \to [h, \downarrow 2] \to a_1$ and $x \to [g, \downarrow 2] \to d_1$. The process is iterated on $a_1$ to yield $a_2, d_2$, etc.

**(b)** Haar filters: $h = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$, $g = \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$.

**Level-1 Approximation** $a_1[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1[0] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4 + 6) = \tfrac{10}{\sqrt{2}} = 5\sqrt{2} \\
a_1[1] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(10 + 12) = \tfrac{22}{\sqrt{2}} = 11\sqrt{2} \\
a_1[2] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(8 + 6) = \tfrac{14}{\sqrt{2}} = 7\sqrt{2} \\
a_1[3] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4 + 2) = \tfrac{6}{\sqrt{2}} = 3\sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Level-1 Detail** $d_1[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1[0] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4 - 6) = -\sqrt{2} \\
d_1[1] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(10 - 12) = -\sqrt{2} \\
d_1[2] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(8 - 6) = \sqrt{2} \\
d_1[3] &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(4 - 2) = \sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$a_1 = [5\sqrt{2},\; 11\sqrt{2},\; 7\sqrt{2},\; 3\sqrt{2}], \quad d_1 = [-\sqrt{2},\; -\sqrt{2},\; \sqrt{2},\; \sqrt{2}]$$

**Energy check (Parseval)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum x^{2}[n] &= 16 + 36 + 100 + 144 + 64 + 36 + 16 + 4 = 416 \\
\sum a_1^{2} + \sum d_1^{2} &= (50 + 242 + 98 + 18) + (2 + 2 + 2 + 2) = 408 + 8 = 416 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy is conserved — confirming orthonormal decomposition.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps** (drawn from 2022–2024 KTU answer scripts):
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}}$ normalization in CWT** — costs 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Confusing $g[n] = (-1)^{n} h[N-1-n]$ with $g[n] = -h[n]$** — the latter is WRONG; always include the time-reversal $N-1-n$.
> 3. **Writing the down-sampling index as $2k$ instead of $k-2n$** in Mallat's equation. Both are correct conventions, but be **consistent**.
> 4. **Skipping the Parseval/energy verification** in numerical DWT problems — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks for the energy-conservation check.
> 5. **Forgetting $\sum h[n] = \sqrt{2}$** (not 1!) — this is a frequently-tested normalization.
> 6. **In MRA questions, not explicitly stating $V_{j+1} = V_j \oplus W_j$ with $\oplus$** — verbal explanation alone loses the mark; the direct-sum notation is required.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CWT** = continuous scale + translation; **DWT** = dyadic (powers of 2) sampling — non-redundant, orthogonal, fast.
- **Mother wavelet** must be admissible: finite energy, zero mean, band-pass behavior.
- **Scale $a$** ∝ $\frac{1}{\text{frequency}}$: large $a$ = low-freq probe, small $a$ = high-freq probe.
- **Scaling function $\phi(t)$** spans approximation subspace $V_j$; **wavelet function $\psi(t)$** spans detail subspace $W_j$.
- **Two-scale equation**: $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum h[n]\phi(2t-n)$ and $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2}\sum g[n]\phi(2t-n)$.
- **QMF relation**: $g[n] = (-1)^{n} h[N-1-n]$ — *must memorize*.
- **Mallat's pyramid**: convolve with $h$ (or $g$), then **downsample by 2**; iterate on approximation.
- **Parseval identity**: $\Vert x \Vert^{2} = \sum \vert a_J \vert^{2} + \sum_{j} \sum \vert d_j \vert^{2}$.
- **Haar (db1) filters**: $h = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, 1]$, $g = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}[1, -1]$ — the *only* orthonormal real-coefficient compact-support symmetric wavelet.
- **MRA nesting**: $V_{j+1} = V_j \oplus W_j$ with $V_j \perp W_j$.
- **Biomedical Applications** (must mention at least one for full marks): ECG denoising, EEG spike detection, EMG classification, MRI/CT compression (JPEG2000).
- **CWT is for analysis** (exploratory, scalogram); **DWT is for processing** (compression, denoising, feature extraction).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filter Banks and Applications of DWT: Wavelet Denoising, ECG Feature Detection, Heart Sound Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Filter Banks and Applications of DWT: Wavelet Denoising, ECG Feature Detection, Heart Sound Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Filter Banks in DWT — Formal Definition

A **Filter Bank** is a parallel/sequential arrangement of digital filters used to decompose a signal into multiple sub-band components. In the context of the **Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT)**, a two-channel filter bank splits the input signal $x[n]$ into a low-frequency (approximation) component and a high-frequency (detail) component, typically followed by **down-sampling by 2** to remove redundancy.

The filter bank has two stages:
- **Analysis Filter Bank**: $H_0(z)$ (low-pass) and $H_1(z)$ (high-pass) — used during decomposition.
- **Synthesis Filter Bank**: $G_0(z)$ (low-pass) and $G_1(z)$ (high-pass) — used during reconstruction.

Two important classes of two-channel filter banks for DWT are:
1. **Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) Bank**
2. **Conjugate Quadrature Filter (CQF) Bank**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Perfect Reconstruction (PR)** means the reconstructed signal $\hat{x}[n]$ equals the original $x[n]$ (possibly with delay). This is the gold-standard property for lossless wavelet sub-band coding.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Spectacles" of a Signal

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Stethoscope of Frequencies):**
> Imagine a doctor's stethoscope listening to a heart. The stethoscope has a **bell** (low frequencies — the "lub-dub" S1/S2 sounds) and a **diaphragm** (high frequencies — murmurs). A **filter bank does exactly this to a signal**: it splits the signal into two "ears" — one tuned to bass (low-pass) and one tuned to treble (high-pass). The **QMF bank** ensures the two ears are *mirror images* of each other (no overlap distortion), while the **CQF bank** ensures the two ears are *mathematically orthogonal* (independent and clean). Together, they form the heart of any DWT-based biomedical analyzer.

### 1.3 Quadrature Mirror Filter (QMF) Bank

**Definition:** A QMF bank is a two-channel filter bank where the high-pass filter is obtained from the low-pass filter by the relation:

$$H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$$

**Key Properties:**
- It is **alias cancellation** by design.
- It is **not** perfect reconstruction in general (perfect reconstruction is only achieved with specific choices like the **CQF**).
- The name "quadrature mirror" reflects the **mirror-image symmetry** of the magnitude responses about $\pi/2$.

### 1.4 Conjugate Quadrature Filter (CQF) Bank

**Definition:** A CQF bank is a two-channel filter bank in which the filters satisfy:

$$H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$$

where $N$ is the filter order. This means the high-pass filter is the **time-reversed, conjugated mirror** of the low-pass filter.

**Key Properties:**
- Provides **Perfect Reconstruction (PR)** in the absence of quantization.
- Equivalent to **orthogonal wavelet** filter banks (e.g., Daubechies wavelets).

### 1.5 Relationship Between CQF and QMF

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU:**
> - **CQF** $\Rightarrow$ Perfect Reconstruction (PR) ✓, Alias-canceled ✓
> - **QMF** (general) $\Rightarrow$ Alias-canceled ✓, but NOT PR unless designed as CQF
> - Every CQF is a QMF, but not every QMF is a CQF.
> - A CQF with linear phase is **impossible** except for the trivial Haar filter (length 2).

| Feature | QMF | CQF |
|---|---|---|
| Relation | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$ |
| Alias Cancellation | Yes | Yes |
| Perfect Reconstruction | Only for special cases | Yes (in principle) |
| Linear Phase | Possible | No (except Haar) |
| Orthogonal Wavelets | No (in general) | Yes |

### 1.6 Applications of DWT in Biomedical Signal Processing

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillar Applications in KTU Module 2:**
> 1. **Wavelet Denoising** — Removing noise (e.g., 50 Hz powerline interference) from biomedical signals.
> 2. **ECG Feature Detection** — Locating QRS complexes, R-peaks, P and T waves.
> 3. **Heart Sound (PCG) Analysis** — Detecting S1, S2 sounds and murmurs from phonocardiograms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
