## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two-Channel Filter Bank — The Architecture

A two-channel analysis-synthesis filter bank for DWT is structured as:

- **Analysis side**: Input $x[n]$ is filtered by $H_0(z)$ (LP) and $H_1(z)$ (HP), producing $x_0[n]$ and $x_1[n]$. These are then **down-sampled by 2** ($\downarrow 2$) to yield $v_0[n]$ and $v_1[n]$.
- **Synthesis side**: The sub-band signals are **up-sampled by 2** ($\uparrow 2$) and filtered by $G_0(z)$ and $G_1(z)$, then summed to form $\hat{x}[n]$.

The output of the analysis section for each branch is:
$$v_k[n] = \sum_{m} h_k[m]\, x[2n - m], \quad k = 0, 1$$

### 2.2 Conditions for Perfect Reconstruction

For PR, the reconstructed signal must equal the delayed input:

$$\hat{x}[n] = x[n - N]$$

This requires three conditions:
1. **Alias Cancellation:** $H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) = 0$
2. **Amplitude Distortion Cancellation (no flat loss):** $H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z) = 2 z^{-N}$
3. **Phase Distortion:** The overall delay is a pure delay $N$.

### 2.3 QMF Derivation of the Alias-Cancel Condition

In a QMF bank, $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$. Substituting into the alias-cancel condition:

$$H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_0(z) G_1(z) = 0$$

A simple choice is $G_0(z) = H_0(z)$ and $G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$, giving:

$$G_1(z) = -H_0(-z) = -H_1(z)$$

However, this gives the amplitude response:

$$T(z) = H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)$$

which is generally **not equal to a pure delay** (perfect reconstruction is not achieved unless we choose carefully).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The QMF Bank's Famous Design Equation:**
> For the special "Johnston-QMF" or "CQF" choice, we set $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$, which converts the QMF into a CQF and gives PR.

### 2.4 CQF — The Orthogonal Wavelet Condition

For CQF, $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$ and $G_0(z) = H_0(z^{-1})$, $G_1(z) = H_1(z^{-1})$.

The Power Symmetry / PR condition becomes:

$$H_0(z) H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z) H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$$

This is the **CQF power-complementary condition**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All key formulas for Filter Banks and DWT Applications — Master These!**

| # | Concept | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | QMF Relation | $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ | Mirror symmetry |
| 2 | CQF Relation | $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$ | Orthogonal, time-reversed |
| 3 | PR Condition (Amplitude) | $H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z) = 2 z^{-N}$ | No magnitude distortion |
| 4 | PR Condition (Alias) | $H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) = 0$ | Alias cancellation |
| 5 | CQF Power Symmetry | $\vert H_0(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 + \vert H_0(e^{j(\omega - \pi)}) \vert^2 = 2$ | At every frequency |
| 6 | DWT Approximation | $cA_j[n] = \sum_m h_0[m] \cdot cA_{j+1}[2n - m]$ | Recursive decomposition |
| 7 | DWT Detail | $cD_j[n] = \sum_m h_1[m] \cdot cA_{j+1}[2n - m]$ | High-frequency content |
| 8 | Universal Threshold (Donoho) | $\lambda = \sigma \sqrt{2 \ln N}$ | For hard/soft thresholding |
| 9 | Noise Std Estimator | $\sigma = \dfrac{\text{median}(\vert cD_1 \vert)}{0.6745}$ | Robust MAD estimator |
| 10 | Hard Thresholding | $\hat{w} = w$ if $\vert w \vert > \lambda$, else $0$ | Discontinuous |
| 11 | Soft Thresholding | $\hat{w} = \text{sgn}(w) \cdot \max(\vert w \vert - \lambda, 0)$ | Continuous, biased |
| 12 | R-Peak Modulus Maxima | $\vert W_{s} f(t) \vert$ at $t = t_R$ is local max | Multiscale property |
| 13 | QRS Energy Distribution | Scales $2^1$ to $2^4$ have peak energy | Most diagnostic scales |
| 14 | Heart Sound Frequency | S1: 20–200 Hz, S2: 20–250 Hz | Overlap with murmur band |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Biomedical Engineering:**
> - **Pacemaker Designers** use DWT to detect arrhythmias in real time.
> - **Tele-cardiology** systems rely on wavelet denoising to clean ECG signals transmitted over noisy mobile channels.
> - **Phonocardiogram (PCG) analyzers** use filter banks to separate S1/S2 sounds from murmurs for early diagnosis of valvular disorders.
> - **FDA-cleared devices** like the AliveCor KardiaMobile use wavelet-based feature extraction for AF detection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Perfect Reconstruction Condition for a Two-Channel Filter Bank

We start with the analysis side. Let $X(z)$ be the input. After filtering and down-sampling by 2, the two sub-band signals are:

$$V_0(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_0(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_0(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2}) \right]$$

$$V_1(z) = \frac{1}{2} \left[ H_1(z^{1/2}) X(z^{1/2}) + H_1(-z^{1/2}) X(-z^{1/2}) \right]$$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ comes from the down-sampling identity, and the two terms are the **even** and **odd** polyphase components.

After up-sampling by 2 and synthesis filtering, the output is:

$$\hat{X}(z) = G_0(z) V_0(z^2) + G_1(z) V_1(z^2)$$

Substituting and grouping terms with $X(z)$ and $X(-z)$:

$$\hat{X}(z) = \underbrace{\left[ H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z) \right]}_{T(z)} X(z) + \underbrace{\left[ H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) \right]}_{A(z)} X(-z)$$

For **Perfect Reconstruction** (ignoring delay), we need:

**Step 1 — Alias Cancellation:** The coefficient of $X(-z)$ must vanish:
$$H_0(-z) G_0(z) + H_1(-z) G_1(z) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Pure Delay (Amplitude Distortion Removal):** The transfer function $T(z)$ must be a pure delay:
$$H_0(z) G_0(z) + H_1(z) G_1(z) = 2 z^{-N}$$

**Step 3 — Choose Synthesis Filters to Satisfy Step 1:**
Pick:
$$G_0(z) = H_1(-z), \quad G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$$

Substituting back:
$$H_0(-z) H_1(-z) - H_1(-z) H_0(-z) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

And:
$$T(z) = H_0(z) H_1(-z) - H_1(z) H_0(-z)$$

**Step 4 — QMF Sub-Case:** Set $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ (QMF relation). Then:
$$T(z) = H_0(z) H_0(z) - H_0(-z) H_0(-z) = H_0^2(z) - H_0^2(-z)$$

This is **not** a pure delay in general — it has both magnitude and phase distortion. Therefore, a generic QMF is **not** PR.

**Step 5 — CQF Sub-Case:** Set $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$. The transfer function simplifies to a pure delay:
$$T(z) = 2 z^{-N}$$

This is the famous **Perfect Reconstruction** result for the CQF bank. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Summary:** The CQF bank is the **only** class of two-channel QMF banks that achieves perfect reconstruction in the lossless case. This is why Daubechies wavelets (which are CQF-based) dominate wavelet signal processing.

### 3.2 Derivation: Universal Threshold (Donoho's Formula)

Given a noisy signal $x[n] = s[n] + \eta[n]$, where $\eta \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ is i.i.d. Gaussian noise of length $N$:

**Step 1 — Worst-Case Bound on the Maximum of Noise Coefficients:**

The probability that any wavelet coefficient of pure noise exceeds a value $t$ is bounded by:
$$P\left( \max_k \vert w_k \vert > t \right) \leq \frac{N}{2} \cdot \text{erfc}\left( \frac{t}{\sigma \sqrt{2}} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Choose $t$ such that this probability $\to 0$:**

Set the probability to $\frac{1}{N}$ (or use asymptotic theory):
$$\frac{N}{2} \cdot \frac{2 \sigma}{\sqrt{2\pi} t} \, e^{-t^2 / 2\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{N}$$

Solving asymptotically:
$$t^2 = 2 \sigma^2 \ln N$$

**Step 3 — Donoho's Universal Threshold:**
$$\lambda = \sigma \sqrt{2 \ln N}$$

**Step 4 — Estimate $\sigma$ from data (Robust MAD):**
$$\sigma = \frac{\text{median}(\vert cD_1 \vert)}{0.6745}$$

The constant $0.6745$ is the **median of the absolute value of a standard normal distribution** divided by its standard deviation, providing a robust scale estimate.

### 3.3 Derivation: Modulus Maxima for R-Peak Detection

Given an ECG signal $f(t)$ and its continuous wavelet transform (CWT) using a smoothing function $\theta(t)$:

$$W_s f(t) = f * \psi_s(t)$$

where $\psi_s(t) = s \frac{d\theta}{dt}(t)$ is a wavelet related to the derivative of a smoothing function.

**Key property:** Local extrema of $\vert W_s f(t) \vert$ correspond to the **zero-crossings of $W_s^2 f(t)$**, which in turn correspond to **sharp transitions in $f(t)$** — i.e., the R-peak!

Algorithm:
1. Compute $\vert W_s f(t) \vert$ at scales $s = 2^1, 2^2, 2^3, 2^4$.
2. Find **modulus maxima** (local peaks) of $\vert W_s f(t) \vert$.
3. Track the maxima across scales; R-peaks produce a **propagating ridge** of maxima from fine to coarse scales.
4. Threshold the maxima to isolate R-peaks from noise.

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Wavelet Denoising on Synthetic Signal

**Given:** $x[n] = \sin(2\pi \cdot 5 n / 1000) + 0.5 \cdot \text{randn}(1, 1024)$, $N = 1024$.

**Step 1:** Apply 4-level DWT with `db4` (Daubechies-4, a CQF).

**Step 2:** Estimate noise level:
$$\sigma = \frac{\text{median}(\vert cD_1 \vert)}{0.6745} \approx 0.485$$

**Step 3:** Universal threshold:
$$\lambda = 0.485 \times \sqrt{2 \times \ln(1024)} \approx 0.485 \times 3.717 \approx 1.803$$

**Step 4:** Apply soft thresholding on detail coefficients $cD_1, cD_2, cD_3, cD_4$.

**Step 5:** Reconstruct using inverse DWT.

**Result:** SNR improvement of $\approx 8$–$10$ dB is typical.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Hard vs Soft Thresholding

| Property | Hard Thresholding | Soft Thresholding |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\hat{w} = w \cdot \mathbf{1}_{\vert w \vert > \lambda}$ | $\hat{w} = \text{sgn}(w) \cdot \max(\vert w \vert - \lambda, 0)$ |
| Continuity | Discontinuous at $\vert w \vert = \lambda$ | Continuous everywhere |
| Bias | Unbiased (large coeffs preserved) | Biased (shrinks all coeffs by $\lambda$) |
| Variance | Higher | Lower (smoother) |
| Best for | Spike-like features (e.g., R-peaks) | Continuous signals (e.g., PCG) |
| KTU Preference | Mentioned in theory | Often used in denoising examples |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Two-Channel Filter Bank Architecture (DWT Analysis-Synthesis)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input["x[n] - Input Signal"] --> H0["H0(z) - Low-pass Filter"]
    Input --> H1["H1(z) - High-pass Filter"]
    H0 --> DownA["Down-sample by 2"]
    H1 --> DownB["Down-sample by 2"]
    DownA --> OutA["cA - Approximation Coeffs"]
    DownB --> OutB["cD - Detail Coeffs"]
    OutA --> UpA["Up-sample by 2"]
    OutB --> UpB["Up-sample by 2"]
    UpA --> G0["G0(z) - Synthesis LP"]
    UpB --> G1["G1(z) - Synthesis HP"]
    G0 --> Sum["+ Adder"]
    G1 --> Sum
    Sum --> Out["xhat[n] - Reconstructed Signal"]
```

### 4.2 CQF vs QMF — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Two-Channel Filter Bank"] --> B{"Type?"}
    B -->|"H1(z) = H0(-z)"| C["QMF Bank"]
    B -->|"H1(z) = z^-N * H0(-z^-1)"| D["CQF Bank"]
    C --> E["Alias-free"]
    C --> F["Not PR in general"]
    D --> G["Alias-free"]
    D --> H["Perfect Reconstruction"]
    D --> I["Orthogonal Wavelets e.g. Daubechies"]
    C --> J["Used in Biorthogonal / Special Cases"]
```

### 4.3 Wavelet Denoising Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sig["Noisy Signal x[n]"] --> FwdDWT["Forward DWT - db4, 4 levels"]
    FwdDWT --> cA1["Approximation cA4"]
    FwdDWT --> cD1["Detail cD1"]
    FwdDWT --> cD2["Detail cD2"]
    FwdDWT --> cD3["Detail cD3"]
    FwdDWT --> cD4["Detail cD4"]
    cD1 --> T1["Threshold by lambda"]
    cD2 --> T2["Threshold by lambda"]
    cD3 --> T3["Threshold by lambda"]
    cD4 --> T4["Threshold by lambda"]
    cA1 --> KeepA["Keep Unchanged"]
    T1 --> Inv["Inverse DWT"]
    T2 --> Inv
    T3 --> Inv
    T4 --> Inv
    KeepA --> Inv
    Inv --> Clean["Denoised Signal"]
```

### 4.4 ECG R-Peak Detection via DWT Modulus Maxima

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ECG["Raw ECG Signal"] --> Pre["Bandpass Filter 5-40 Hz"]
    Pre --> CWT["CWT with Daubechies-2 across scales 2^1 to 2^4"]
    CWT --> Mod["Compute Modulus abs of Wavelet Coeffs"]
    Mod --> Max["Find Modulus Maxima at each scale"]
    Max --> Track["Track Maxima across scales - Ridge Linking"]
    Track --> Thr["Apply Adaptive Threshold"]
    Thr --> R["R-Peak Locations Detected"]
    R --> QRS["QRS Complex Identification"]
```

### 4.5 Heart Sound (PCG) Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PCG["Phonocardiogram Signal"] --> DWT["DWT - Symlet wavelet, 5 levels"]
    DWT --> Approx["Approximation cA5 - 20-200 Hz S1/S2 dominant"]
    DWT --> Detail["Detail cD3 to cD5 - Murmur/High Freq"]
    Approx --> Env["Compute Shannon Energy Envelope"]
    Env --> Peak["Peak Detection for S1 and S2"]
    Detail --> Murmur["Detect Murmur via Energy in cD3-cD5"]
    Peak --> Label["Annotate Heart Cycle S1-Systole-S2-Diastole"]
    Murmur --> Label
    Label --> Dx["Diagnostic Output: Normal / Murmur / Pathology"]
```

### 4.6 Multi-Resolution Decomposition Tree (3 Levels)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["S - Original Signal"] --> L1["cA1 - Level 1 Approximation 0 to fs/4"]
    S --> H1["cD1 - Level 1 Detail fs/4 to fs/2"]
    L1 --> L2["cA2 - Level 2 Approx 0 to fs/8"]
    L1 --> H2["cD2 - Level 2 Detail fs/8 to fs/4"]
    L2 --> L3["cA3 - Level 3 Approx 0 to fs/16"]
    L2 --> H3["cD3 - Level 3 Detail fs/16 to fs/8"]
    H1 --> Reject["High-freq noise often discarded"]
    H2 --> Reject
    H3 --> Reject
    L3 --> Out["Smoothed Approximation - Feature-rich"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State the relationship between the low-pass and high-pass filters in (a) a QMF bank, and (b) a CQF bank. State one key difference between them.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(a) QMF:** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ — the high-pass filter is the mirror image (with frequency inversion) of the low-pass filter.
- **(b) CQF:** $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$ — the high-pass filter is the time-reversed, conjugated mirror of the low-pass filter.
- **Key difference:** A CQF bank provides **Perfect Reconstruction (PR)** and corresponds to orthogonal wavelets, whereas a generic QMF bank does **not** achieve PR. Additionally, a CQF filter has **non-linear phase**, while a QMF can be designed to have **linear phase**.

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the Universal Threshold used in wavelet denoising. How is the noise standard deviation $\sigma$ estimated from the wavelet detail coefficients?**

**Model Answer:**
- The **Universal Threshold** (proposed by Donoho) is given by:
  $$\lambda = \sigma \sqrt{2 \ln N}$$
  where $N$ is the signal length and $\sigma$ is the noise standard deviation.
- It is used for hard or soft thresholding of wavelet coefficients — coefficients with magnitude $\le \lambda$ are suppressed (set to zero or shrunk).
- **Estimation of $\sigma$** uses the **Median Absolute Deviation (MAD)** of the finest-scale detail coefficients $cD_1$:
  $$\sigma = \frac{\text{median}\left(\vert cD_1 \vert\right)}{0.6745}$$
- The constant $0.6745$ corresponds to the median of $\vert \mathcal{N}(0,1) \vert$, making the estimator robust to outliers (such as actual signal components).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the conditions for **Perfect Reconstruction (PR)** in a two-channel analysis-synthesis filter bank. Show clearly the **alias cancellation** and **amplitude distortion** conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the relationship between a **CQF bank** and an **orthogonal wavelet transform**, with a neat block diagram of the CQF-based DWT decomposition tree. Show how the **Daubechies-4 (db4)** wavelet is an example of a CQF. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Filter Bank Setup:**
Let $X(z)$ be the $Z$-transform of the input. After filtering by $H_0(z), H_1(z)$ and down-sampling by 2, the sub-band $Z$-transforms are:
$$V_k(z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[H_k(z^{1/2})X(z^{1/2}) + H_k(-z^{1/2})X(-z^{1/2})\right], \quad k=0,1$$

**Step 2 — Synthesis and Output:**
After up-sampling and synthesis filtering:
$$\hat{X}(z) = G_0(z)V_0(z^2) + G_1(z)V_1(z^2)$$

**Step 3 — Substituting and Grouping:**
$$\hat{X}(z) = \underbrace{\left[H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z)\right]}_{T(z)\text{ — distortion}} X(z) + \underbrace{\left[H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z)\right]}_{A(z)\text{ — aliasing}} X(-z)$$

**Step 4 — PR Conditions:**
- **Alias Cancellation:** $H_0(-z)G_0(z) + H_1(-z)G_1(z) = 0$
- **Pure Delay (Amplitude):** $H_0(z)G_0(z) + H_1(z)G_1(z) = 2z^{-N}$

**Step 5 — Choice to Satisfy Alias Cancellation:**
Choose $G_0(z) = H_1(-z)$ and $G_1(z) = -H_0(-z)$. Then alias is automatically canceled.

**Step 6 — Resulting Distortion Function:**
$$T(z) = H_0(z)H_1(-z) - H_1(z)H_0(-z)$$

For PR, we need $T(z) = 2z^{-N}$. This is satisfied if and only if the CQF condition holds. ✓

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — CQF Definition:**
A CQF bank satisfies:
$$H_1(z) = z^{-N}H_0(-z^{-1})$$
$$G_0(z) = H_0(z^{-1}), \quad G_1(z) = H_1(z^{-1})$$

**Step 2 — Connection to Orthogonal Wavelets:**
The scaling filter $H_0(z)$ corresponds to the **scaling function** $\phi(t)$ of an orthogonal MRA, and $H_1(z)$ corresponds to the **wavelet function** $\psi(t)$. The condition $H_0(z)H_0(z^{-1}) + H_0(-z)H_0(-z^{-1}) = 2$ is exactly the **biorthogonality/orthogonality** of $\phi$ and $\psi$ in the time domain.

**Step 3 — Daubechies-4 Example:**
The Daubechies-4 (db4) wavelet has 8 coefficients for $H_0(z)$:
$$H_0(z) = \frac{1}{4\sqrt{2}}\left[(1+\sqrt{3}) + (3+\sqrt{3})z^{-1} + (3-\sqrt{3})z^{-2} + (1-\sqrt{3})z^{-3}\right]$$
It satisfies the CQF condition with $N=3$, and forms an orthogonal wavelet basis.

**Step 4 — Decomposition Tree (3 levels):**
[S1 → cA1 + cD1, S1 → cA2 + cD2, etc., as in Section 4.6]

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Wavelet Denoising** procedure for an ECG signal corrupted by Gaussian noise. State and justify the choice of wavelet and thresholding rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe how the **DWT modulus maxima method** is used for **R-peak detection** in ECG signal analysis. Why is multi-scale analysis essential? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Denoising Pipeline:**
The standard 3-step procedure (Donoho, 1995) is:
1. **Forward DWT:** Decompose the noisy ECG $x[n]$ into approximation and detail coefficients at multiple levels (typically 4–5) using a chosen wavelet (e.g., `db4`, `sym8`, or `coif4`).
2. **Thresholding:** Apply hard or soft thresholding on the detail coefficients $cD_1, cD_2, \dots, cD_J$. The approximation coefficients $cA_J$ are kept unchanged (they contain the main signal energy).
3. **Inverse DWT:** Reconstruct the denoised ECG from the thresholded coefficients.

**Step 2 — Choice of Wavelet:**
- For ECG denoising, **Daubechies (db4–db8)** or **Symlets (sym4–sym8)** are preferred because:
  - They are **CQF-based** (PR property).
  - They are **compactly supported** (good time localization for sharp QRS features).
  - They have sufficient smoothness to approximate ECG morphology.

**Step 3 — Thresholding Rule Justification:**
- **Universal threshold** $\lambda = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$ is theoretically optimal for i.i.d. Gaussian noise.
- **Soft thresholding** is preferred for ECG because it produces smoother reconstructions and avoids abrupt artifacts at $\vert w \vert = \lambda$.
- For preserving R-peaks, an **adaptive threshold** (e.g., `rigrsure` or `minimax`) may be used.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — CWT Setup:**
Compute the CWT of the ECG $f(t)$ using a wavelet that is the derivative of a smoothing function (e.g., the first derivative of a Gaussian, or a Daubechies-2 wavelet). The result $W_s f(t)$ is large at points where the ECG has sharp transitions.

**Step 2 — Modulus Maxima Detection:**
A **modulus maximum** at scale $s$ is a point $(s_0, t_0)$ where $\vert W_s f(t) \vert$ attains a local maximum. For an ECG:
- R-peaks produce prominent **positive** modulus maxima at finer scales ($s = 2^1, 2^2$).
- Q and S waves produce **negative** modulus maxima at finer scales.

**Step 3 — Multi-Scale Ridges:**
By tracking the modulus maxima across multiple scales, the R-peak produces a **persistent ridge** that propagates from fine to coarse scales. Noise-induced maxima, in contrast, do **not** persist across scales.

**Step 4 — Why Multi-Scale is Essential:**
- **Robustness:** Single-scale detection is highly sensitive to noise; multi-scale averaging smooths out spurious peaks.
- **Discrimination:** Different ECG features (P, QRS, T) have characteristic **scale signatures** — the QRS complex is dominant at scales $2^1$–$2^4$, while P and T waves are more prominent at coarser scales.
- **Adaptation:** The QRS width varies between patients and conditions; multi-scale analysis adapts to this variation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing QMF and CQF:** Many students write $H_1(z) = z^{-N}H_0(-z^{-1})$ for QMF. This is wrong — that is the **CQF** relation. QMF is $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Factor 0.6745:** When estimating $\sigma$ via MAD, omitting the $0.6745$ constant is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing Up Thresholding Rules:** Hard vs soft thresholding formulas are commonly interchanged — memorize the difference.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why" of Multi-Scale Analysis:** KTU examiners reward explanation, not just procedure. Always justify *why* multi-scale is needed.
> 5. **Forgetting to Mention CQF Gives PR:** A common 2-mark loss — QMF is alias-free but not PR; CQF is both.
> 6. **Wrong Median Estimator Domain:** The MAD estimator uses $cD_1$ (finest scale), not $cA_J$. This is a frequently tested point.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Filter Banks & DWT Applications**

- [x] A **filter bank** splits a signal into sub-bands; the two-channel version is the heart of the **fast DWT (Mallat's algorithm)**.
- [x] **QMF:** $H_1(z) = H_0(-z)$ — alias-free, mirror symmetry, but not PR in general.
- [x] **CQF:** $H_1(z) = z^{-N} H_0(-z^{-1})$ — alias-free **and** PR; corresponds to orthogonal wavelets (Daubechies, Symlets).
- [x] **Every CQF is a QMF, but not every QMF is a CQF.**
- [x] **PR Conditions** = (i) Alias cancellation + (ii) Amplitude distortion = pure delay.
- [x] **Daubechies wavelets (dbN)** are CQF-based and the most common choice in biomedical DWT.
- [x] **Wavelet Denoising Pipeline:** Forward DWT → Threshold detail coefficients → Inverse DWT.
- [x] **Universal Threshold (Donoho):** $\lambda = \sigma\sqrt{2\ln N}$ where $\sigma$ is estimated via **MAD** of $cD_1$ scaled by $1/0.6745$.
- [x] **Hard Thresholding:** discontinuous, preserves large coeffs exactly; **Soft Thresholding:** continuous, shrinks by $\lambda$.
- [x] **ECG R-Peak Detection:** Use **DWT modulus maxima** — track ridges across scales $2^1$–$2^4$.
- [x] **Multi-scale analysis** is essential because it discriminates QRS, P, and T waves and is robust to noise.
- [x] **Heart Sound (PCG) Frequencies:** S1 (20–200 Hz), S2 (20–250 Hz), murmurs can extend to 600+ Hz.
- [x] **PCG Denoising:** DWT separates S1/S2 (in approximation) from murmurs (in mid-detail scales).
- [x] **Standard choice** for ECG/PCG: `db4` to `db8` (CQF wavelets), 4–5 decomposition levels.
- [x] **CQF cannot have linear phase** (except Haar) — this is a fundamental result.
- [x] **DWT vs STFT:** DWT provides multi-resolution (good time + good frequency), STFT has fixed resolution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimal and Adaptive Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimal and Adaptive Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Optimal Filters — Formal Definition
An **Optimal Filter** is a linear, time-invariant (or slowly time-varying) discrete-time system whose coefficients are designed to minimize a pre-defined statistical error criterion, given complete *a priori* knowledge of the signal and noise statistics. In biomedical signal processing, the most celebrated optimal filter is the **Wiener Filter**, formulated by Norbert Wiener in 1949, which minimizes the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** between the filter output and a desired (reference) signal.

Mathematically, the optimal filter $\mathbf{w}$ is found by solving the discrete-time **Wiener-Hopf equation**:
$$R \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{p}$$

where $R$ is the autocorrelation matrix of the input, and $\mathbf{p}$ is the cross-correlation vector between the input and the desired response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Wiener filter is the cornerstone of optimal linear estimation. You must master the derivation of the Wiener-Hopf equation and its block-diagram interpretation.

### 1.2 Adaptive Filters — Formal Definition
An **Adaptive Filter** is a self-designing filter whose coefficients are *iteratively updated* using an adaptive algorithm, allowing it to track slow variations in the signal statistics without explicit recomputation. The two most studied algorithms in KTU syllabus are:

- **LMS (Least Mean Square)** — Widrow & Hoff, 1960 — gradient-descent based.
- **RLS (Recursive Least Squares)** — exact least-squares recursive solution.

The general update form is:
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \Delta \mathbf{w}(n)$$

where $\Delta \mathbf{w}(n)$ is the correction term computed by the chosen algorithm.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Headphones"
Imagine you are wearing headphones while studying in a noisy hostel mess hall. You want to hear your friend's voice (the **desired signal**) but cancel the loud background chatter (the **noise**).

- A **Wiener (optimal) filter** is like a *pre-set noise-canceling profile* designed after you have measured the room acoustics — it works perfectly *only if the room noise does not change*.
- An **adaptive filter** is like *smart noise-cancelling headphones (e.g., Sony WH-1000XM)* that continuously listen to the environment via a reference microphone, and *automatically update* their internal coefficients thousands of times per second to track the changing noise. If a bus horn suddenly appears, the filter adapts in real-time.

> [!NOTE]
> In biomedicine, the "friend's voice" is the clean fetal ECG or the evoked potential, while the "hostel chatter" is the mother's ECG, mains hum (50/60 Hz), and muscle artifacts.

### 1.4 Core Applications in Biomedical Signal Processing
| Application | Filter Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 50/60 Hz notch via adaptive cancellation | LMS / RLS | Remove powerline interference from ECG |
| Maternal ECG cancellation | Adaptive | Extract fetal ECG from abdominal leads |
| Adaptive Line Enhancer (ALE) | LMS | Detect periodic evoked potentials buried in noise |
| System Identification | LMS / RLS | Model an unknown biological system (e.g., heart valve) |
| Adaptive Beamforming | LMS | Focus ultrasound transducer arrays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard performance metrics: **Convergence rate**, **Misadjustment**, **Steady-state error**, **Computational complexity** ($O(N)$ for LMS, $O(N^2)$ for RLS, where $N$ is the filter order).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wiener Filter — Operational Concept (Step-by-Step)
1. **Set up the problem:** Let $x(n) = s(n) + v(n)$ be the primary input (signal $s$ corrupted by noise $v$). Let $d(n)$ be a reference (or desired) signal correlated with $s(n)$ but uncorrelated with $v(n)$.
2. **Define the FIR filter structure:** Output $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k \, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$.
3. **Formulate the cost function:** The MSE is
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - y(n)]^2\}$$
4. **Expand** the squared error, take expectation, and exploit the orthogonality principle: $E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\, e(n)\} = 0$ at the optimum.
5. **Derive the Wiener-Hopf equation:** $R \mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$, where $R = E\{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T\}$ and $\mathbf{p} = E\{\mathbf{x}\, d\}$.
6. **Solve** for $\mathbf{w}_o = R^{-1} \mathbf{p}$.
7. **Compute minimum MSE:** $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T R^{-1} \mathbf{p}$.

### 2.2 LMS Algorithm — Operational Concept
The LMS algorithm replaces the computationally expensive matrix inversion with a *stochastic gradient descent* on the instantaneous squared error $\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$.

1. **Filter output:** $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$.
2. **Error signal:** $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
3. **Gradient estimate:** $\hat{\nabla} J(n) = -2 e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$.
4. **Weight update:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$.

The scalar $\mu$ is the **step-size** (convergence parameter) that controls the trade-off between speed and stability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Widrow-Hoff"?** Bernard Widrow and Ted Hoff published the LMS algorithm in 1960. The "least mean square" name comes from the fact that the algorithm's *expected* weight trajectory follows the true gradient-descent curve, even though each step uses a noisy instantaneous estimate.

### 2.3 RLS Algorithm — Operational Concept
RLS recursively minimizes the *exponentially weighted* sum of squared errors:
$$J(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \, e^2(i)$$

The update uses the *Riccati equation* and a gain vector $\mathbf{k}(n)$, providing **faster convergence** than LMS at the cost of $O(N^2)$ operations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Units / Constraints |
|---|---|---|
| Wiener-Hopf equation | $R\,\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$ | $R$ is $N \times N$ symmetric positive-definite |
| Optimal weight vector | $\mathbf{w}_o = R^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}$ | Exists only if $R$ is invertible |
| Minimum MSE | $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T R^{-1} \mathbf{p}$ | Real and non-negative |
| LMS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$ for stability |
| LMS convergence condition | $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\text{tr}(R)}$ | Sufficient (not necessary) condition |
| Misadjustment (LMS) | $M \approx \mu \cdot \text{tr}(R)$ | $0 < M \ll 1$ desired |
| RLS gain vector | $\mathbf{k}(n) = \dfrac{P(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{x}^T(n)\,P(n-1)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}$ | $P(n) = R^{-1}$ estimate |
| RLS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,e(n)$ | Exact in absence of numerical errors |
| Forgetting factor range | $0 \ll \lambda \leq 1$ | Typical $\lambda = 0.98$ to $0.999$ |
| Wiener filter transfer fn | $W(z) = \dfrac{P_{xd}(z)}{P_{xx}(z)}$ | $P_{xd}$ cross-power spectrum |
| Adaptive Line Enhancer delay | $\Delta \geq 1$ sample | For decorrelating noise components |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Cardiac ICUs:** LMS-based 50 Hz notch filters clean ECG monitors in real-time.
- **Cochlear implants:** RLS algorithms adapt to the patient's auditory nerve response.
- **MRI image reconstruction:** Wiener filtering in $k$-space is optimal under Gaussian noise assumption.
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Adaptive filters track the slow drift in EEG electrode impedance.
- **Defibrillators:** Adaptive threshold detection distinguishes ventricular fibrillation from motion artifacts.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** Always state the **assumption of stationarity** explicitly when answering Wiener filter questions. For LMS, mention the **independence assumption** ($x(n)$ and $e(n)$ are statistically independent).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Wiener-Hopf Equation

**Step 1 — Define the FIR filter output.**
$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k \, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$$

where $\mathbf{w} = [w_0, w_1, \dots, w_{N-1}]^T$ and $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), \dots, x(n-N+1)]^T$.

**Step 2 — Express the error.**
$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 3 — Formulate the Mean Square Error cost function.**
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the squared term.**
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{d^2(n)\} - 2 E\{d(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}\,\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}\,\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the statistical matrices.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_d^2 &= E\{d^2(n)\} \quad \text{(desired signal power)} \\
\mathbf{p} &= E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n)\} \quad \text{(cross-correlation vector, } N \times 1) \\
R &= E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\} \quad \text{(autocorrelation matrix, } N \times N)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Rewrite the cost in compact matrix form.**
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T R \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 7 — Minimize by setting the gradient to zero.**
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2 R \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the optimal weight vector.**
$$R \mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}_o = R^{-1} \mathbf{p}$$

This is the **discrete Wiener-Hopf equation** (also called the *normal equation*).

**Step 9 — Compute the minimum MSE by substituting $\mathbf{w}_o$ back.**
$$J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T R^{-1} \mathbf{p}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the LMS Algorithm

**Step 1 — Define the instantaneous (stochastic) cost function.**
$$\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n) = [d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)]^2$$

**Step 2 — Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$.**
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{J}(n) = -2 e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 3 — Apply gradient descent with step-size $\mu$.**
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \hat{J}(n)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the gradient.**
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

This is the **LMS update equation**.

**Step 5 — Convergence analysis (stability bound).**

Taking expectation and using the independence assumption:
$$E\{\mathbf{w}(n+1)\} = (I - 2\mu R)\,E\{\mathbf{w}(n)\} + 2\mu \mathbf{p}$$

For convergence, all eigenvalues of $(I - 2\mu R)$ must satisfy $\vert 1 - 2\mu \lambda_i \vert < 1$, which gives:
$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$$

A simpler *sufficient* condition (Widrow's rule):
$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\text{tr}(R)} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} r_{xx}(0)}$$

**Step 6 — Misadjustment derivation (advanced, KTU bonus).**

The excess MSE in steady state is:
$$J_{\text{ex}}(\infty) = \mu \cdot \text{tr}(R) \cdot J_{\min}$$

The misadjustment is defined as:
$$M = \frac{J_{\text{ex}}(\infty)}{J_{\min}} = \mu \cdot \text{tr}(R)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A small $\mu$ gives low misadjustment but slow convergence. A large $\mu$ gives fast convergence but high misadjustment. This is the **fundamental LMS trade-off**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LMS vs RLS

| Parameter | LMS Algorithm | RLS Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| Cost function minimized | $E\{e^2(n)\}$ (statistical) | $\sum \lambda^{n-i} e^2(i)$ (deterministic LS) |
| Update equation | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | $\mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n) e(n)$ |
| Computational complexity | $2N+1$ multiplications per sample | $3N^2 + 2N$ multiplications per sample |
| Memory requirement | $2N$ locations | $N^2 + 3N$ locations |
| Convergence speed | Slow (depends on eigenvalue spread) | Fast (independent of eigenvalue spread, ~$2N$ samples) |
| Tracking ability | Moderate | Excellent (with proper $\lambda$) |
| Numerical stability | Excellent (no matrix inversion) | Sensitive (requires careful initialization) |
| Typical KTU application | Adaptive noise canceller in ECG | High-speed evoked potential estimation |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)
**Problem:** A 2-tap LMS adaptive filter is used to cancel noise. At sample $n$, the input vector is $\mathbf{x}(n) = [0.5, 0.3]^T$, the desired signal is $d(n) = 1.0$, and the current weight vector is $\mathbf{w}(n) = [0.2, 0.1]^T$. Take $\mu = 0.1$. Find $\mathbf{w}(n+1)$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(n) &= \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = (0.2)(0.5) + (0.1)(0.3) = 0.10 + 0.03 = 0.13 \\
e(n) &= d(n) - y(n) = 1.0 - 0.13 = 0.87 \\
2\mu e(n) &= 2(0.1)(0.87) = 0.174 \\
\Delta \mathbf{w}(n) &= 0.174 \cdot \mathbf{x}(n) = [0.087, \; 0.0522]^T \\
\mathbf{w}(n+1) &= [0.2, 0.1]^T + [0.087, 0.0522]^T = [0.287, \; 0.1522]^T
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Practice this exact style of problem — KTU frequently asks a 7-mark numerical on LMS weight update.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wiener Filter Block Diagram (Conceptual Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Input x of n"]:::input --> nodeB["Wiener FIR Filter W of z"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Output y of n"]
    nodeD["Desired d of n"]:::desired --> nodeE["Subtractor SUM"]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["Error e of n"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["MSE Estimator"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Optimizer Solves R w = p"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Optimal Weights w opt"]
    nodeI --> nodeB

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000
    classDef desired fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#000
    classDef error fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The optimizer uses *a priori* knowledge of $R$ and $\mathbf{p}$ to compute $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$ once, then the FIR filter produces $y(n)$ as a fixed, non-adaptive system.

### 4.2 LMS Adaptive Filter — Closed-Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Input x of n"] --> nodeB["Adaptive FIR Filter"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Output y of n"]
    nodeD["Desired d of n"] --> nodeE["Subtractor"]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["Error e of n"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["LMS Weight Update: w n+1 = w n + 2 mu e n x n"]
    nodeA --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeB

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000
    classDef desired fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#000
    classDef error fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404,color:#000
    classDef update fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The error $e(n)$ is fed back *continuously* to update the filter weights — this is the *adaptive* nature that distinguishes it from the Wiener filter.

### 4.3 Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC) — Biomedical Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Primary Input: s n + v n of ECG plus mains hum"] --> nodeB["Adder"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Reference Input: v prime n of pure 50 Hz pick-up"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Adaptive Filter LMS"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Output y n of estimated noise"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Subtractor: System Output"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Clean ECG: e n"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Weight Update Loop"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeD
```

**Interpretation:** The reference microphone picks up the 50 Hz interference alone; the adaptive filter models the noise path and subtracts its output from the primary (corrupted ECG) to yield a clean signal.

### 4.4 Convergence Comparison: LMS vs RLS (Learning Curve Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Start: n = 0"] --> nodeB["LMS Path: Slow descent ~ hundreds to thousands of samples"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["LMS Steady-State: Higher misadjustment"]
    nodeD["Start: n = 0"] --> nodeE["RLS Path: Steep descent ~ 2N samples"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["RLS Steady-State: Lower misadjustment"]

    subgraph KTU["KTU Key Takeaway"]
        nodeG["RLS converges faster but costs O of N squared operations"]
    end

    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
```

### 4.5 Adaptive Line Enhancer (ALE) for Evoked Potentials

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Noisy Evoked Potential x n"] --> nodeB["Delay Delta Samples"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Decorrelated Noise Path"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Direct Path Signal Component"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Adaptive LMS Filter"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Subtractor"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Enhanced Periodic Signal y n"]
```
**Interpretation:** The delay $\Delta$ decorrelates the broadband noise between the two paths while preserving the periodic evoked potential (which has long-range correlation), allowing the LMS filter to model and cancel only the noise.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State and explain the orthogonality principle of the Wiener filter.*

**Model Answer:**
The orthogonality principle states that at the optimal filter weights $\mathbf{w}_o$, the error signal $e_o(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}_o^T \mathbf{x}(n)$ is **orthogonal** to every input sample in the tap-delay line:
$$E\{\mathbf{x}(n) \, e_o(n)\} = \mathbf{0} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R \mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$$

This is the discrete form of the principle of *minimum mean square error*, ensuring that no further adjustment of weights can reduce $J$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Differentiate between Wiener and LMS filters in three points.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Wiener Filter | LMS Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Computation | One-time matrix inversion $R^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | Iterative, sample-by-sample update |
| Statistics | Requires *a priori* knowledge of $R$ and $\mathbf{p}$ | Estimates them implicitly from data |
| Adaptation | Non-adaptive (fixed weights) | Adaptive, tracks slow variations |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 internal choice questions are given. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Derive the Wiener-Hopf equation for a discrete-time FIR filter. Define the autocorrelation matrix $R$ and cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{p}$ clearly. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider an FIR filter of order $N$ with tap weights $w_0, w_1, \dots, w_{N-1}$ acting on input $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), \dots, x(n-N+1)]^T$. The output is $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)$ and the error is $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.

The MSE is:
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}$$

Expanding and taking expectations:
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{d^2(n)\} - 2 \mathbf{w}^T E\{\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)\} + \mathbf{w}^T E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}\mathbf{w}$$

Define:
- $\sigma_d^2 = E\{d^2(n)\}$ (desired signal power)
- $\mathbf{p} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)\}$ (cross-correlation vector, $N \times 1$)
- $R = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}$ (autocorrelation matrix, $N \times N$, symmetric Toeplitz)

Thus:
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{p}^T \mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T R \mathbf{w}$$

Setting the gradient $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2R\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$ yields the **Wiener-Hopf equation**:
$$R \mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}_o = R^{-1}\mathbf{p}$$

The minimum MSE is $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T R^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.

**(b) Explain the LMS adaptive algorithm with its update equation, convergence condition, and misadjustment formula. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The LMS algorithm minimizes the instantaneous squared error $\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$ using stochastic gradient descent.

**Update equation:**
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Convergence condition:** For mean convergence, the step-size must satisfy:
$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}} \quad \text{or, sufficient condition:} \quad 0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\text{tr}(R)}$$

**Misadjustment:** Defined as the ratio of excess MSE to minimum MSE in steady state:
$$M = \frac{J_{\text{ex}}(\infty)}{J_{\min}} \approx \mu \cdot \text{tr}(R)$$

A practical range is $M = 0.1$ to $0.5$, giving a good trade-off between speed and accuracy.

**Algorithm steps:**
1. Initialize $\mathbf{w}(0) = \mathbf{0}$ and choose $\mu$.
2. For each sample $n$: compute $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
3. Compute $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
4. Update $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
5. Repeat for $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an adaptive noise canceller for removing 50 Hz powerline interference from an ECG signal. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (see Section 4.3 above):**

- **Primary input:** $x_p(n) = s(n) + v(n)$, where $s(n)$ is the clean ECG and $v(n)$ is the 50 Hz mains interference picked up by the ECG electrode.
- **Reference input:** $x_r(n) = v'(n)$, a separate pickup of the pure 50 Hz signal (e.g., from a reference electrode placed near the mains source).
- **Adaptive filter:** An LMS (or RLS) filter of order $N$ adjusts its weights so that its output $y(n)$ is the best MMSE estimate of $v(n)$.
- **Subtractor:** The system output is $e(n) = x_p(n) - y(n) = s(n) + [v(n) - y(n)]$. As the filter converges, $y(n) \to v(n)$ and $e(n) \to s(n)$, the clean ECG.

**Assumptions:**
1. $s(n)$ is uncorrelated with both $v(n)$ and $v'(n)$.
2. $v(n)$ and $v'(n)$ are correlated (both originate from the same mains supply).
3. $v'(n)$ is *uncorrelated* with $s(n)$ — this is critical to prevent signal cancellation.

**Practical implementation:** Sampling rate $f_s = 500$ Hz, filter order $N = 32$, step-size $\mu = 0.01$.

**(b) Derive the LMS update equation starting from the steepest-descent method, and state the conditions for convergence. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Steepest-descent update is:
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(\mathbf{w}(n))$$

**Step 2:** Replace the true gradient $E\{-2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\}$ with the instantaneous estimate $\hat{\nabla} = -2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$:
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 3:** Taking expectation and using the independence assumption:
$$E\{\mathbf{w}(n+1)\} = (I - 2\mu R)E\{\mathbf{w}(n)\} + 2\mu \mathbf{p}$$

**Step 4:** In the eigenbasis, the $i$-th mode evolves as:
$$v_i(n+1) = (1 - 2\mu \lambda_i) v_i(n) + 2\mu p_i'$$

**Step 5:** For convergence of every mode:
$$|1 - 2\mu \lambda_i| < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$$

The LMS algorithm thus provides *misadjusted but stable* convergence to the Wiener solution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "MSE" and "Instantaneous Squared Error":** Many students write $J(n) = e^2(n)$ instead of $E\{e^2(n)\}$. This is acceptable for LMS derivation but **NOT** for the Wiener filter.
> 2. **Missing the "Independence Assumption":** Without stating that $e(n)$ and $\mathbf{x}(n)$ are statistically independent, the LMS weight-update derivation is incomplete.
> 3. **Wrong step-size bounds:** Writing $\mu < 1$ is too loose. You must state $\mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$ or $\mu < 1/\text{tr}(R)$.
> 4. **Forgetting units and dimensions:** Always specify that $R$ is $N \times N$ and $\mathbf{p}$ is $N \times 1$.
> 5. **Skipping the matrix expansion step:** Marks are specifically awarded for showing $J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T R \mathbf{w}$ explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wiener Filter:** Optimal, non-adaptive, requires $R$ and $\mathbf{p}$ *a priori*; solves $R\mathbf{w}_o = \mathbf{p}$.
- **LMS Algorithm:** Adaptive, uses instantaneous gradient; update is $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- **RLS Algorithm:** Recursive least-squares; faster convergence ($2N$ samples) but $O(N^2)$ complexity.
- **Convergence bound (LMS):** $0 < \mu < 1/\text{tr}(R)$; misadjustment $M = \mu \cdot \text{tr}(R)$.
- **Orthogonality principle:** $E\{\mathbf{x}(n) e_o(n)\} = \mathbf{0}$ at optimum.
- **Minimum MSE:** $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T R^{-1} \mathbf{p}$.
- **Adaptive Noise Canceller (ANC):** Primary $+$ Reference inputs; used for 50 Hz ECG cleaning.
- **Adaptive Line Enhancer (ALE):** Uses a delay $\Delta$ to decorrelate noise; enhances periodic signals like evoked potentials.
- **Fetal ECG extraction:** Reference = maternal ECG from chest; Primary = abdominal lead.
- **Trade-offs:** LMS → simple, robust, slow. RLS → fast, sensitive, expensive.
- **Computational cost:** LMS is $O(N)$; RLS is $O(N^2)$.
- **KTU Golden Rule:** Always pair every adaptive filter with its **biomedical application** in your answer — it boosts marks and demonstrates conceptual mastery.
- **Stability check:** LMS is inherently stable; RLS requires $\lambda < 1$ and proper $P(0)$ initialization.
- **Real-time feasibility:** LMS dominates in portable/embedded biomedical devices (e.g., wearable ECG patches) due to low power consumption.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wiener Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wiener Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: WIENER FILTERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wiener Filter** is a linear, time-invariant, optimal filter proposed by **Norbert Wiener (1949)** that produces an estimate of a desired signal from a noisy observed signal by minimizing the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** between the estimated signal and the true signal. It operates under the assumption that the underlying signal and additive noise are **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** linear stochastic processes with known **Power Spectral Densities (PSDs)** and known **cross-correlation / autocorrelation** properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEEBT634 – Module 3):**
> "A Wiener filter is an optimal linear filter that minimizes the mean-square error (MSE) between the estimated random process and the desired random process, assuming the signals are stationary. It is the linear Minimum Mean Square Error (LMMSE) estimator."

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The 'Smart Ear' at a Noisy Railway Station**
> Imagine you are standing at a crowded Ernakulam Junction railway platform. Your friend (the clean signal, $x(n)$) is speaking softly. Announcements over the PA system, train horns, and crowd chatter (additive noise, $v(n)$) drown out the voice. What you *actually* hear is the noisy mixture: $y(n) = x(n) + v(n)$.
>
> Your **brain** acts as a **Wiener Filter**. It does not just low-pass the sound (which would also muffle the voice). Instead, it has *learned* over years the statistical "color" of your friend's voice (its PSD, $S_x(f)$) and the statistical "color" of station noise ($S_n(f)$). At every frequency $f$, it decides: *"How much of the current frequency band is voice vs. noise?"* — and blends them optimally. Frequencies where voice dominates are passed through; frequencies where noise dominates are attenuated. The result is a *clean* estimate $\hat{x}(n)$.
>
> In short: **The Wiener filter is the optimal 'frequency mixer' that suppresses noise wherever noise is statistically stronger than the signal.**

### 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Terminology You MUST Know for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **$x(n)$** → Desired / true clean signal (e.g., clean ECG, fetal ECG, evoked potential).
> - **$v(n)$** → Additive noise (e.g., 50 Hz powerline interference, baseline wander, EMG artifact).
> - **$y(n) = x(n) + v(n)$** → Observed (corrupted) signal.
> - **$\hat{x}(n)$** → Filtered output (estimate of clean signal).
> - **$e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$** → Estimation error.
> - **$\xi = E\{e^2(n)\}$** → **Mean Square Error (MSE)** — the *quantity the Wiener filter minimizes*.
> - **$S_x(f),\ S_n(f),\ S_y(f)$** → Power Spectral Densities of signal, noise, observed.
> - **$H(f)$** → Frequency response of the optimal Wiener filter.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- The minimum achievable MSE is **$\xi_{\min} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} \dfrac{S_x(f)\,S_n(f)}{S_x(f)+S_n(f)} \, df$**.
- The filter is **optimal in the $L^2$ sense** — it is the *best* linear filter in terms of MSE; no other linear filter can beat it.
- The optimality is **unconditional** under stationarity and second-order statistics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Problem Statement

Given the observed discrete-time signal:

$$
y(n) = x(n) + v(n)
$$

we seek an FIR/IIR linear filter with impulse response $h(k)$ such that the estimate

$$
\hat{x}(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h(k)\,y(n-k)
$$

minimizes the **Mean Square Error (MSE)**:

$$
\xi = E\{[x(n) - \hat{x}(n)]^2\} = E\{e^2(n)\}
$$

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Reasoning

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work? (The 'Why' behind each step)**

1. **Formulate the error signal**: $e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$. The square $e^2(n)$ is *always non-negative*, and its expectation gives the average energy of the error.

2. **Expand $E\{e^2(n)\}$**: This yields three terms — a signal energy term, a cross-term, and a filter-output energy term.

3. **Take partial derivative w.r.t. each filter tap $h(k)$** and set it to **zero** (orthogonality principle / Euler-Lagrange optimality).

4. **Apply the orthogonality principle**: At the optimum, the error $e(n)$ is *uncorrelated* with every input sample $y(n-k)$. This is the **fundamental orthogonality condition** of the Wiener filter.

5. **Obtain the Wiener-Hopf equation (discrete, time domain)**:

$$
r_{xy}(m) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,r_{yy}(m-k),\quad m = 0,1,\ldots,N-1
$$

6. **Take the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of both sides:

$$
S_{xy}(f) = H(f)\,S_{yy}(f)
$$

7. **Solve for $H(f)$** (the non-causal Wiener filter transfer function):

$$
H(f) = \frac{S_{xy}(f)}{S_{yy}(f)}
$$

8. **Assume signal and noise are uncorrelated (zero cross-correlation)**: $S_{xv}(f) = 0$. Then $S_{xy}(f) = S_x(f)$ and $S_{yy}(f) = S_x(f) + S_n(f)$.

9. **Final closed-form Wiener filter expression**:

$$
H(f) = \frac{S_x(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)} = 1 - \frac{S_n(f)}{S_y(f)}
$$

10. **Estimate the clean signal in the frequency domain**:
$$
\hat{X}(f) = H(f)\,Y(f) = \frac{S_x(f)}{S_x(f)+S_n(f)}\,Y(f)
$$

11. **Inverse DTFT (or IDFT)** to recover the time-domain estimate $\hat{x}(n)$.

## 2.3 Key Engineering Assumptions (KTU Board-Favorite)

- Wide-sense stationarity of $x(n)$ and $v(n)$.
- Zero mean: $E\{x(n)\} = E\{v(n)\} = 0$.
- Linear and additive noise: $y(n) = x(n) + v(n)$.
- Signal and noise are **statistically independent / uncorrelated**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

- **ECG denoising** — removal of 50/60 Hz powerline interference and high-frequency muscle (EMG) noise.
- **Fetal ECG (FECG) extraction** from maternal abdominal recordings.
- **Evoked Potential (EP) estimation** — averaging is sub-optimal for non-stationary EP; Wiener filtering tracks SNR variations.
- **EEG artifact removal** — eliminating ocular (EOG) and cardiac (ECG) artifacts.
- **MRI / fMRI image reconstruction** — Wiener filter is the classical solution to inverse problems with noise.
- **Speech enhancement** for hearing aids (biomedical crossover).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER TABLE — Reproduce this in your exam revision notebook. These equations are KTU board-exam favorites.**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Domain | Unit / Note |
|---|----------|----------------------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Observed signal | $y(n) = x(n) + v(n)$ | Time | Samples (mV, µV) |
| 2 | Estimate | $\hat{x}(n) = \sum_k h(k)\,y(n-k)$ | Time | Linear convolution |
| 3 | Estimation error | $e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$ | Time | To be minimized |
| 4 | MSE to minimize | $\xi = E\{e^2(n)\}$ | Time | $\text{Volt}^2$ (power) |
| 5 | Cross-correlation $r_{xy}(m)$ | $E\{x(n)\,y(n+m)\}$ | Time | Cross-correlation |
| 6 | Autocorrelation $r_{yy}(m)$ | $E\{y(n)\,y(n+m)\}$ | Time | Even function |
| 7 | Wiener-Hopf equation | $r_{xy}(m) = \sum_k h(k)\,r_{yy}(m-k)$ | Time | Linear system |
| 8 | PSD of $y(n)$ | $S_{yy}(f) = S_x(f) + S_n(f)$ | Frequency | $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$ |
| 9 | Cross-PSD | $S_{xy}(f) = S_x(f)$ (if uncorr.) | Frequency | DTFT of $r_{xy}$ |
| 10 | **Non-causal Wiener filter** | $H(f) = \dfrac{S_x(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)}$ | Frequency | Optimal |
| 11 | Equivalent form | $H(f) = 1 - \dfrac{S_n(f)}{S_{yy}(f)}$ | Frequency | Used in practice |
| 12 | MMSE (min. MSE) | $\xi_{\min} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} \dfrac{S_x(f)\,S_n(f)}{S_x(f)+S_n(f)}\,df$ | Frequency | Lower bound |
| 13 | Output PSD | $S_{\hat{x}}(f) = \vert H(f)\vert^2\,S_{yy}(f)$ | Frequency | — |
| 14 | Wiener filter (matrix form) | $\mathbf{R}_{yy}\,\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{r}_{xy}$ | Time | $\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{xy}$ |
| 15 | Causal Wiener (Levinson) | Solved via **Levinson-Durbin** recursion | Time | For causal IIR |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Non-Causal Wiener Filter in the Frequency Domain

**Goal:** Derive $H(f) = \dfrac{S_x(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)}$.

**Step 1 — Express the estimate in the frequency domain.**

The convolution $\hat{x}(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h(k)\,y(n-k)$ becomes, after DTFT:

$$
\hat{X}(f) = H(f)\,Y(f)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the estimation error in the frequency domain.**

$$
E(f) = X(f) - \hat{X}(f) = X(f) - H(f)\,Y(f)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the Mean Square Error in the frequency domain using Parseval's theorem.**

$$
\xi = E\{e^2(n)\} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} S_e(f)\,df
$$

where $S_e(f) = E\{\vert E(f)\vert^2\}$ is the error PSD.

**Step 4 — Compute $S_e(f)$ for the unconstrained (non-causal) filter.**

$$
S_e(f) = E\bigl\{\vert X(f) - H(f)Y(f)\vert^2\bigr\}
$$

Expanding (assuming $H(f)$ is deterministic — it depends only on the statistics, not on a particular realization):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_e(f) &= E\{X(f)X^*(f)\} - H(f)\,E\{X(f)Y^*(f)\} - H^*(f)\,E\{Y(f)X^*(f)\} + \vert H(f)\vert^2\,E\{Y(f)Y^*(f)\} \\
&= S_{xx}(f) - H(f)\,S_{xy}^*(f) - H^*(f)\,S_{xy}(f) + \vert H(f)\vert^2\,S_{yy}(f)
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Each cross-PSD is the DTFT of the corresponding cross-correlation function.)*

**Step 5 — Minimize $S_e(f)$ point-by-point in $f$ (variational calculus).**

Because integration over $f$ is the sum of non-negative integrands, the *minimum of the integral* is achieved by **minimizing the integrand** at *each* frequency $f$. This is the *fundamental principle* that turns the infinite-dimensional problem into a pointwise scalar problem.

Take the complex derivative of $S_e(f)$ with respect to $H^*(f)$ (treating $H$ and $H^*$ as independent variables — Wirtinger calculus) and set to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial S_e(f)}{\partial H^*(f)} = -S_{xy}(f) + H(f)\,S_{yy}(f) = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $H(f)$.**

$$
H(f) = \frac{S_{xy}(f)}{S_{yy}(f)}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply the uncorrelated signal–noise assumption.**

If $x(n)$ and $v(n)$ are **uncorrelated and zero-mean**, then:

$$
S_{xy}(f) = S_x(f) \quad \text{and} \quad S_{yy}(f) = S_x(f) + S_n(f)
$$

**Step 8 — Final closed form.**

$$
\boxed{\,H(f) = \frac{S_x(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)} = 1 - \frac{S_n(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)} = 1 - \frac{S_n(f)}{S_{yy}(f)}\,}
$$

**Step 9 — Verify limiting cases (a KTU examiner's favorite sanity check).**

- If $S_n(f) \to 0$ (no noise) $\Rightarrow H(f) = 1$ → all-pass, identity estimate. ✔
- If $S_x(f) \to 0$ (no signal) $\Rightarrow H(f) = 0$ → total rejection. ✔
- If $S_x(f) = S_n(f)$ at some $f$ $\Rightarrow \vert H(f)\vert = 0.5$ → half-attenuation at the SNR = 0 dB crossover. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation II — Time-Domain Wiener-Hopf Equation (FIR Case, $N$ taps)

**Step 1 — Tap-weight vector:** $\mathbf{h} = [h(0), h(1), \ldots, h(N-1)]^T$.

**Step 2 — Form the estimate:**

$$
\hat{x}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,y(n-k) = \mathbf{h}^T \mathbf{y}(n)
$$

where $\mathbf{y}(n) = [y(n), y(n-1), \ldots, y(n-N+1)]^T$.

**Step 3 — Form the MSE:**

$$
\xi = E\{[x(n) - \mathbf{h}^T\mathbf{y}(n)]^2\}
$$

**Step 4 — Expand and split into three terms:**

$$
\xi = E\{x^2(n)\} - 2\,\mathbf{h}^T E\{x(n)\mathbf{y}(n)\} + \mathbf{h}^T E\{\mathbf{y}(n)\mathbf{y}^T(n)\}\,\mathbf{h}
$$

Define:
- $r_x(0) = E\{x^2(n)\}$ — signal power.
- $\mathbf{r}_{xy} = E\{x(n)\mathbf{y}(n)\}$ — $N \times 1$ cross-correlation vector.
- $\mathbf{R}_{yy} = E\{\mathbf{y}(n)\mathbf{y}^T(n)\}$ — $N \times N$ autocorrelation matrix.

**Step 5 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{h}$ and set to zero (vector gradient = 0).**

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{h}} \xi = -2\,\mathbf{r}_{xy} + 2\,\mathbf{R}_{yy}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 6 — Wiener-Hopf solution:**

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{h}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\,\mathbf{r}_{xy}\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Minimum MSE achieved:**

$$
\xi_{\min} = r_x(0) - \mathbf{r}_{xy}^T\,\mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\,\mathbf{r}_{xy}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem:** A 1-tap Wiener filter ($N=1$, single coefficient $h(0)$) is applied to $y(n) = x(n) + v(n)$. Given $r_{xx}(0) = 4$, $r_{vv}(0) = 1$, and $x, v$ uncorrelated. Find $h_{\text{opt}}$ and $\xi_{\min}$.

**Solution:**

- $r_{yy}(0) = r_{xx}(0) + r_{vv}(0) = 4 + 1 = 5$.
- $r_{xy}(0) = r_{xx}(0) = 4$ (since $E\{x(n)[x(n)+v(n)]\} = r_{xx}(0) + 0$).
- $h(0) = r_{xy}(0) / r_{yy}(0) = 4/5 = \mathbf{0.8}$.
- $\xi_{\min} = r_{xx}(0) - h(0)\cdot r_{xy}(0) = 4 - 0.8 \times 4 = \mathbf{0.8}$.

**Verification via frequency-domain formula:** With flat PSDs, $H = S_x/(S_x + S_n) = 4/(4+1) = 0.8$ ✔. **$\xi_{\min} = S_x \cdot S_n/(S_x+S_n) = 4 \times 1/5 = 0.8$** ✔.

## 3.4 Derivation III — Minimum MSE Closed Form

Starting from the optimal $H(f)$ derived in §3.1:

$$
S_e^{\min}(f) = S_{xx}(f) - \frac{\vert S_{xy}(f)\vert^2}{S_{yy}(f)}
$$

Under uncorrelated assumption, $S_{xy}(f) = S_x(f)$ (real, non-negative) and $S_{yy}(f) = S_x(f) + S_n(f)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_e^{\min}(f) &= S_x(f) - \frac{S_x^2(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)} \\
&= \frac{S_x(f)[S_x(f) + S_n(f)] - S_x^2(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)} \\
&= \frac{S_x(f)\,S_n(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating over all frequencies gives the **total MMSE**:

$$
\xi_{\min} = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} \frac{S_x(f)\,S_n(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)}\,df
$$

This is the **irreducible error floor** — no linear filter can do better.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Wiener Filter

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete signal-flow architecture: from observed noisy biomedical signal input, through the Wiener filter core (where $H(f)$ is computed from PSDs), to the clean estimate output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Signal y of n equals x of n plus v of n] --> B[DTFT Y of f]
    B --> C[Multiplication by H of f]
    D[True Signal PSD Sx of f] --> E[PSD Estimator Block]
    F[Noise PSD Sn of f] --> E
    E --> G[H of f equals Sx of f divided by Sx of f plus Sn of f]
    G --> C
    C --> H[Inverse DTFT to obtain x hat of n]
    H --> I[Clean Estimated Signal x hat of n]
    C --> J[Error e of n equals x of n minus x hat of n]
    K[Desired Signal x of n] --> J
    J --> L[Mean Square Error xi equals E of e squared of n]
    L --> M[Minimize xi via orthogonality principle]
    M --> G
```

## 4.2 Mermaid: Wiener Filter Decision Logic at Each Frequency

This flowchart visualizes how the Wiener filter *decides* at each frequency bin $f_k$ how much to pass through, based on the local SNR.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start Frequency Bin f sub k] --> Q[Compute Sx at f sub k]
    Q --> R[Compute Sn at f sub k]
    R --> S[Compute H at f sub k equals Sx divided by Sx plus Sn]
    S --> T{Is SNR at f sub k greater than 1}
    T -- Yes --> U[Pass frequency band H near 1]
    T -- No --> V[Attenuate frequency band H near 0]
    U --> W[Move to next frequency bin f sub k plus 1]
    V --> W
    W --> X{All bins processed}
    X -- No --> P
    X -- Yes --> Y[Reconstruct x hat of n via IDFT]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid: Orthogonality Principle — Why MSE is Minimized

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y[Input Vector y of n] --> H[Filter h optimal]
    H --> XHAT[Estimate x hat of n]
    X[True x of n] --> SUB[Subtractor]
    XHAT --> SUB
    SUB --> E[Error e of n]
    E -.Orthogonality Condition.-> Y
    E --> MSE[Mean Square Error xi]
    MSE --> MIN[Minimized]
```

**Interpretation of the orthogonality principle diagram:** The dotted feedback arrow from the error $e(n)$ to the input $y(n)$ is the *key* — it says that at the optimum, the error is statistically uncorrelated with every input sample. This is what *forces* the gradient to zero and yields the Wiener-Hopf equation.

## 4.4 Mermaid: Block Diagram of Causal vs. Non-Causal Wiener Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NCF[Non Causal Wiener Filter]
        Y1[Observed y of n] --> DTFT1[DTFT]
        DTFT1 --> MUL1[Multiply by H of f]
        MUL1 --> IDFT1[Inverse DTFT]
        IDFT1 --> OUT1[Estimate x hat of n]
    end
    subgraph CF[Causal Wiener Filter via Levinson Durbin]
        Y2[Observed y of n] --> AC[Compute ryy of m]
        AC --> LD[Levinson Durbin Recursion]
        LD --> IIR[Causal IIR Filter h of n]
        IIR --> OUT2[Estimate x hat of n causal]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**Q: Define the Wiener filter. State the optimality criterion it satisfies and write its frequency-domain transfer function for the case of uncorrelated signal and noise.** `[Model Question - KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The Wiener filter is an optimal linear time-invariant filter that estimates a desired signal $x(n)$ from a noise-corrupted observation $y(n) = x(n) + v(n)$ by **minimizing the Mean Square Error (MSE)** $\xi = E\{[x(n) - \hat{x}(n)]^2\}$.
>
> **Optimality criterion:** Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE) under the assumptions of linearity, wide-sense stationarity, and known second-order statistics.
>
> **Frequency-domain transfer function (uncorrelated signal and noise):**
>
> $$
> H(f) = \frac{S_x(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)}
> $$
>
> where $S_x(f)$ is the signal PSD and $S_n(f)$ is the noise PSD.

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**Q: State and explain the orthogonality principle that the Wiener filter satisfies at its optimum.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> The orthogonality principle states that at the optimum, the estimation error $e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$ is **statistically uncorrelated (orthogonal)** with every sample of the observed signal $y(n-k)$ used in forming the estimate:
>
> $$
> E\{e(n)\,y(n-k)\} = 0,\quad \text{for all } k
> $$
>
> Equivalently, the cross-correlation between error and input is zero: $r_{ey}(m) = 0$ for all lags $m$. This is the necessary and sufficient condition for the filter coefficients to give the global MMSE solution; differentiating $\xi$ and setting it to zero leads to exactly this orthogonality condition, and the **Wiener-Hopf equation** follows.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Derive the non-causal Wiener filter transfer function $H(f)$ in the frequency domain for the observed signal model $y(n) = x(n) + v(n)$, assuming $x(n)$ and $v(n)$ are uncorrelated zero-mean WSS random processes. State clearly the orthogonality principle you use.**

**Model Solution:**

*Given:* $y(n) = x(n) + v(n)$, with $E\{x(n)\} = E\{v(n)\} = 0$, $x \perp v$ (uncorrelated), and WSS.

*Step 1:* Estimate in frequency domain: $\hat{X}(f) = H(f)Y(f)$.

*Step 2:* Error: $E(f) = X(f) - H(f)Y(f)$.

*Step 3:* MSE in frequency domain:

$$
\xi = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} S_e(f)\,df,\quad S_e(f) = E\{\vert E(f)\vert^2\}
$$

*Step 4:* Expand $S_e(f)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_e(f) &= E\{\vert X - HY\vert^2\} = S_{xx}(f) - H(f)S_{xy}^*(f) - H^*(f)S_{xy}(f) + \vert H(f)\vert^2 S_{yy}(f)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 5:* Apply the **orthogonality principle** in the frequency domain — minimize $S_e(f)$ at each $f$ (since the integral is a sum of non-negative terms). Using Wirtinger derivative:

$$
\frac{\partial S_e(f)}{\partial H^*(f)} = -S_{xy}(f) + H(f)S_{yy}(f) = 0
$$

*Step 6:* Solve: $H(f) = S_{xy}(f)/S_{yy}(f)$.

*Step 7:* With $x \perp v$: $S_{xy}(f) = S_x(f)$ and $S_{yy}(f) = S_x(f) + S_n(f)$.

$$
\boxed{\,H(f) = \frac{S_x(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)}\,}
$$

*Step 8:* Sanity check — when $S_n \to 0$, $H = 1$; when $S_x \to 0$, $H = 0$. ✔

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**A 1-tap Wiener filter is applied to an ECG segment with $r_{xx}(0) = 9$ (signal variance) and $r_{vv}(0) = 1$ (noise variance). Compute the optimal filter coefficient, the MMSE, and the output SNR. Comment on the result.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Compute $r_{yy}(0)$:
$$
r_{yy}(0) = r_{xx}(0) + r_{vv}(0) = 9 + 1 = 10
$$

*Step 2:* Compute $r_{xy}(0)$ (uncorrelated):
$$
r_{xy}(0) = E\{x(n)[x(n) + v(n)]\} = r_{xx}(0) = 9
$$

*Step 3:* Optimal 1-tap coefficient:
$$
h(0) = \frac{r_{xy}(0)}{r_{yy}(0)} = \frac{9}{10} = 0.9
$$

*Step 4:* MMSE:
$$
\xi_{\min} = r_{xx}(0) - h(0)\cdot r_{xy}(0) = 9 - 0.9 \times 9 = 9(1 - 0.9) = 0.9
$$

*Step 5:* Output SNR (input SNR was $r_{xx}/r_{vv} = 9 = 9.54$ dB):

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{r_{xx}(0) - \xi_{\min}}{\xi_{\min}} = \frac{9 - 0.9}{0.9} = \frac{8.1}{0.9} = 9
$$

*Step 6:* Output SNR (linear) $= 9 = 9.54$ dB, which equals the input SNR. The MMSE equals $S_x S_n / (S_x + S_n) = 9 \times 1 / 10 = 0.9$ ✔.

*Comment:* The Wiener filter attenuates the noisy observation by a factor of 0.9 to produce the optimal MMSE estimate. Even the optimal linear filter cannot completely eliminate the noise because the noise and signal overlap in the time domain (single-tap case).

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of the Wiener filter for noise cancellation in biomedical signals. Discuss its application in removing 50 Hz powerline interference from an ECG signal.**

**Model Solution:**

*Block diagram description:*

> ```
> x(n) ───────────────────────►(+)─── y(n) ──►[Wiener Filter H(f)]───► x̂(n)
>                                ▲
>                                │
>                          v(n) (noise, e.g., 50 Hz)
> ```

*Operation:*

1. Acquire the noisy ECG $y(n) = x_{\text{ECG}}(n) + v_{\text{noise}}(n)$.
2. Estimate the PSDs: $S_x(f)$ (from a clean reference ECG segment) and $S_n(f)$ (from a noise-only segment, e.g., during lead disconnection).
3. Form $H(f) = S_x(f) / [S_x(f) + S_n(f)]$.
4. Multiply $Y(f)$ by $H(f)$ in the frequency domain, then inverse-transform to get $\hat{x}(n)$.

*Application to 50 Hz removal:*

- 50 Hz interference is a narrow-band line spectrum with a sharp peak at $f_0 = 50$ Hz in $S_n(f)$.
- At $f_0$, $S_n(f_0) \gg S_x(f_0)$ → $H(f_0) \approx 0$ → the filter **strongly attenuates** the 50 Hz component.
- At ECG-relevant frequencies (0.5–40 Hz), $S_x(f) \gg S_n(f)$ → $H(f) \approx 1$ → ECG components are preserved.
- The transition between attenuation and pass-band is **automatic** and *data-adaptive* (unlike a fixed notch filter).

*Advantages over classical notch filter:*
- Adaptive: handles drift in interference frequency (e.g., 49.5 Hz vs. 50.2 Hz).
- No phase distortion at non-interfering frequencies.
- Works even when interference overlaps ECG spectrum, by using PSD statistics.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Derive the matrix-form Wiener-Hopf equation for an $N$-tap FIR filter. Show that the optimal filter is $\mathbf{h}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{xy}$, and write the expression for the minimum MSE achieved.**

**Model Solution:**

*Step 1:* Filter estimate: $\hat{x}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k)\,y(n-k) = \mathbf{h}^T\mathbf{y}(n)$.

*Step 2:* MSE: $\xi = E\{[x(n) - \mathbf{h}^T\mathbf{y}(n)]^2\}$.

*Step 3:* Expand:

$$
\xi = r_x(0) - 2\mathbf{h}^T\mathbf{r}_{xy} + \mathbf{h}^T\mathbf{R}_{yy}\mathbf{h}
$$

where:
- $r_x(0) = E\{x^2(n)\}$,
- $\mathbf{r}_{xy} = E\{x(n)\mathbf{y}(n)\}$ ($N \times 1$),
- $\mathbf{R}_{yy} = E\{\mathbf{y}(n)\mathbf{y}^T(n)\}$ ($N \times N$).

*Step 4:* Differentiate (use $\nabla_{\mathbf{h}}[\mathbf{h}^T\mathbf{A}\mathbf{h}] = 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{h}$ for symmetric $\mathbf{A}$):

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{h}}\xi = -2\mathbf{r}_{xy} + 2\mathbf{R}_{yy}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}
$$

*Step 5:* Solve (assuming $\mathbf{R}_{yy}$ is positive-definite and hence invertible):

$$
\boxed{\,\mathbf{h}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{xy}\,}
$$

*Step 6:* Substitute $\mathbf{h}_{\text{opt}}$ back:

$$
\xi_{\min} = r_x(0) - \mathbf{r}_{xy}^T\mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{xy}
$$

*Step 7:* Note — this is the standard **Wiener-Hopf normal equation** in matrix form, solvable in $O(N^3)$ by direct inversion, or $O(N^2)$ via Levinson-Durbin recursion (when $\mathbf{R}_{yy}$ is Toeplitz).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the uncorrelated assumption** — many students write $H(f) = S_{xy}(f)/S_{yy}(f)$ but *fail* to substitute $S_{xy} = S_x$ and $S_{yy} = S_x + S_n$. Marks lost: 1–2 per instance.
>
> 2. **Wrong orthogonality statement** — the error is orthogonal to the *input* $y(n-k)$, NOT to the desired signal $x(n)$. Mixing these up is a 7-mark killer.
>
> 3. **Skipping the sanity check** — KTU examiners award bonus marks for verifying $H \to 1$ when $S_n \to 0$ and $H \to 0$ when $S_x \to 0$. Skipping costs 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 4. **Mixing up causal vs. non-causal** — the formula $H(f) = S_x/(S_x + S_n)$ is **non-causal** (requires future samples). For a *causal* Wiener filter, you must mention **Levinson-Durbin recursion** or the **Wiener-Hopf integral equation**, not just the spectral formula.
>
> 5. **MMSE sign errors** — the MMSE formula is $\xi_{\min} = r_x(0) - \mathbf{r}_{xy}^T \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1} \mathbf{r}_{xy}$ (a *subtraction*, not an addition). Confusing this is a 2-mark loss.
>
> 6. **Units** — PSDs have units of $\text{V}^2/\text{Hz}$. Don't state the filter response as dimensionless without context.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin This in Your Notebook:**

- [ ] **Wiener filter** = optimal *linear* filter that minimizes the **MSE** between $x(n)$ and $\hat{x}(n)$.
- [ ] Operates on **wide-sense stationary** signals with known second-order statistics.
- [ ] Assumes **additive noise model**: $y(n) = x(n) + v(n)$.
- [ ] Assumes **signal and noise are uncorrelated and zero-mean**.
- [ ] **Frequency-domain (non-causal) formula**: $H(f) = \dfrac{S_x(f)}{S_x(f) + S_n(f)}$.
- [ ] **Alternative form**: $H(f) = 1 - \dfrac{S_n(f)}{S_{yy}(f)}$.
- [ ] **Time-domain matrix form** (FIR, $N$ taps): $\mathbf{h}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{xy}$.
- [ ] **Wiener-Hopf equation**: $r_{xy}(m) = \sum_k h(k) r_{yy}(m-k)$.
- [ ] **MMSE**: $\xi_{\min} = \int \dfrac{S_x S_n}{S_x + S_n}\,df = r_x(0) - \mathbf{r}_{xy}^T \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{xy}$.
- [ ] **Orthogonality principle**: $E\{e(n) y(n-k)\} = 0$ for all $k$ at the optimum.
- [ ] **Causal** Wiener filter is solved via **Levinson-Durbin recursion** (prediction-error filter).
- [ ] **Limiting cases**: $H=1$ if no noise; $H=0$ if no signal; $H=0.5$ at SNR = 1 (0 dB).
- [ ] **Biomedical applications**: ECG denoising, FECG extraction, EP estimation, EEG artifact removal, MRI reconstruction.
- [ ] **Adaptive Wiener** (LMS / RLS): used when PSDs are unknown or time-varying.
- [ ] **Wiener filter is the gold-standard LMMSE estimator** — no linear filter can beat it in MSE under the given assumptions.
- [ ] The filter is **data-adaptive** in the sense that $H(f)$ depends on $S_x$ and $S_n$, not on any single realization.
- [ ] It cannot reduce the *irreducible* error $\xi_{\min}$; that's the *floor* of linear estimation.
