topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT634 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** EEG Denoising and Computation of Diagnostic Signal Parameters of ECG: Heart Rate and QRS Detection Using Multivariate Analysis (PCA and ICA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electroencephalogram (EEG) and its Denoising
The **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** is a non-invasive recording of the brain's electrical activity captured from scalp electrodes, sampled typically at **256 Hz to 512 Hz** with amplitudes in the microvolt range (**µV**). A raw EEG trace is almost always contaminated by physiological and environmental artifacts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EEG Denoising (KTU 2024 Definition):** The process of removing unwanted noise components (power-line interference, baseline wander, eye-blink EOG, muscle EMG, and motion artifacts) from multichannel EEG recordings while preserving the underlying neural signal of interest (delta 0.5–4 Hz, theta 4–8 Hz, alpha 8–13 Hz, beta 13–30 Hz, gamma >30 Hz).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are standing in a crowded market trying to listen to one specific friend. Your brain automatically separates voices — this is exactly what **ICA (Independent Component Analysis)** does mathematically. PCA, in contrast, is like a *statistical shadow* of the data: it finds the directions of maximum variance regardless of whether the source is signal or noise.

### 1.2 Electrocardiogram (ECG) and Diagnostic Parameters
The **Electrocardiogram (ECG)** is the time-varying electrical potential generated by cardiac depolarization and repolarization, conventionally sampled at **500 Hz** with a bandwidth of **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**. The waveform contains the P wave, QRS complex, and T wave.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnostic Parameters of ECG (KTU 2024 Focus):** The two principal time-domain parameters extracted in this module are:
> 1. **Heart Rate (HR)** — the number of cardiac cycles per minute.
> 2. **QRS Complex Detection** — the precise localization of the R-peak in each cardiac cycle.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of the QRS complex as the *loudest drumbeat* in the heart's rhythm. The Pan-Tompkins algorithm and ICA-based methods act as a smart microphone that locks onto this drumbeat even when other instruments (noise, T-waves, baseline drift) are playing simultaneously.

### 1.3 Multivariate Analysis — PCA and ICA
> [!NOTE]
> **Multivariate Analysis** in biomedical signal processing refers to statistical techniques that exploit the *simultaneous* recording of multiple channels (e.g., 8, 16, 32, or 64 EEG electrodes, or multi-lead ECG) to extract latent components that are not observable in any single channel.

- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA):** A second-order statistical method that decorrelates the multichannel data by projecting it onto orthogonal axes of maximum variance.
- **Independent Component Analysis (ICA):** A higher-order statistical method that decomposes the multichannel data into *statistically independent* non-Gaussian source components.

**Mathematical Intuition:** PCA is the *"orthogonal projector"*; ICA is the *"blind un-mixer"*. For ECG QRS detection, ICA can isolate the maternal and fetal QRS complexes from abdominal recordings. For EEG, ICA separates eye blinks from brain activity.

### 1.4 Standard Physiological Constants and Metrics
| Parameter | Standard Value | KTU Significance |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| Mains power-line frequency (India) | **50 Hz** | Notch filter centre |
| ECG sampling rate | **500 Hz** | Nyquist = 250 Hz |
| QRS bandwidth | **5–15 Hz** | Pan-Tompkins bandpass |
| EEG sampling rate | **256–512 Hz** | Module-4 standard |
| Normal adult Heart Rate | **60–100 bpm** | Diagnostic threshold |
| Bradycardia threshold | **<60 bpm** | Diagnostic threshold |
| Tachycardia threshold | **>100 bpm** | Diagnostic threshold |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Model of Multichannel Observations
Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times T}$ be an $N$-channel observation matrix, where $N$ is the number of sensors (electrodes) and $T$ is the number of samples. The **generative model** assumes:

$$\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{A} \mathbf{S} + \mathbf{N}_a$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is the **mixing matrix** ($N \times N$), $\mathbf{S}$ is the **source matrix** ($N \times T$), and $\mathbf{N}_a$ is additive measurement noise.

- **PCA assumption:** Sources are *uncorrelated* and *Gaussian*.
- **ICA assumption:** Sources are *statistically independent* and *non-Gaussian* (justified by the Central Limit Theorem).

### 2.2 Noise Taxonomy in EEG
1. **Power-line interference (50 Hz / 60 Hz):** Narrowband, removed by notch filter or harmonic PCA component.
2. **Baseline wander (<0.5 Hz):** Caused by electrode-skin impedance variations and respiration.
3. **EOG (eye-blink/eye-movement):** 0.5–5 Hz, large amplitude (200–800 µV).
4. **EMG (muscle artifacts):** >20 Hz, broad spectrum.
5. **Motion artifacts:** Broadband, transient.

### 2.3 Heart Rate Computation
The heart rate is computed from the **R-R interval** (time between successive R-peaks):

$$HR_{\text{bpm}} = \frac{60}{T_{RR}} \quad \text{where } T_{RR} \text{ is in seconds}$$

$$HR_{\text{bpm}} = \frac{1500}{T_{RR,\text{ms}}} \quad \text{(approximation, ECG at 25 mm/s)}$$

$$HR_{\text{bpm}} = \frac{300}{\text{number of large boxes between R peaks}}$$

**Average HR over $M$ beats:**

$$\overline{HR} = \frac{60 \cdot (M-1)}{\sum_{i=1}^{M-1} T_{RR,i}}$$

### 2.4 Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Algorithm
A cascaded sequence of operations:
1. **Bandpass filtering** (5–15 Hz) to isolate QRS energy.
2. **Derivative operator** to emphasize the steep R-wave slope.
3. **Squaring** to make all values positive and amplify large slopes.
4. **Moving-window integration** to produce a smooth envelope.
5. **Adaptive thresholding** with two thresholds (signal and noise levels).
6. **Search-back** for missed beats based on RR-interval physiology (RR between **0.2 s and 1.4 s**).

### 2.5 PCA-Based Denoising
Given the data matrix $\mathbf{X}$:
1. Mean-centre each row: $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$.
2. Compute the **sample covariance matrix**:

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{T-1} \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{X}_c^{\mathsf{T}}$$

3. Eigendecompose: $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{U}^{\mathsf{T}}$.
4. **Sort** eigenvalues $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \dots \geq \lambda_N$.
5. Retain top $K$ eigenvectors and reconstruct:

$$\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{U}_K \mathbf{U}_K^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{X}_c + \boldsymbol{\mu}$$

The discarded components represent noise. The choice of $K$ is governed by the **cumulative variance ratio (CVR)**:

$$\text{CVR}(K) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i} \geq 0.95 \quad (\text{typical threshold})$$

### 2.6 ICA-Based Denoising (FastICA, Hyvärinen)
The unmixing problem seeks a matrix $\mathbf{W}$ such that:

$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{S}$$

**Negentropy** measures non-Gaussianity and is maximized as the independence objective:

$$J(\mathbf{y}) = H(\mathbf{y}_{\text{gauss}}) - H(\mathbf{y}) = \frac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}\left[\mathbf{y}^2\right] - H(\mathbf{y})$$

The FastICA contrast function for negentropy approximation:

$$J_G(\mathbf{y}) \approx k_1 \left[ \mathbb{E}[G(\mathbf{y})] \right]^2, \quad G(u) = \frac{1}{a}\log\cosh(au)$$

The **fixed-point update** for a single unit:

$$\mathbf{w}^{+} \leftarrow \mathbb{E}\left[ \mathbf{X} \, g(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{X}) \right] - \mathbb{E}\left[ g'(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{X}) \right] \mathbf{w}$$

$$\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{w}^{+}}{\Vert \mathbf{w}^{+} \Vert}$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{S}$ | Linear mixing model | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{T-1}\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{X}_c^{\mathsf{T}}$ | Sample covariance | V² (squared signal units) |
| 3 | $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{U}^{\mathsf{T}}$ | Spectral decomposition | — |
| 4 | $\text{CVR}(K) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K}\lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}\lambda_i}$ | Variance retention | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{U}_K \mathbf{U}_K^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{X}_c + \boldsymbol{\mu}$ | PCA reconstruction | signal units |
| 6 | $J(\mathbf{y}) = H(\mathbf{y}_{\text{gauss}}) - H(\mathbf{y})$ | Negentropy | nats |
| 7 | $G(u) = \frac{1}{a}\log\cosh(au)$ | FastICA non-linearity | — |
| 8 | $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{w}^{+} - (\mathbf{w}^{+\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{w})\mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w}^{+} - (\mathbf{w}^{+\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{w})\mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Decorrelation step (multi-unit) | — |
| 9 | $HR = \frac{60}{T_{RR}}$ | Heart Rate | bpm |
| 10 | $\overline{HR} = \frac{60(M-1)}{\sum T_{RR,i}}$ | Mean HR | bpm |
| 11 | $f_s > 2 f_{\max}$ | Nyquist criterion | Hz |
| 12 | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-M}^{M} w[k] \, x[n-k]$ | Pan-Tompkins MWA | signal units |
| 13 | $H_{\text{beat}} = \int \vert x_{\text{filtered}}(t) \vert^2 dt$ | Beat energy | J·Ω⁻¹ |
| 14 | $y(t) = x(t) + j \, \mathcal{H}\{x(t)\}$ | Analytic signal | — |
| 15 | $E_{\text{signal}} = \sum_{i} \lambda_i^{\text{PC}} / N$ | Average eigen-energy | V² |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Clinical EEG:** Artifact-free traces enable accurate diagnosis of epilepsy, sleep disorders, and encephalopathies.
- **Fetal ECG monitoring:** ICA separates maternal and fetal QRS in abdominal recordings.
- **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI):** Denoised signals feed P300 spellers and motor-imagery classifiers.
- **Holter / wearable ECG:** R-peak detection drives HRV analysis, arrhythmia screening, and atrial-fibrillation detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heart Rate from R-R Intervals
**Given:** Three consecutive R-peaks detected at sample indices $n_1, n_2, n_3$ in an ECG sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Convert sample indices to time (seconds):**

$$t_i = \frac{n_i}{f_s}, \quad i = 1,2,3$$

**Step 2 — Compute successive R-R intervals:**

$$T_{RR,1} = t_2 - t_1, \qquad T_{RR,2} = t_3 - t_2$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous heart rates:**

$$HR_1 = \frac{60}{T_{RR,1}}, \qquad HR_2 = \frac{60}{T_{RR,2}}$$

**Step 4 — Mean heart rate over the segment:**

$$\overline{HR} = \frac{HR_1 + HR_2}{2} = \frac{30}{T_{RR,1}} + \frac{30}{T_{RR,2}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**
Let $n_1 = 2500$, $n_2 = 3000$, $n_3 = 3520$, $f_s = 500$ Hz.

$$t_1 = \frac{2500}{500} = 5.000 \text{ s}, \quad t_2 = 6.000 \text{ s}, \quad t_3 = 7.040 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{RR,1} = 1.000 \text{ s} \Rightarrow HR_1 = 60 \text{ bpm}$$

$$T_{RR,2} = 1.040 \text{ s} \Rightarrow HR_2 = 57.69 \text{ bpm}$$

$$\overline{HR} = \frac{60 + 57.69}{2} = 58.85 \text{ bpm} \;\;(\text{Bradycardia region})$$

### 3.2 Derivation: PCA Reconstruction of a Denoised Signal
**Given:** $N=4$ EEG channels, each of length $T=2000$ samples, organized as rows of $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 2000}$.

**Step 1 — Row-wise mean removal:**

$$\boldsymbol{\mu} = \frac{1}{T} \mathbf{X} \mathbf{1}_T, \qquad \mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}\mathbf{1}_T^{\mathsf{T}}$$

**Step 2 — Covariance matrix:**

$$\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{T-1} \mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{X}_c^{\mathsf{T}} \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 4}$$

**Step 3 — Eigendecomposition:**

$$\mathbf{C} \mathbf{u}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{u}_i, \quad i = 1,2,3,4$$

$$\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{U}^{\mathsf{T}}, \quad \mathbf{U} = [\mathbf{u}_1 \; \mathbf{u}_2 \; \mathbf{u}_3 \; \mathbf{u}_4]$$

**Step 4 — Sort eigenvalues:** Assume after sorting,

$$\lambda_1 = 12.4, \;\; \lambda_2 = 3.10, \;\; \lambda_3 = 0.20, \;\; \lambda_4 = 0.05$$

**Step 5 — Compute total variance and CVR:**

$$\sum \lambda_i = 15.75$$

$$\text{CVR}(1) = \frac{12.4}{15.75} = 0.787$$

$$\text{CVR}(2) = \frac{15.5}{15.75} = 0.984$$

Choosing $K = 2$ captures **98.4 %** of variance, satisfying the **95 % threshold**. The third and fourth components (likely EMG and channel-noise) are rejected.

**Step 6 — Reconstruction:**

$$\mathbf{U}_K = [\mathbf{u}_1 \;\; \mathbf{u}_2] \in \mathbb{R}^{4 \times 2}$$

$$\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{U}_K \mathbf{U}_K^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{X}_c + \boldsymbol{\mu}\mathbf{1}_T^{\mathsf{T}}$$

The reconstruction is the **orthogonal projection** of the mean-centred data onto the principal-component subspace, automatically suppressing the noise subspace spanned by $\mathbf{u}_3, \mathbf{u}_4$.

### 3.3 Derivation: FastICA Fixed-Point Iteration
**Goal:** Find $\mathbf{w}$ that maximises $J_G(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x})$ subject to $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$.

**Step 1 — Lagrangian formulation:**

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbb{E}\left[ G(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x}) \right] - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{w} - 1)$$

**Step 2 — First-order optimality condition (KKT):**

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ \mathbf{x} \, g(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x}) \right] - \lambda \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

where $g(u) = G'(u) = \tanh(au)$ for $G(u) = \frac{1}{a}\log\cosh(au)$.

**Step 3 — Newton update (Hyvärinen 1999).** The Jacobian of the left-hand side is:

$$\mathbf{J}_F = \mathbb{E}\left[ \mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf{T}} g'(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x}) \right] - \lambda \mathbf{I}$$

Using the approximation $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf{T}} g'(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x})] \approx \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{\mathsf{T}}] \cdot \mathbb{E}[g'(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x})] = \mathbb{E}[g'] \cdot \mathbf{I}$ (after sphering), the Newton step becomes:

$$\mathbf{w}^{+} = \mathbf{w} - \frac{\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\, g(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x})] - \lambda \mathbf{w}}{\mathbb{E}[g'(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x})] - \lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Final FastICA update:**

$$\mathbf{w}^{+} \leftarrow \mathbb{E}\left[ \mathbf{x} \, g(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x}) \right] - \mathbb{E}\left[ g'(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x}) \right] \mathbf{w}$$

$$\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{w}^{+}}{\Vert \mathbf{w}^{+} \Vert}$$

**Step 5 — Deflationary orthogonalisation for multiple units:**

$$\mathbf{w}_p^{+} \leftarrow \mathbf{w}_p - \sum_{j=1}^{p-1} (\mathbf{w}_p^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{w}_j) \mathbf{w}_j, \quad \mathbf{w}_p \leftarrow \frac{\mathbf{w}_p^{+}}{\Vert \mathbf{w}_p^{+} \Vert}$$

**Step 6 — Convergence test:** $\vert 1 - \mathbf{w}_{\text{new}}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{w}_{\text{old}} \vert < \epsilon$, with $\epsilon = 10^{-4}$ typical.

### 3.4 Derivation: Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Chain (Z-domain analysis)
**Step 1 — Low-pass stage (gain = 36, 2nd-order Butterworth):**

$$H_{LP}(z) = \frac{(1 - z^{-6})^2}{(1 - z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 2 — High-pass stage (subtraction of delayed LP from all-pass):**

$$H_{HP}(z) = z^{-16} - H_{LP}(z) = \frac{-1 + 32z^{-16} + z^{-32}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Combined bandpass (cascade):**

$$H_{BP}(z) = H_{LP}(z) \cdot H_{HP}(z)$$

**Step 4 — Five-point derivative:**

$$H_d(z) = \frac{1}{8T}\left(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z^{1} + z^{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Squaring and 30-sample moving average:**

$$H_{MWA}(z) = \frac{1}{30}\left(z^{29} + z^{28} + \dots + 1\right)$$

**Step 6 — Adaptive thresholding rules.** Let $SPK$ be signal peak level, $NPK$ be noise peak level, $\alpha = 0.125$, $\beta = 0.5$:

$$TH_{1} = NPK + \alpha (SPK - NPK)$$

$$TH_{2} = 0.5 \, TH_{1}$$

A peak above $TH_1$ is a QRS; if no QRS detected for **1.66 × current RR**, the algorithm searches back using $TH_2$.

### 3.5 QRS Detection via ICA: Mathematical Rationale
When the abdominal ECG is modelled as a linear mixture $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{S}$, the maternal QRS, fetal QRS, maternal respiration, and muscle noise appear as **independent sources**. The ICA output components whose kurtosis exceeds a threshold (e.g., $\vert \kappa \vert > 2$) and whose spectral content lies in **10–40 Hz** are flagged as **QRS components**, and R-peaks are detected by simple thresholding on the envelope of these components.

**Kurtosis formula:**

$$\kappa = \mathbb{E}\left[ \left(\frac{y - \mu_y}{\sigma_y}\right)^4 \right] - 3$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Multivariate EEG/ECG Denoising Pipeline
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multichannel Raw EEG or ECG X] --> B[Pre-processing: Mean Centring and Bandpass]
    B --> C[Whitening via Eigendecomposition of Covariance]
    C --> D1[PCA Branch: Sort Eigenvalues]
    C --> D2[ICA Branch: Sphering and FastICA]
    D1 --> E1[Keep Top K Components CVR greater than 95 percent]
    D2 --> E2[Negentropy Maximisation g equals tanh]
    E1 --> F1[Reconstruct Signal via U_K U_K transpose X]
    E2 --> F2[Identify Artifact ICs by Kurtosis and Spectrum]
    F1 --> G1[Denoised Multichannel Output]
    F2 --> G2[Denoised Multichannel Output]
    G1 --> H[R-peak Detection and HR Computation]
    G2 --> H[R-peak Detection and HR Computation]
    H --> I[Diagnostic Parameters HR in bpm and QRS Timing]
```

### 4.2 Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Block Diagram
```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw ECG x n] --> bp[Bandpass 5 to 15 Hz]
    bp --> der[Derivative Operator]
    der --> sq[Squaring Function]
    sq --> mwa[Moving Window Integration 150 ms]
    mwa --> th[Adaptive Thresholding]
    th --> det[R-peak Detected]
    det --> rr[RR Interval Buffer]
    rr --> hr[Heart Rate in bpm]
    rr --> sb[Search Back Logic]
    sb --> th
```

### 4.3 PCA Denoising — Decision Flow
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Input N channel EEG matrix X] --> s2[Subtract per channel mean]
    s2 --> s3[Compute covariance C equals X X transpose]
    s3 --> s4[Eigendecompose C equals U Lambda U transpose]
    s4 --> s5[Sort eigenvalues descending]
    s5 --> s6{Compute CVR K}
    s6 -->|CVR less than 0.95| s7[Increment K and recompute]
    s7 --> s6
    s6 -->|CVR greater than 0.95| s8[Select first K eigenvectors]
    s8 --> s9[Reconstruct X hat equals U_K U_K transpose X]
    s9 --> s10[Output Denoised EEG]
```

### 4.4 ICA-Based QRS Detection for Fetal ECG
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    m1[Abdominal Multichannel ECG] --> m2[Apply FastICA]
    m2 --> m3[Independent Components Y1 Y2 ... YN]
    m3 --> m4[Compute Kurtosis and Spectrum per IC]
    m4 --> m5{IC contains QRS energy 10 to 40 Hz}
    m5 -->|Yes| m6[Tag as QRS component]
    m5 -->|No| m7[Reject as noise]
    m6 --> m8[Envelope Detection and Peak Picking]
    m8 --> m9[Fetal and Maternal RR Intervals]
    m9 --> m10[Fetal HR in bpm]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Independent Component Analysis (ICA) and state its fundamental assumption that distinguishes it from PCA.**

**Model Answer:**
ICA is a blind source-separation technique that decomposes a multichannel signal $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{S}$ into statistically independent source components $\mathbf{S}$ by estimating the unmixing matrix $\mathbf{W}$ such that $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{S}$. The fundamental assumption that distinguishes ICA from PCA is that the underlying sources are **(i) statistically independent** and **(ii) non-Gaussian**. PCA only enforces uncorrelatedness and Gaussianity, whereas ICA enforces the stronger condition of full statistical independence, enabling it to recover physiologically meaningful sources (e.g., fetal vs. maternal QRS, eye-blink vs. cortical EEG).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] The R-R interval in a 500 Hz sampled ECG is measured as 2500 samples. Calculate the heart rate in bpm.**

**Model Answer:**

$$T_{RR} = \frac{2500}{500} = 5.0 \text{ s}$$

$$HR = \frac{60}{T_{RR}} = \frac{60}{5.0} = 12 \text{ bpm}$$

This abnormally low value indicates **severe bradycardia** or a sensor / detection fault (a normal resting HR lies between **60 and 100 bpm**).

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** for real-time QRS detection. Mention the role of each block. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compute the heart rate (in bpm) from the following R-peak sample indices of an ECG sampled at 250 Hz: *R₁ = 1250, R₂ = 1750, R₃ = 2275, R₄ = 2825*. Classify the cardiac rhythm. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pan-Tompkins Algorithm:**
Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.2**.
- **Bandpass filter (5–15 Hz):** Isolates QRS energy and suppresses baseline wander, T-waves, and high-frequency muscle noise. Implemented as a cascade of 2nd-order low-pass and high-pass Butterworth filters.
- **Derivative operator:** $H_d(z) = \frac{1}{8T}(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z^{1} + z^{2})$ — emphasises the steep slope of the R-wave.
- **Squaring:** $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ — makes all values positive and enhances large slopes nonlinearly.
- **Moving window integration (MWA):** $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}x^2[n-k]$ with $N \approx 0.15 \cdot f_s$ — produces a smooth pulse envelope whose peak coincides with the R-wave.
- **Adaptive thresholding:** Two thresholds $TH_1$ and $TH_2$ are updated using signal-peak (SPK) and noise-peak (NPK) levels with learning coefficients $\alpha = 0.125$ and $\beta = 0.5$. A peak above $TH_1$ is declared a QRS; $TH_2$ enables *search-back* for missed beats using the physiological RR window of **0.2 s to 1.4 s** (i.e., **43–300 bpm**).

**(b) Heart Rate Computation:**

$$T_{RR,1} = \frac{1750-1250}{250} = 2.000 \text{ s} \Rightarrow HR_1 = \frac{60}{2.000} = 30 \text{ bpm}$$

$$T_{RR,2} = \frac{2275-1750}{250} = 2.100 \text{ s} \Rightarrow HR_2 = \frac{60}{2.100} = 28.57 \text{ bpm}$$

$$T_{RR,3} = \frac{2825-2275}{250} = 2.200 \text{ s} \Rightarrow HR_3 = \frac{60}{2.200} = 27.27 \text{ bpm}$$

$$\overline{HR} = \frac{30 + 28.57 + 27.27}{3} = 28.61 \text{ bpm}$$

**Classification:** Since $\overline{HR} \approx 28.6$ bpm $< 60$ bpm, the rhythm is classified as **severe bradycardia**. The progressively lengthening RR intervals (2.0 s, 2.1 s, 2.2 s) also indicate a **decelerating rhythm**, which is clinically significant and warrants further evaluation.

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Derive the **mathematical model and step-by-step procedure for PCA-based denoising** of a 4-channel EEG signal. State clearly the criterion for selecting the number of principal components. (7 marks)
**(b)** Explain the **FastICA algorithm** for blind source separation. Derive the negentropy-based fixed-point update rule. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PCA-Based Denoising (Derivation):**
Refer to **Section 3.2** for the exhaustive derivation. The procedure is:
1. **Model:** $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{S}$ (after mean removal).
2. **Covariance:** $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{T-1}\mathbf{X}_c \mathbf{X}_c^{\mathsf{T}}$.
3. **Eigendecomposition:** $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{U}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{U}^{\mathsf{T}}$.
4. **Sort** eigenvalues in descending order.
5. **Selection criterion — Cumulative Variance Ratio (CVR):**

$$\text{CVR}(K) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_i} \geq 0.95$$

6. **Reconstruction:** $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{U}_K \mathbf{U}_K^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{X}_c + \boldsymbol{\mu}\mathbf{1}_T^{\mathsf{T}}$.
The remaining components (orthogonal to the first $K$) are assumed to represent noise and are discarded.

**(b) FastICA Algorithm (Derivation):**
Refer to **Section 3.3** for the KKT-based derivation. The algorithm is:
1. **Centre** the data: $\mathbf{X}_c = \mathbf{X} - \boldsymbol{\mu}$.
2. **Whiten** the data: $\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = \boldsymbol{\Lambda}^{-1/2}\mathbf{U}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{X}_c$ so that $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\mathbf{X}}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\mathsf{T}}] = \mathbf{I}$.
3. **Initialise** $\mathbf{w}$ randomly with $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$.
4. **Update rule (derived via Newton's method on the negentropy contrast):**

$$\mathbf{w}^{+} \leftarrow \mathbb{E}\left[ \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \, g(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}) \right] - \mathbb{E}\left[ g'(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}) \right] \mathbf{w}$$

5. **Normalise:** $\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w}^{+} / \Vert \mathbf{w}^{+} \Vert$.
6. **Decorrelate** for multiple units via Gram-Schmidt.
7. **Convergence test:** $\vert 1 - \mathbf{w}_{\text{new}}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{w}_{\text{old}} \vert < 10^{-4}$.

The contrast function $G(u) = \frac{1}{a}\log\cosh(au)$ with $a \approx 1$ is the standard choice because it provides a robust non-linearity for both sub- and super-Gaussian sources.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 4):**
> 1. **Confusing decorrelation with independence** — many students write that PCA "makes components independent", which is **wrong**. PCA only *decorrelates*; ICA is what enforces independence.
> 2. **Forgetting mean-centring** before PCA — failing to subtract the per-channel mean invalidates the covariance computation and is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing up $f_s$ units** — students frequently forget to convert the ECG sample count to seconds before computing heart rate. Always divide sample-difference by $f_s$ first.
> 4. **Wrong threshold for component selection** — using a fixed $K$ (e.g., "always keep 2 components") instead of justifying with the **CVR ≥ 95 %** criterion loses full marks.
> 5. **Misinterpreting ICA output** — ICA components are *not ordered by variance*; their order is essentially arbitrary. Students claiming "IC1 is the most important" lose marks unless they justify with kurtosis or spectral content.
> 6. **Pan-Tompkins bandpass range** — writing 0.5–40 Hz instead of **5–15 Hz** is a common error; the QRS energy is concentrated in 5–15 Hz, and broader bands admit muscle noise.
> 7. **Bradycardia / Tachycardia thresholds** — students frequently misquote these as <50 and >110; the KTU-prescribed clinical thresholds are **<60 bpm** (bradycardia) and **>100 bpm** (tachycardia).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **EEG Denoising** is the suppression of **50 Hz power-line, baseline wander, EOG, EMG, and motion artifacts** from multichannel recordings.
- **Heart Rate (bpm) = 60 / R-R interval (seconds)**; the clinical normal range is **60–100 bpm**.
- **Bradycardia** $< 60$ bpm; **Tachycardia** $> 100$ bpm.
- **Pan-Tompkins** uses the sequence: **Bandpass (5–15 Hz) → Derivative → Squaring → MWA → Adaptive Thresholding → Search-back**.
- **Physiological RR window** for QRS detection is **0.2 s – 1.4 s** (i.e., **43–300 bpm**).
- **PCA** requires: (i) mean-centring, (ii) covariance computation, (iii) eigendecomposition, (iv) **CVR ≥ 95 %** selection, (v) projection reconstruction.
- **PCA limitation:** it only **decorrelates** and assumes **Gaussian** sources — it cannot separate independent physiological artifacts.
- **ICA** enforces full **statistical independence** and **non-Gaussianity**, justified by the **Central Limit Theorem**.
- **ICA model:** $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{S}$, recovered via $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}$ using **FastICA fixed-point iteration** on **negentropy**.
- **FastICA update:** $\mathbf{w}^{+} = \mathbb{E}[\mathbf{x}\,g(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x})] - \mathbb{E}[g'(\mathbf{w}^{\mathsf{T}}\mathbf{x})]\mathbf{w}$ with $g(u) = \tanh(au)$.
- **Deflationary orthogonalisation** (Gram-Schmidt) extracts multiple independent components one by one.
- **ICA component identification** uses **kurtosis** ($\vert \kappa \vert > 2$ ⇒ non-Gaussian, likely physiological) and **spectral analysis** (QRS energy lies in 10–40 Hz).
- **Fetal ECG** is the classic KTU application of ICA on abdominal multichannel recordings.
- **Whitening (sphering)** is a mandatory pre-processing step before FastICA: $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\mathbf{X}}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^{\mathsf{T}}] = \mathbf{I}$.
- **Cumulative Variance Ratio (CVR)** is the universal PCA stopping rule, with the standard threshold $\geq 0.95$ capturing **95 %** of total signal energy.
- The **derivative operator** in Pan-Tompkins is $H_d(z) = \frac{1}{8T}(-z^{-2} - 2z^{-1} + 2z^{1} + z^{2})$.
- The **MWA window length** in Pan-Tompkins is approximately $0.15 \cdot f_s$ samples.
- **Nyquist sampling** requires $f_s > 2 f_{\max}$; for ECG with QRS energy up to 40 Hz, $f_s = 500$ Hz is more than adequate.
- ICA is **non-deterministic** — different runs may permute and sign-flip the components; this is **not an error**.
- **PCA is a special case of ICA** when the sources are Gaussian and the contrast is the variance.
- Always state the **sampling rate $f_s$** explicitly in any HR / RR computation on the KTU answer script.
- **Pre-whitening reduces ICA complexity** from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N)$ per iteration.
